Any future proposals to alter support for medical degree students will require a comparable process of engagement at official and Ministerial level and the involvement of the SLC and EA as delivery partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration she has given to introducing financial support for students who are enrolled in an undergraduate medical degree, as their second degree.
answer_text: It is current policy that public funding for student support should be targeted towards students entering higher education for the first time. Second degrees are therefore generally not eligible for student support. The rationale for applying this policy is to prioritise students who have not had a chance to experience higher education.	As exceptions to the above policy position, students studying a second undergraduate degree in medicine are eligible for maintenance loan support.  Additionally, from the current academic year students studying a graduate entry medicine course locally are eligible for a tuition fee loan. My Department is engaging with stakeholders to agree the scope and approach of a review of Higher Education funding. It is currently proposed that financial support provided to students will be within the scope of the wider review.  This will include consideration of whether such support should be extended beyond its current coverage, having regard to affordability both for students and the public purse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of legislation to prevent those who have been deemed medically unfit to drive, from being able to do so.
answer_text: Whilst it has not been possible to develop legislation regarding medical fitness to drive due to a lack of resources and competing priorities, my officials have been keeping a watching brief on developments across Britain and Ireland in this area, including those on proposals for changes to vision standards, which are in the very early stages of consideration.However, other measures have been taken to address the issue of fitness to drive, including the introduction of guidance on how to make a Source of Danger Notification on the NI Direct website. This initiative allows someone who knows a person with a medical condition or disability that affects their driving, to tell the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) by putting their concerns in writing. Such a notification gives the Department an opportunity to exercise its statutory powers to commence medical investigations, irrespective as to whether the licence holder has made any declaration concerning their health.My Department also launched a social media campaign on 4 July to raise awareness around fitness to drive. The campaign was aimed at reminding drivers to ensure that all their driving information is up to date, including any relevant medical details. The second part of the campaign was aimed at encouraging consideration, conversation and reporting of concerns around Fitness to Drive. This aligns with the roll out of a revised policy and new NI Direct page on Notification of Source of Danger.The DVA continues to work closely with key stakeholders to increase awareness and improve processes within the existing legislative framework. This includes a recent online webinar with GPNI, which covered a range of topics including fitness to drive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to the introduction of traffic calming measures on Whitehall Parade, South Belfast, to address (i) dangerous driving; and (ii) the volume of traffic, on the street.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits, and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review in the near future.  Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.Persistent speeding problems or persons driving inappropriately in the area should be reported to the PSNI on its non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to the introduction of a 20mph speed limit on Whitehall Parade, South Belfast.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits, and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review in the near future.  Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department collects statistics on the number of (i) fatalities; or (ii) serious injuries, caused by drivers who have been deemed medically unfit to drive, broken down by drivers who had (a) reported their medical conditions to the Driver and Vehicle Agency or the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency; and (b) not done so, pursuant to the statistics gathered by her Department on road safety issues.
answer_text: My Department does not collect statistics on the number of fatalities or serious injuries, caused by drivers who have been deemed medically unfit to drive. The data used to publish statistics in relation to Road Traffic Collisions is sourced from the PSNI and does not include a marker in relation to fitness to drive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that vulnerable groups including (i) those with disabilities; and (ii) the elderly, are not excluded from accessing essential goods and services, due to cashless policies.
answer_text: I am committed to working with my Executive colleagues to ensure that barriers are removed and everyone in Northern Ireland can participate in society on an equal basis, for example through the work my Department leads on the Social Inclusion Strategies. This work is driven by real and meaningful engagement with those with lived experience of the issues faced.While the impact of cashless policies has not been raised in the work to date, my officials will ensure that this engagement continues throughout development and implementation of the Strategies, and that issues raised are addressed by the actions of the Executive departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the Financial Conduct Authority regarding extending access to cash protections to include acceptance of cash by retailers.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 35404/22-27 around this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration that has been given to the introduction of legislation to protect consumers’ right to pay with cash.
answer_text: I raised this issue with the Financial Conduct Authority at my Banking Roundtable on 3 December, and they advised that it is not within their remit to enforce cash acceptance, nor are they aware of any plans at this time by the Treasury to extend their current access to cash powers, under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023, to include a requirement for businesses and organisations to accept cash in regulations.  However, I do recognise that it is important that consumers, especially those most vulnerable in our society, have a choice of payment methods, including cash. I will be raising this with the Treasury when I share my summary report from the Banking Roundtable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration that has been given to the creation of a goods and services directorate for older people, under the auspices of the Commissioner for Older People.
answer_text: COPNI’s role is to safeguard and promote the needs of older people and would not have the statutory remit for a directorate of this kind.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 32351/22-27, to detail any progress that has been made on integrating Northern Ireland into the Tell Us Once service.
answer_text: Bereavement provision here is currently made by the Bereavement Service, which is run by the Department for Communities. Following a constructive meeting with Minister Lyons in September 2025 I agreed that my officials would investigate the legislative work that would be necessary to allow my department to share relevant death registration data with a Tell Us Once type service.That scoping work is at an early stage. My officials are examining a number of potential legal gateways to verify if there are viable routes that could enable the sharing of death registration information as described. If no suitable route is identified, work to consider alternative legislative options would be required.I am supportive of lessening the burden on bereaved families at very difficult times in their lives, and my department will do as much as possible to support this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the role her Department has in holding housing developers accountable for meeting quality standards; and (ii) the existing protections for purchasers, when those standards are not met.
answer_text: The planning system generally has an important enabling role to play in facilitating the delivery of housing and my Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) requires councils to facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality housing to meet the needs of everyone and supports good design standards and positive place making for all types of development. Councils determine the vast majority of planning applications for housing development, and under the provision of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, have the general discretion to take enforcement action in response to alleged breaches of planning control, such as non-compliance with planning conditions.  While my Department continues to support the delivery of homes to meet the full range of housing needs of society through regional planning policy and guidance it has limited influence in holding developers to account in meeting quality standards in new housing developments and in providing protections for purchasers beyond its responsibility for the planning legislative and policy framework for the planning system. On a wider note, my Department has been working collectively with local government, and other key stakeholders to deliver a Planning Improvement Programme with the aim of enhancing and making a tangible difference to the planning system in the North. My Department has made recent changes to the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015, to introduce statutory planning application validation checklists for councils, designed to improve planning performance with better quality applications entering the system, resulting in shorter processing times, more efficient consultee responses and quicker planning decisions. This should also give rise to better quality decisions in the planning process for all types of development, including for housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30535/22-27, (i) for an update on how the £5 million allocated to support and expand breast services across Health and Social Care Trusts has been spent; and (ii) to detail what impact it has had on red flag breast cancer waiting times.
answer_text: Further to AQW 30535/22-27 answered in September 2025, I can confirm that the £5 million recurrent funding allocated under the Elective Care Framework (ECF) to support and expand breast services across Health and Social Care Trusts has been directed towards a range of targeted initiatives designed to alleviate service pressures and improve patient access.(i) How the funding has been spentThe £5 million Elective Care Framework funding is currently being utilised non-recurrently to commission additionality for breast services including breast assessment whilst work is ongoing towards increasing core HSC capacity using more sustainable and more efficient care pathway models.  Approximately £44,000 of non-recurrent funding was allocated up to September 2025 to support waiting list initiatives. This enabled the assessment and treatment of 236 additional patients through a combination of inhouse and Independent Sector capacity.  In October 2025, a bespoke pilot was introduced to address immediate pressures in breast services and inform longer-term elective care reform. This pilot adopts a Payment by Results model, remunerating consultants based on the number of patients seen rather than traditional sessional hours.  Building on successful practice within Western Health & Social Care Trust, this approach aims to unlock additional HSC capacity for red flag breast assessment clinics.  Key benefits include alignment with long-term elective care reform goals, encouraging efficient, outcome-driven care delivery, and offering flexibility and sustainability beyond traditional sessional models.  The £5 million will be used to fund the associated additional pilot activity planned October to December 2025 and in the final quarter ending March 2026.My Department, in partnership with key stakeholders to implement sustainable, long-term solutions.  DOH official are collaborating with Professor Juliette Murray, Consultant Breast Surgeon and National Clinical Director at the Centre for Sustainable Delivery, NHS Scotland. Professor Murray brings valuable expertise, having successfully led the redesign of breast services in Scotland in response to similar challenges around demand and capacity. A regional workshop is scheduled for 26 November to explore service redesign opportunities, including the development and integration of advanced practice roles.(ii) Impact on red flag breast cancer waiting timesMy Department is working closely with Trusts to ensure Red Flag referrals are prioritised within existing core capacity to ensure timely access for the most clinically urgent cases.  Following introduction of the bespoke Payment by Results model, a number of additional regional waiting list clinics were delivered during evening and weekends throughout October.  Further extra clinics have been confirmed for all Trusts in November 2025 and December 2025.These measures, combined with increased core activity post-summer, have contributed to a reduction in waiting times for

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration she has given to the development of more active travel routes, including the proposed Blackstaff Greenway, as part of the development required to host matches during the FIFA Women's World Cup 2035.
answer_text: Greenways are generally council led with my Department providing grant funding of up to 50% of the capital costs of approved projects.  Belfast City Council is considering the findings of a recently completed preliminary feasibility study on a proposed Blackstaff Greenway and my officials will continue to work positively with the Council on any future development of those proposals.The Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan sets out a plan for prioritised delivery of active travel infrastructure in Belfast, however future delivery will also be informed by developments such as the Eastern Transport Plan. My Department would welcome discussions with the organisers of the FIFA Women's World Cup 2035 to explore any potential opportunities for collaborative working that may exist.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration she has given to upgrading Adelaide train halt, as part of the development required to host matches during the FIFA Women's World Cup 2035.
answer_text: I have not been made aware of any requirements to upgrade Adelaide Train halt in advance of the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2035.Translink recently extended the platforms at Adelaide Station, to accommodate 6 Car Trains Sets, in summer 2024. Translink also transferred a new designated pedestrian walkway between Adelaide Train Station and Windsor Park to the Irish Football Association for improved local linkages to the stadium.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures she can introduce to improve road safety along Windsor Avenue, Belfast, for pupils at St Brides Primary School.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.However, my department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to limited budget resources, meaning we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.  Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, the location did not score highly enough for inclusion of a controlled crossing in the 2025/26 works programme. The scheme will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become availableThe most recent traffic calming assessment in Windsor Avenue was conducted in 2021, and I have asked my officials to undertake a review of this location given the passage of time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration she has given to the introduction of a residents' parking scheme for (i) Cleaver Park; (ii) Cleaver Gardens; (iii) and Cleaver Avenue, South Belfast.
answer_text: I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to residents’ parking schemes that provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future residents’ parking schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the independent Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum completed in June 2025.  The review, undertaken by Lucy Crehan, provided a focused, time-bound evaluation of the current Northern Ireland curriculum.The Review was clear, Northern Ireland needs a new curriculum which is purpose-led, knowledge-rich, continuous and coherent, specific and focused, flexible and inclusive. A curriculum that ensures every child enjoys an ambitious and knowledge rich curriculum that develops their learning in a well sequenced and explicit manner.On 27 August, I announced the establishment of the Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee that will oversee, direct and lead the reform of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.Led by Christine Counsell with Lucy Crehan as Deputy, the Committee includes a wealth of experience of curriculum design, classroom practice, evaluation and implementation. Their role is to guide the development of a new, coherent and knowledge-rich curriculum framework.Currently, the membership of subject working groups is being finalised. The working groups are made up of teachers, academics and subject specialists, who will draft the reformed content for each subject area from foundation stage to Key Stage 3.  The groups will be fully operational in the coming weeks.  A new framework will be presented to me in March 2026, in advance of a public consultation.   It is my ambition that the new Curriculum Framework will be implemented for September 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the enforcement action her Department can take, in relation to vehicles parked on residential streets that (i) do not have a valid MOT; and or (ii) are untaxed.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency can enforce MOT compliance where road safety has been compromised, and it is in the public interest to do so. This may include issuing a £60 Fixed Penalty Notice to the offending driver or vehicle user or referring the matter for prosecution.However, an MOT offence only arises when a vehicle without a valid MOT certificate is observed being driven on a public road. The DVA cannot take enforcement action when an unaccompanied motor vehicle is observed parked on a residential street.Vehicle Excise Duty (motor tax) is an excepted matter and is administered and enforced by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea, on behalf of the British Treasury. The DVLA operates a comprehensive package of measures to tackle untaxed vehicles. These range from issuing reminder letters and penalties through to court prosecutions and the wheelclamping and/or removal of untaxed vehicles. The use of Automatic Number Plate Readers is one aspect of this approach and targets vehicles that are being used on the road. If you wish to request further information from the DVLA you can contact them using the following dedicated email address for elected representatives at DVLAMinisterials@dvla.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on an AI strategy.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33273/22-27, to detail the reason he did not engage, or consult with, employers on changes to how Level 2 Essential Skills qualifications are measured.
answer_text: The consultation related to proposed changes to how qualifications are counted for school system level performance measures.  The consultation was not specifically about Essential Skills; it related to how qualifications with different guided learning hours, levels and grading scales are measured and combined for the purpose of developing school performance measures. None of the proposals changed the specification or assessment requirements of qualifications which would require wider engagement.  As the changes related to school performance measures, engagement was with school leaders and their TUS representatives.  We also advised the Education Committee of the content and scope of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the outcome of any discussions between her Department and the UK Government in relation to the car insurance market since the restoration of the Assembly.
answer_text: My officials engage monthly with the British Government in relation to the car insurance market. As part of this ongoing engagement, the Motor Insurance Taskforce was established in October 2024. The Taskforce is jointly chaired by the Department for Transport and the Treasury. The Terms of Reference for the Taskforce is to investigate the causes of high motor insurance premiums and to formulate an action plan to address them. My Officials have engaged with the Taskforce throughout the year, ensuring that relevant regional issues are considered. The Taskforce is due to publish its report by end of year. My Department will consider the report's findings once published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the outcome of any discussions she has had with UK Government Ministers in relation to the car insurance market.
answer_text: The regulation of Financial Services including insurance services is a reserved matter.  Complaints received by my Department’s Trading Standards Service Consumerline about car insurance are referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work she is undertaking to ensure Artificial Intelligence is being used ethically within Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Ensuring that Artificial Intelligence (AI) is developed and deployed ethically and responsibly is vital to maximising the benefits of this technology for business and wider society. The AI Collaboration Centre (AICC), funded by my Department and Invest NI, plays a central role in promoting AI awareness and responsible adoption among businesses in the north. The AICC places a strong emphasis on ethical and responsible innovation, including the recent launch of its Responsible AI Hub, which provides resources, guidance, and support to help organisations navigate the ethical challenges of AI and ensure best practice in its use.In addition, my Department is supporting the development of skills and expertise in ethical AI through the funding of postgraduate awards via the AICC, including a dedicated MSc in Ethical and Responsible Artificial Intelligence at Ulster University. These initiatives are helping to build a pipeline of talent equipped to address the ethical, legal, and societal implications of AI.I have set up a new dedicated AI Team with the aim to fully realise the opportunities presented by the transition to AI and digital technologies, while keeping ethical and responsible innovation at the heart of our approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31232/22-27, to detail the findings of the initial assessment, into the Reading with AI research project.
answer_text: The Reading with AI Research Project is being taken forward as a Cross-Cutting Initiative within the RAISE Programme. Oxford Brookes University is leading the study to evaluate the effectiveness of evidence-based AI literacy tools on improving reading outcomes in Northern Ireland.Training sessions were offered to teachers participating in the project by Oxford Brookes University in September 2025 to help teachers with preparation for benchmark assessments.The participating pupils in Years P3, P5, P7 and Y8 will first complete a brief 15-minute benchmark assessment to evaluate their reading skills and identify potential challenges.  This will provide a clear understanding of each pupil’s reading level and enable progress to be measured.  Over the duration of the study, pupils will engage with Amira Learning for frequent reading sessions totalling 20-40 minutes weekly during which ongoing assessment data will be collected.  A similar assessment will also take place at the end of the data collection period in six months. Schools will have access to live and actionable data throughout the project.  However, evidence-based findings on reading outcomes based on the assessments will not be available to the Department until after the completion of the project following the end of the academic year 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31104/22-27, to detail when she will make an announcement as to how future residents’ parking schemes will be assessed by her Department.
answer_text: I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to residents’ parking schemes that provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future residents’ parking schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consultation he had with (i) the Minister for the Economy; and (ii) employers, in relation to changes to level 2 essential skills qualifications.
answer_text: Last year, my officials consulted with schools on proposed changes to how qualifications with different guided learning hours, levels and grading scales are counted to produce school system level performance measures such as the proportion of learners achieving 5 GCSEs (including equivalents) at A*-C. The changes related to how we measure school performance and therefore the consultation was targeted at these key stakeholders.  As outlined in DE Circular Number 2025/01 - Approval of Qualifications for Delivery by Post-Primary Schools in Northern Ireland, performance points ‘relate to how the qualification is counted for the annual return of examination results and are not an indicator of how the qualification is viewed by employers and/or Further/Higher Education.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he gave to expanding the Initial Teacher Education bursary scheme to other subject areas.
answer_text: As announced, the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) Bursary Scheme will apply to new entrants of relevant subjects, beginning their ITE programmes from September 2026 onwards.The purpose of the pilot Bursary Scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in post-primary subjects which are experiencing the most acute teacher shortages; Mathematics, Chemistry, Physics, Computing, Technology and Design and Irish-Medium Education. The decision to prioritise these subjects was informed by the Recruitment and Retention Survey carried out by my Department in 2024, along with both current and historical ITE applications data provided by the Higher Education Institutions, who deliver the ITE programmes.Extending eligibility of the scheme to subjects other than those selected would not increase either applications or enrolments in those critical areas experiencing teacher shortages and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the pilot. The pilot scheme will be subject to a full evaluation and future strategic workforce planning will continue to monitor teacher supply across all subject areas. This will help identify emerging needs and inform any necessary adjustments to the scheme’s remit to ensure it remains responsive to the areas of greatest shortage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any consideration being given to the need to safeguard our environment as part of the development of the Office of AI & Digital.
answer_text: The Office of AI & Digital has been established to drive innovation in public service delivery, with a strong emphasis on ethical, inclusive, and responsible use of AI technology.The Office of AI & Digital, part of the Office of Science and Technology, is assessing operational needs for AI adoption and will develop an AI Strategy and Action Plan.We recognise that safeguarding the environment is integral to responsible innovation and development. As such, environmental considerations and safeguards will be included in the AI Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) three; and (ii) four, bedroom social houses within the Greater Belfast area.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the consideration she has given to the potential environmental impact of the enhanced energy needs, stemming from the growing use of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: I recognise that the growing use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) is driving increased energy demand, particularly through the development of large-scale data centres and the infrastructure needed to support advanced AI applications. While AI presents significant opportunities for economic growth, productivity, and innovation, it is essential that these benefits are balanced with our environmental responsibilities and net-zero commitments. My officials are actively assessing the increasing adoption of Artificial Intelligence and considering appropriate policy responses, informed by recent studies such as those from Matrix and the AI Collaboration Centre, for example. My officials are establishing a working group to explore how we can collaborate with industry, academia, and wider society to both manage and fully realise the opportunities arising from the transition to AI and wider digital technologies. While my Department has no formal role in nominating projects, we are aware that two applications from the North have been submitted under the British Government’s AI Growth Zones initiative, which aims to accelerate the development of large-scale data centres and supporting AI infrastructure. Notably, the British Government’s criteria specify that applications demonstrating the use of renewable or low-carbon energy sources are to be viewed more favourably.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the work his Department is doing to facilitate nature recovery.
answer_text: Protecting and restoring our terrestrial and marine environment is one of my priorities within DAERA’s corporate plan 2025-2027.  Officials are working collaboratively to implement the Environmental Improvement Plan and the Lough Neagh Action Plan, to deliver ambitious measures to improve our air and water quality and restore biodiversity on land and sea.  I have made significant progress, to date, across a number of key environmental objectives. In June this year, I was delighted to release the first tranche of the new Farming with Nature Package. This package aims to improve biodiversity and sustainability through increasing the extent and improving the quality and connectivity of habitats within farmland across Northern Ireland.   My officials are working at pace to develop further phases to include focused support for Designated Areas and Priority Habitats and to facilitate collaboration and advisory support on a landscape scale to address specific environmental concerns.In September, I was pleased to publish the Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy which provides a framework for the conservation and restoration of peatland habitats and also plays a key role in restoring biodiversity.  I have already turned targets into action, with funding secured to restore 12,500 ha of peatland habitat by 2028.My Department is finalising the Marine Protected Areas Strategy for the Northern Ireland inshore region 2025-2030, which I intend to publish by spring 2026. I also have plans in place to develop Action Plans to aid recovery of some of Northern Ireland’s priority species; to develop an Elasmobranch Conservation Strategy; to progress a Seabird Conservation Strategy and Action Plan; and to deliver a new Nature Recovery Strategy to reflect how we will address our international commitments in the Global Biodiversity Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any consideration she has given to using Artificial Intelligence tools to support the more efficient analysis of consultation responses.
answer_text: We are aware of the Humphrey suite of tools currently being developed by the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT). One of these tools (Consult) is being trialled specifically for the purpose of analysing consultation responses.We are monitoring developments in this area with interest and, in the fullness of time, expect to gain access to the Humphrey suite to support our work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she will publish the departmental analysis of the responses to the support for low carbon heat in the residential sector consultation.
answer_text: My Department consulted on future support for low carbon heating in residential properties, which closed in January this year. Officials are currently finalising the analysis of responses provided, which will be used to inform policy decisions on the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, including heat pumps. My Department plans to publish a response to the consultation in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when her Department will publish the departmental analysis of the Credit Union (Modernisation and Reform) public consultation.
answer_text: Departmental analysis of the Credit Union (Modernisation and Reform) public consultation will be published in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the reason why Northern Ireland is not included in the Tell Us Once service operated by the UK Government.
answer_text: When the Tell Us Once Service was introduced in 2011, the Department for Communities explored the possibility of adopting the service. However, implementation was not possible at that time as the required participation of the General Register Office for Northern Ireland; and supporting legislative changes, were not agreed. More recently discussion has resumed about extending bereavement services in Northern Ireland. However significant obstacles to data sharing remain with no timescale agreed for new legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has had conversations with the UK Government about integrating Northern Ireland into the Tell Us Once service.
answer_text: Early in this mandate, I engaged with the Department of Finance for Northern Ireland and the Department for Work and Pensions to begin preliminary scoping work with regards to extending bereavement services in Northern Ireland. I am supportive of this service and welcome its introduction to Northern Ireland. It would be for the Finance Minister to bring forward the legislation required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 27653/22-27; (ii) AQW 27654/22-27; and (iii) AQW 27655/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26540/22-27, to provide an update on the (i) development; and (ii) implementation, of the Women's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: Officials are continuing to develop the Women’s Health Action Plan.  This is a wide-ranging piece of work which seeks to bring together the range of ongoing developments in women’s health such as tackling gynae waiting lists, improving maternity safety and the Live Better work which addresses the health inequalities between different groups of women.This is a challenging piece of work in view of resource constraints across the whole of the Health and Social Care Sector and I want to ensure that the plan is both realistic and achievable and meets the expectations of women and stakeholders.My Department is also continuing to support, and work alongside, Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast on their large-scale public listening exercise with women in NI.  The first phase of this work, a Women’s Health Survey which was funded by DoH, has now concluded.  My Department has also provided further funding for the second phase, currently underway, which involves a series of focus groups targeted at “hard-to-reach” women. This work is due to be completed this year and will be incorporated into the Action Plan.Whilst development of the Action Plan continues, work on the key initiatives / actions that will form the basis of the plan are continuing and will have their own implementation arrangements.  This includes for example the implementation of the gynae GIRFT recommendations and the maternity safety work, subject to available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the next steps she will take, following the publication of the Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research report.
answer_text: Recommendation 1 of the Research Report provides for the creation of an on-going childcare business advisor role or service. I have agreed with the Minister for Education that my Department will take the operational lead on the design and delivery of this service. Its longer-term strategic ambitions will be overseen by the Early Learning & Childcare Task and Finish Group on which my Department is represented.  My officials are working to develop a costed proposal for this important service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether he had any discussions with the Economy Minister in relation to the development of a childcare workforce strategy.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department for the Economy, the Department of Health and the Department for Communities to develop actions to support the childcare workforce, through the Early learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy Task and Finish Group and associated workforce subgroup. I will bring forward a draft Strategy, which will include specific actions for the childcare workforce, to my Executive colleagues in the coming weeks with the aim of securing agreement for issue to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given any consideration to updating the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 to introduce a statutory requirement to ensure there is sufficient childcare provision to meet the needs of parents in their local area.
answer_text: The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 places a duty upon HSC Trusts to provide childcare for children in need, consistent with other duties placed upon them to provide services for children in need and their families. However, lead policy responsibility for childcare rests with the Education Minister, and ensuring the availability and accessibility of childcare for all parents and carers who require it will be key aims of the developing Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to Recommendation 1 within the Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research report, to detail whether it is envisaged that this service will provide advice to all providers, including childminders.
answer_text: I have agreed with the Minister for Education that my Department will take the operational lead on the design and delivery of Recommendation 1; namely, the creation of an on-going childcare business advisor role or service. Its longer-term strategic ambitions will be overseen by the Early Learning & Childcare Task and Finish Group on which my Department is represented.  	My Department is scoping out a costed proposal for how this service will be delivered and my officials will provide me with further details. I am mindful of the need for it to be available to voluntary and community providers, childminders, and childcare businesses alike. It is also important that the service is provided on an ongoing basis so that we can gain a better understanding of the financial needs of the sector and tailor support over time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the work she is undertaking to (i) equip; and (ii) encourage, small businesses to take on more apprentices.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes are demand-led, with employers establishing apprenticeship opportunities in line with business need.   DfE fully funds the off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship, investing over £45 million annually across the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeship programmes – a significant increase over recent years. This funding ensures that all employers are not financially liable for the delivery of apprenticeship training.For SMEs, which form the backbone of our economy, support is given to incentivise employers to take on apprentices in the form of Employer Incentive Payments.   These vary from £670 to £2,008 and are given to employers per completed framework to offset costs. 	The Apprenticeship Action Plan recognises the need to encourage more employers to consider recruiting apprentices and includes deliverables such as piloting Apprenticeship Navigators to support SMEs employing their first apprentices and commissioning research to quantify the financial impact of the apprenticeship system on individual businesses and the economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how enterprise support funding will be (i) organised; and (ii) distributed, from April 2026.
answer_text: The NI Enterprise Support Service, or “Go Succeed”, is funded by the British Government’s Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Growth (MHCLG) via its Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). The SPF is due to end on 31 March 2026. When announcing its Spending Review on 11 June 2025, the British Government referred to the establishment of a ‘Local Growth fund’, which is the anticipated successor to the SPF. The Executive has agreed that the Department of Finance will lead on engaging with the British Government in relation to the Local Growth Fund. My officials continue to liaise with both the Department of Finance and MHCLG to seek further clarity and to understand how future funding for enterprise support might be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the re-establishment of the entrepreneurship co-design group.
answer_text: I have established the SME Entrepreneurship Co-design Group to bring together entrepreneurs, academics and policymakers to explore how we can better support and grow the north of Ireland’s enterprise culture, especially among small and micro businesses.This group is about listening to those with lived experience of starting and scaling businesses and working together to shape practical actions that make entrepreneurship more accessible and sustainable across all parts of our economy.The group held its first meeting in September 2025 and are working to develop an action plan which will be published next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12, following the closure of the public consultation on adult child ratios and the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration countersigned by their GP.
answer_text: The consultation exercise on the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care closed on Friday 20 June 2025.   All responses have been collated and are currently being reviewed by the department.  Once completed a consultation report is to be produced.If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these changes will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers.The Review Reference Group will also be considering the wider Minimum Standards in detail and assisting with the drafting of a redesigned set of Standards for consultation in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the consultation on the proposed traffic calming scheme for Ravenhill Park, Onslow Parade, Belfast.
answer_text: During the consultation period on this proposed traffic calming scheme my Department received several objections / representations.  These will be considered in the coming months, and I have asked my officials to engage with the elected members and residents’ association before any decision is made on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what consideration has been given to the installation of a footpath along the full stretch of the Glencregagh Road, South Belfast, given the large amount of school pupils who walk in the area.
answer_text: My Department has not previously received a request to provide a new footway on Glencregagh Road, however officials have completed an assessment to determine its viability.  This involved an initial assessment against three criteria, Safety, Accessibility and Integration, and Economy. Unfortunately, due to constrained resources, work of this nature has to be taken forward on a prioritised basis and this potential scheme has not scored highly enough to pass an initial threshold. I can advise that my Department has recently completed a footway extension on Church Road, which connects to Manse Road and officials are developing plans to construct an additional 620m of new footway on the Manse Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what consideration has been given to the installation of a footpath along the Rocky Road, South Belfast, given the large number of school pupils who walk in the area.
answer_text: In 2022, my Department completed an assessment to determine the viability of extending the existing footway on Rocky Road to Manse Road.  This involved an initial assessment against three criteria, Safety, Accessibility and Integration, and Economy. Unfortunately, due to constrained resources, work of this nature has to be taken forward on a prioritised basis and this potential scheme has not scored highly enough to pass an initial threshold. However, I can advise that my Department has recently completed a footway extension on Church Road which connects to Manse Road and officials are currently developing plans to construct an additional 620m of new footway on the Manse Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her Department's assessment of the safety for pedestrians on the Rocky Road, South Belfast.
answer_text: In 2022, my Department completed an assessment to determine the viability of extending the existing footway on Rocky Road to Manse Road.  This involved an initial assessment against three criteria, Safety, Accessibility and Integration, and Economy. Unfortunately, due to constrained resources, work of this nature has to be taken forward on a prioritised basis and this potential scheme has not scored highly enough to pass an initial threshold. However, I can advise that my Department has recently completed a footway extension on Church Road which connects to Manse Road and officials are currently developing plans to construct an additional 620m of new footway on the Manse Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the latest road traffic assessment was conducted along the (i) Rocky Road; and (ii) Glencregagh Road, South Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that my officials last assessed Rocky Road in July 2025 in relation to the use of the road by HGVs and request for additional signage; and Glencregagh Road in July 2024 in relation to a traffic calming request.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the improvements in (i) service delivery; (ii) time savings; (iii) cost reductions; and (iv) patient outcomes that were made through the introduction of Artificial Intelligence to support the detection of fractures in Emergency Departments and Minor Injury Units in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust during the two month trial period between 20 January 2025 and 20 March 2025.
answer_text: Implementation of this Artificial Intelligence (AI) model at Northern Health and Social Care Trust is continuing for the duration of 2025/26 and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are now live.  This service model is expected to be rolled out to other Trusts in the 2025/26 financial year pending the necessary local information governance approval. This AI tool has improved the diagnostic accuracy of extremity x-rays being performed by emergency staff at the immediate point of care, prior to specialist radiology interpretation of the x-ray.Benefits have included increased diagnostic accuracy which has resulted in a reduction in missed fractures from 6.7% to 1.2% compared to the pre-go-live evaluation stage.  However, more data will be required to detail the full cost reductions. The reduction in missed fracture rates has improved patient outcomes due to greater accuracy in results and correct treatment.  As a result, fewer patients had to be recalled for a treatment change. At present it can take approximately 48 – 72 hours before specialist radiology staff perform the second reading of the x-ray.  However, AI interpretation occurs in real-time, therefore there is a potential time saving of up to three days. Improved diagnostic accuracy has also led to a reduction in phone calls and queries relating to missed fractures by 80% thereby freeing up more consultant time to perform other tasks.  My Department will continue to monitor the benefits of this new service model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of research to evaluate how Artificial Intelligence tools can support literacy among disadvantaged pupils and those with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Reading with AI research project which is being taken forward as a Cross-Cutting Initiative within the RAISE Programme, will provide participating schools with an opportunity to see how AI tools can support literacy. The study is led by Oxford Brookes University, using Amira Learning, a reading support tool powered by AI, and is aligned with research-informed approaches to early literacy.More than 150 schools and over 15,000 pupils (including the control group) are taking part in the research project.  In order to support participating schools, in-person training was provided to Reading Champions on 12 September 2025 and two further online training webinars took place on 23 and 24 September for teachers. An initial benchmark assessment will take place for all children participating in the project in early October.  It is expected that the project will be completed by the end of the 2025-26 Academic Year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 27857/22-27, for an update on (i) the utilisation of Artificial Intelligence in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) whether it will be rolled out to other Health and Social Care Trusts within the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: Implementation of this Artificial Intelligence (AI) model at Northern Health and Social Care Trust is continuing for the duration of 2025/26 and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are now live.  This service model is expected to be rolled out to other Trusts in the 2025/26 financial year pending the necessary local information governance approval. This AI tool has improved the diagnostic accuracy of extremity x-rays being performed by emergency staff at the immediate point of care, prior to specialist radiology interpretation of the x-ray.Benefits have included increased diagnostic accuracy which has resulted in a reduction in missed fractures from 6.7% to 1.2% compared to the pre-go-live evaluation stage.  However, more data will be required to detail the full cost reductions. The reduction in missed fracture rates has improved patient outcomes due to greater accuracy in results and correct treatment.  As a result, fewer patients had to be recalled for a treatment change. At present it can take approximately 48 – 72 hours before specialist radiology staff perform the second reading of the x-ray.  However, AI interpretation occurs in real-time, therefore there is a potential time saving of up to three days. Improved diagnostic accuracy has also led to a reduction in phone calls and queries relating to missed fractures by 80% thereby freeing up more consultant time to perform other tasks.  My Department will continue to monitor the benefits of this new service model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on (i) the implementation of part-time 20mph speed limit zones outside schools in South Belfast; and (ii) when future tranches will be announced.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.This third tranche focuses on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies while considering the safety implications of introducing lower speed limits. There are no schools in South Belfast included in Tranche 3.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 27653/22-27; (ii) AQW 27654/22-27; and (iii) AQW 27655/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6941/22-27, for an update on the implementation of the findings and recommendations of the Department’s review of residents’ parking schemes, including (i) the expected timeframe for announcing the new application; and (ii) the assessment process for demand-led schemes.
answer_text: I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to residents’ parking schemes that provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future residents’ parking schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 27178/22-27, to provide an update on the publication of the childcare business support scoping study.
answer_text: The Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Research Report published on my department’s website on 24 September and can be accessed at Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research  .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) how she will address road safety concerns on the Hillsborough Road, Carryduff; and (ii) when a road safety assessment will be carried out at this location.
answer_text: In July 2025, and in response to a similar query regarding road safety concerns, my officials carried out a review of the Hillsborough Road. The road is currently subject to a 30mph speed limit and has central hatching and edge line road marking traffic calming features to narrow the running lanes.  An investigation of the history of personal injury collisions was also carried out and this did not highlight any areas of particular concern. As such the Department has no plans to alter the current road arrangement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of times her Department instigated enforcement proceedings against a developer under the Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980, in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My Department have issued 16 enforcement notices under Article 11 of the Private Streets Order in the 2024/25 year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the road adoption process for Bracken Hill Drive, South Belfast.
answer_text: Bracken Hill Drive, Belfast is a private development site being developed by Dermont No.2 LLP Ltd.  The planning approved development is approved under reference Y/2013/0030/F. The works are incomplete due to defective traffic calming measures and outstanding geotechnical and Vehicle Restraint System certifications.  The responsibility for completion of the works lies with the developer at this time.My department will continue to engage with Dermont No.2 LLP Ltd to achieve a resolution for residents, whereby the developer fulfils their obligation to provide adoptable standards for Bracken Hill Drive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of bonded stretches of roads awaiting adoption.
answer_text: There are currently 3521 bonded stretches in my Department’s Private Streets database. This figure includes bonded stretches at various stages of completion, ranging from those where construction work has yet to be commenced to those which are in the process of final adoption.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether it is his Department's policy that Health and Social Care Trusts take account of a family’s Disability Living Allowance or personal finances when deciding on the allocation of services for children with disabilities.
answer_text: There is no specific departmental policy stipulating that Health and Social Care Trusts are to take account of a family’s Disability Living Allowance or personal finances when deciding on the allocation of services for Children with Disabilities. The assessment of support service need, and assessment of support service allocation for families who care for children with disability is based on an assessment of need / eligibility completed by the respective Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) legal; and (ii) policy, basis on which Health and Social Care Trusts may request information from families about how Disability Living Allowance is spent when assessing or allocating children’s social care services.
answer_text: Disability Living Allowance for children may help with the extra costs of looking after a child who:is under 16has difficulty walking or needs more looking after than a child of the same age who does not have a disability.My department has not currently issued guidance to Health and Social Care Trusts instructing Trusts on the use of Disability Living Allowance information in service allocation. Within Children’s Services, the Children (NI) Order underpins the assessment of need and service provision. Across all HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland – the Understanding The Needs of Children In Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) assessment framework is the standardised assessment model that is used within children’s services to measure assessed need for children and families – this includes assessed need in terms of support required, and also for assessment of service allocation based on need. The UNOCINI is an inter-agency assessment model that was developed in Northern Ireland to:improve the quality of assessment within stakeholder agencies.assist in communicating the needs of children across agencies.avoid the escalation of children’s needs through early identification of need and effective intervention. UNOCINI has three assessment areas which are divided into four domains: The needs of the child or young person. The capacity of their parents’ or carers’ to meet these needs. Wider family and environmental factors that impact on parental capacity and children’s needs. With regard to the completion of a holistic assessment of need – understanding the level of Disability Living Allowance paid, and also how this financial benefit is being used would assist HSC Trusts with ensuring a comprehensive assessment of the child and family’s needs. Therefore, a family’s finances may be considered during the assessment process (inclusive of requests regarding how certain financial benefits are being used), as it is important that Trusts understand all factors that may have an impact on a child’s circumstances – inclusive of if there may also be potential financial pressures to consider / financial implications that could potentially impact parental capacity to meet a child’s holistic need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department ensures that families of children with complex needs are treated equitably in service allocation decisions compared to families whose children access Health and Social Care Trust-funded group activities.
answer_text: It is important that HSC Trusts identify and assess the needs of all vulnerable children to ensure that needs are appropriately met at an early stage. Support services and any potential funding that may be available to families of children with disability are based on an assessment of need / eligibility made by the respective Health & Social Care Trust.Across all HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland – the Understanding The Needs of Children In Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) assessment framework is the standardised assessment model that is used within children’s services to measure assessed need for children and families – this includes assessed need in terms of support required, and also for assessment of service allocation based on need. The UNOCINI is an inter-agency assessment model that was developed in Northern Ireland to:improve the quality of assessment within stakeholder agencies.assist in communicating the needs of children across agencies.avoid the escalation of children’s needs through early identification of need and effective intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has issued guidance to Health and Social Care Trusts on the use of Disability Living Allowance information in service allocation processes.
answer_text: Disability Living Allowance for children may help with the extra costs of looking after a child who:is under 16has difficulty walking or needs more looking after than a child of the same age who does not have a disability.My department has not currently issued guidance to Health and Social Care Trusts instructing Trusts on the use of Disability Living Allowance information in service allocation. Within Children’s Services, the Children (NI) Order underpins the assessment of need and service provision. Across all HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland – the Understanding The Needs of Children In Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) assessment framework is the standardised assessment model that is used within children’s services to measure assessed need for children and families – this includes assessed need in terms of support required, and also for assessment of service allocation based on need. The UNOCINI is an inter-agency assessment model that was developed in Northern Ireland to:improve the quality of assessment within stakeholder agencies.assist in communicating the needs of children across agencies.avoid the escalation of children’s needs through early identification of need and effective intervention. UNOCINI has three assessment areas which are divided into four domains: The needs of the child or young person. The capacity of their parents’ or carers’ to meet these needs. Wider family and environmental factors that impact on parental capacity and children’s needs. With regard to the completion of a holistic assessment of need – understanding the level of Disability Living Allowance paid, and also how this financial benefit is being used would assist HSC Trusts with ensuring a comprehensive assessment of the child and family’s needs. Therefore, a family’s finances may be considered during the assessment process (inclusive of requests regarding how certain financial benefits are being used), as it is important that Trusts understand all factors that may have an impact on a child’s circumstances – inclusive of if there may also be potential financial pressures to consider / financial implications that could potentially impact parental capacity to meet a child’s holistic need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work her Department is undertaking regarding the regulation of short-term holiday lets.
answer_text: My Department’s and Tourism NI’s powers to regulate the visitor/tourist accommodation sector, commonly referred to as short-term holiday lets, are set out in the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 (“the 1992 Tourism Order”). The 1992 Tourism Order requires all visitor/tourist accommodation in the North to be inspected and certified by Tourism NI before they can operate, and existing businesses undergo further statutory inspections at least once every four years to ensure that specified minimum quality standards are met.These minimum quality standards are set out in regulations, and denote the quality and provisions of standards, cleanliness, maintenance, safety, essential facilities and comfort etc. Tourism NI enforces these regulations through its statutory Certification Scheme. My Department and Tourism NI recently completed a review of the visitor/tourist accommodation Certification Scheme and its associated statutory criteria regulations, and are currently preparing to launch a public consultation on proposed changes to obtain wider stakeholder and industry views. These proposed changes relate to the significant changes in market trends regarding the types of accommodation being provided by industry, e.g. the rise in new and alternative accommodation offerings such as aparthotels, glamping pods etc., as well as introducing changes to the minimum standards and offerings visitors expect. It should be noted, however, that the 1992 Tourism Order does not provide any power for my Department or Tourism NI to limit, cap or control the number or spread of properties providing accommodation for visitors/tourists.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline when the Review of Higher Education Funding will be published.
answer_text: I have recently endorsed the draft Terms of Reference for the Review of Higher Education Funding and these have been shared with key stakeholders including the Committee, local Higher Education Institutions, as well as unions.As an initial step, the first stage of the Review, a Financial Needs Assessment of our Higher Education Institutions, is underway and due to be completed during the autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 29156/22-27, to outline whether she still intends to introduce all of these Bills within the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: Yes, I do intend to introduce the Bills advised in my previous response of 21 July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps she is taking to increase the amount of electricity produced from renewable sources, given that there was a decrease of 2.8 percentage points on the previous 12 month period, for the year ending June 2025.
answer_text: Delivering additional renewable electricity generation is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance.It requires joined-up working, across government and with our delivery partners. My Department does not hold all the levers, so collaboration is essential to unlock progress.The Energy Strategy Action Plan 2025 outlines a range of actions my Department is taking forward this year to increase the levels of renewable electricity generation.To drive progress, my Department has appointed a dedicated Project Director through the Strategic Investment Board. The Project Director is providing strategic leadership and building effective relationships across departments, industry, and delivery partners. They are working with key stakeholders to shape a practical roadmap that will drive success.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail why MLAs do not have cost free access to Land and Property Services maps as a tool to support constituents.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Land Registry operates on a full cost financial recovery model and fees are set to cover the actual costs of providing registration services, including map searches. This approach ensures that the costs of operating the Registry are recouped from the users of its services.As part of the Land and Property Services (LPS) transformation programme Land Registry will deliver a modern and sustainable IT solution that will enable it to transform current business processes and enable all citizens to access Land Information Services for Registration data online from October 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29334/22-27, to provide an update on the review of funding provided by his Department to support students on pre-registration healthcare programmes.
answer_text: My Department’s review of the funding arrangements for both commissioned, and non-commissioned, tertiary level courses which lead to careers within the HSC is ongoing. This review will help inform the development of options for a public consultation which I hope to launch later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has received the evaluation report in relation to the role of Physician Associates across the health and social care system.
answer_text: I can confirm that my officials have received the draft evaluation report concerning the Physician Associate role across Health and Social Care Trusts. Officials are currently reviewing the report internally, in consultation with the Chief Medical Officer and other relevant colleagues, ahead of its finalisation. I expect to receive the completed report shortly for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how the £5 million allocated to support and expand breast services across Health and Social Care Trusts has been spent; and (ii) what impact it has had on red flag breast cancer waiting times.
answer_text: (i) On 30 May 2025, the Department of Health published the Elective Care Plan Implementation and Funding Plan for 2025/26, which included £5 million in recurrent funding for breast services under the Elective Care Framework (ECF). Between period April to July 2025 approximately £300,000 was spent non-recurrently on waiting list initiatives, enabling the assessment and treatment of an additional 541 patients through a combination of inhouse and Independent Sector capacity.  Further non-recurrent investment is anticipated in the coming months as part of ongoing efforts to reduce the backlog and improve access to timely care.The Department of Health (DoH), in collaboration with Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts, is actively progressing a range of strategic solutions to address both immediate pressures and long-term service sustainability. These include drawing on best practice from other jurisdictions, exploring changes to workforce skill mix, and redesigning care pathways to improve efficiency and outcomes.The development of care pathway solutions and development of detailed business cases is expected to take a further 8–12 months.  This timeline reflects the need for robust planning and due diligence. The funding is anticipated to be committed on a recurrent basis, subject to the outcome of this work and agreement on the necessary service reforms and business case approvals. (ii)  To address immediate pressures and maintain service delivery, the Department has implemented a series of coordinated actions:Prioritisation of red flag referrals within existing core capacity to ensure timely access for the most clinically urgent cases.Ongoing regional coordination to accelerate delivery of targeted interventions, including WLI Breast Mega Clinics.Consideration of Independent Sector support to supplement short-term capacity.The Department remains committed to expanding timely access to breast cancer assessment services.  I fully acknowledge the ongoing challenges, particularly the unacceptable waiting times for red flag patients, and the distress this causes across Northern Ireland. These issues stem from a combination of funding shortfalls and missed opportunities for system reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration she has given to Act Now's report, Invest NI, F-35s and Israel: Public Money for War Crimes.
answer_text: As previously advised, I have instructed Invest NI to undertake a review of its investment portfolio to ensure that no public funds are supporting projects involved in the supply of arms to Israel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 27402/22-27, to detail when he expects to receive a response from Treasury.
answer_text: ANSWERMy officials are continuing to engage with Treasury colleagues on their review and consideration of the Department for the Economy’s non-domestic RHI Scheme business case. Whilst I would hope to receive a response from Treasury as soon as practicable, I am unable to provide a timeline for their response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action she is taking to ensure that manufacturers, being supported by Invest NI, are not contributing in any way to war efforts which are in breach of international law.
answer_text: Invest NI has assured me that it does not support projects that provide arms or their components to Israel. Invest NI is currently carrying out a review on investments in order to provide specific further reassurance on this question. Following the outcome of this review and the ongoing Judicial Review, I will consider if any further actions are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action she is taking to address concerns that Invest NI is providing support for companies that are supplying parts and components being used in Israeli military equipment.
answer_text: As previously advised, I have instructed Invest NI to undertake a review of its investment portfolio to ensure that no public funds are supporting projects involved in the supply of arms to Israel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 14260/22-27, to detail an update on the development of Mid Down Integrated College.
answer_text: The Mid-Down Steering Group, as proposer, has a statutory obligation for implementing the approved Development Proposal. The preferred day-one accommodation will be identified through a business case, following further work by the Steering Group to define the school’s needs. The Education Authority and Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education are supporting the Steering Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 will be published.
answer_text: The Eastern Transport Plan is the largest of the local transport plans and will integrate with the Local Development Plans of the Antrim and Newtownabbey; Ards and North Down; Belfast; Lisburn and Castlereagh; and Mid and East Antrim councils. Due to the five local council areas being at different stages in their Local Development Plans, the Eastern Transport Plan will therefore be published in iterations. As Belfast City Council is most advanced, the first iteration will cover the Belfast City Centre Transport Plan and Metropolitan Area Transport Plan. A draft of these elements of the Eastern Transport Plan is being prepared and is scheduled for public engagement in Autumn 2025. Publication will depend on the engagement responses but is anticipated to be early in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23190/22-27, for an update on what progress has been made on exploring possible future changes to the financial support provided to students on all pre-registration healthcare programmes.
answer_text: My officials are continuing their review into the breadth of current funding provided in support of students on pre-registration healthcare programmes. This review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I would hope to be in a position to launch that consultation later this year.It is not possible to indicate what future changes will be until the consultation has been completed and responses considered.
