question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what legislation she will introduce (i) in the remainder of 2025; and (ii) in the remainder of the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: I have set out below what legislation I plan to introduce in the remainder of the 2022-27 mandate. Employment Rights BillEnergy – Offshore Renewable Energy InstallationEnergy (Renewable Electricity Support Scheme)Onshore Petroleum Licensing BillNon-domestic RHI Scheme ClosureDecarbonisation Utility Regulator PowersCredit Union Bill

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Department plans to involve (i) parents; and (ii) carers, in careers education.
answer_text: The Careers Action Plan recognises the important role of parents and carers as key influencers in. It highlights the importance of enhanced communication with this group and the need for them to have access to resources and information to enable them to better support their children.  The Plan includes a range of supporting actions including: parental engagement from the earliest decision-making stages of young peoples’ career journey through parent/teacher evenings and other school events; test webinars outlining support for parents and carers especially at subject choice and other key milestones;develop themed resources, for parents, carers and community partners which are readily accessible, including via the new Careers Portal; andprovide a dedicated area on the new Careers Portal to offer information to support parents’ career conversations with their young people to understand skills in demand from employers and suggest ways to explore the different routes and pathways available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to the Careers Action Plan 2025-2028, to detail (i) at what age careers education will be introduced in primary schools; and (ii) how this content will be tailored to suit younger pupils at different developmental stages.
answer_text: The ‘Independent Review of Careers’, produced by Dr Deirdre Hughes, highlighted that early engagement with children on careers education is beneficial and recommended the need to introduce careers education in primary schools. The value and importance of this is reflected in the careers action plan.Steps have already been taken to support the delivery of careers education in primary schools through the Drawing the Future programme. In 2024 all primary schools in Northern Ireland were invited to participate in ‘Drawing the Future’.  This programme is aimed at Key Stage 2 learners (Primary 5-7).  It is based on Primary Futures which was developed by the Education and Employers charity whose programmes have featured as examples of good practice in OECD case study collections.  The programme adopts an engaging and age-appropriate approach which involves learners drawing a picture of what job they would like to do in the future and asking them several brief questions, including:if they knew anyone who did that job and if not, how they had heard about it;their favourite school subject; age;gender; why the job is of interest and what other jobs they are interested in; and if they think they might go to university or do an apprenticeship.This is followed up with direct engagement with employers who participate in a range of activities with the children aimed at broadening their awareness of careers, increasing their understanding of the world of work and raising their aspirations. The “Drawing the Future: Exploring the Career Aspirations of Children in Northern Ireland” report was published on 29 January 2025. To share learning about the programme the EA continues to hold virtual events with primary schools.As detailed in the Careers Action Plan, and subject to funding and resources, the Drawing the Future programme will be expanded in primary schools through a mixture of virtual and in-person events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether there are any plans (i) to provide additional training and resources for careers teachers; or (ii) to increase access to careers service specialists within schools, in order to deliver on the Careers Action Plan 2025-2028.
answer_text: Strategic Outcome 3 (establishing quality standards and developing the capacity of all partners within the careers system to deliver high quality and consistent support) in the Careers Action Plan provides details of the actions my department will be taking in relation to providing additional training and resources for careers teachers.  These include:working in partnership with the Northern Ireland Schools & Colleges Careers Association (NISCA) to develop shared standards for careers in schools;develop a careers education delivery framework in consultation with NISCA which details the expected careers education model for KS3 through to Post-16;work with Area Learning Community (ALC) Careers sub-groups to disseminate best practice in careers delivery across all ALCs;strengthen teacher training programmes to include careers education and career guidance theory, research and best practice;Develop a careers framework which sets out the services that should be available within local communities through collaboration with ALCs, local councils and Labour Market Partnerships.DfE’s Careers Service works in partnership with schools to support the careers education programme.  They have a Partnership Agreement in place with each post primary school in Northern Ireland and their Advisors provide professional careers advice and guidance at key transition points.  Questions in relation to the Careers Service would be a matter for the Minister for the Economy however, one of the commitments included under strategic objective 4 of the Careers Action Plan is that the Careers Service will ‘reform the delivery of careers, including the provision offered in schools and with adults and better identify and target those most in need’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the Careers Action Plan 2025-28 will promote a broader understanding of career options, including apprenticeships and vocational training.
answer_text: My Department’s Careers Advisers are already required to offer impartial advice and guidance, and highlight relevant career options, to clients of all ages and abilities. This includes a wide range of Careers Bulletins that set out the wide range of pathways available to access jobs in sectors across our economy. However, the joint Careers Action Plan seeks to establish a much wider, inclusive careers ecosystem that connects people to learning and employment opportunities throughout their lives, including in apprenticeships and vocational training. The main aim of the joint Careers Action Plan is to establish a universally accessible, high quality service that supports people across all stages of life. The Plan also seeks to ensure that all providers of careers, including the Advisers of the Careers Service and teachers in schools, work more closely, share resources and knowledge, embrace new technologies, drive professionalism and collaborate to benefit all of our people. Key outcomes include ensuring shared quality standards across all settings; delivering a new Careers Portal; enhancing special educational needs provision; and developing careers from an earlier stage of the curriculum. One of the seven strategic outcomes of the Careers Action Plan is specifically focused on working with parents, carers and other community partners. This includes an action to provide a dedicated area on the new Careers Portal to support parents’ career conversations with their young people to understand skills in demand from employers and suggest ways to explore the different routes and pathways available, including in apprenticeships.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what discussions she has had with the Minister of Education to ensure the needs of employers are met through the school curriculum.
answer_text: Building on the 14-19 Framework, Ministers have agreed to establish a Cross-Departmental Ministerial Group. This group will ensure that the Department for the Economy and Department of Education work collaboratively to implement joint Ministerial priorities. Additionally, my Department has contributed to the Department of Education’s Strategic Curriculum Review, led by Lucy Crehan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the delivery of continuing healthcare.
answer_text: As you may be aware, my Department is currently subject to legal proceedings regarding the Continuing Healthcare policy in Northern Ireland. There is no further update available at this time, and my Department will consider any action that may be required once the matter has been settled.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of (i) the number of staff employed; and (ii) their job roles at the DfI Roads Eastern Division office.
answer_text: There are a total of 143 staff employed in DfI Roads Eastern Division, undertaking both Professional and Technical and administrative roles.Professional & TechnicalAdministrativeNumber of Staff11132The professional and technical staff are both civil and electrical engineers undertaking work within the division such as road maintenance, traffic management, development control, street lighting, delivery of minor works, collision remedial schemes, and operation of the Traffic Information and Control Centre.Administrative staff undertake back office functions such as lands searches, acquisitions and abandonments, and provide support to the civil and electrical engineering teams.It is important to note that many of the staff employed are in part time and job share roles.  The total number of staff employed is therefore greater than the Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) staffing number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the use of (i) community centres; or (ii) council facilities as emergency shelter for people at risk.
answer_text: Usage of these facilities as emergency shelter is a well-established practice in the UK and is endorsed by the NI Emergency Preparedness Group Emergency Support Centre Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education if he has considered the development of guidance for the use of Artificial Intelligence in schools.
answer_text: My officials are working with a range of education partners to develop guidance for schools on Artificial Intelligence.  I anticipate that guidance will be made available to schools in the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the development of a specialised mother and baby unit; and (ii) whether he will publish the business case in relation to the unit.
answer_text: An earlier independent review identified that the model of the proposed 6/7 bedded Mother and Baby Unit should be that as recommended by the Royal College of Psychiatrists and the location of the Mother and Baby Unit should be the City Hospital site, within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. I have already made it clear to officials within the Department that I wish to see the issue progressed at pace.However, given the importance of the service, the latest position is that PHA colleagues have been asked for their input to strengthen the case for a 7 bedded unit so that the unit can be future proofed. Belfast Trust are at a very advanced stage in the development of a capital and revenue business case. They have advised that once they receive instruction on the number of beds required, it will take 6 weeks to finalise the business case.  However, progression of the unit will remain subject to the necessary funding being identified.While the development of a Mother and Baby Unit remains a longer-term strategic objective, my Department has committed to strengthening existing provision in the interim through:Investment in community-based specialist perinatal mental health services.Upskilling of perinatal clinical staff.Implementation of the Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathways and associated guidanceImplementation of crisis intervention pathways and out-of-hours support for women experiencing acute distress.Ongoing engagement with service users and advocacy groups to ensure care remains person-centred and responsive to the needs of mothers and families. Development of a Service User Leaflet to explain the Perinatal Mental HealthThere are no plans to publish the business case in relation to the unit due to potential commercial sensitivities and the fact that progress of the Mother and Baby Unit remains subject to the necessary funding being identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he is seeking to develop AI literacy skills amongst school pupils, in light of the TechFirst programme recently launched by the UK Government.
answer_text: On 2 June 2025, I announced a new major research study to evaluate how AI tools can support literacy among disadvantaged pupils and those with Special Educational Needs.  This research project is one of seven cross-cutting initiatives recently announced under the RAISE Programme to be delivered in the 2025/26 academic year.Furthermore, the findings of the independent Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum have recently been published.  The report identifies significant weaknesses in the current curriculum, which had not been formally reviewed in over 17 years.  It recommends that my Department develops a new curriculum framework for Northern Ireland which has greater specificity about the important knowledge, concepts and skills that children should learn at each stage of their education.  Whilst not exclusive to AI literacy, the report recommends that ‘Digital Technology’ should be introduced as a strand within the curriculum with its own sequenced statutory content, based within ‘Science and Technology’ from key stage 1 to key stage 3, and as a sub-strand within ‘Employability’ at key stage 4.  It also proposes that the use of digital technology should still be embedded across the curriculum to support the goal of digital capability.  I will respond formally to the report in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to the TechFirst programme recently lunched by the UK Government, to detail how she is seeking to develop Artificial Intelligence literacy skills within our further education system.
answer_text: My Department is committed to addressing the significant opportunities in emerging technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI). My officials are working with the Further Education (FE) Sector to ensure it can develop AI literacy skills within FE Colleges. Through the FE Digital IT Hub, FE Colleges are continually updating the computing curriculum to ensure it is current and industry relevant. This has included development and delivery of AI courses, modules and embedding of AI in curriculum delivery.  The sector works together to enhance the AI skills of all FE lecturing staff and continues to collaborate to share best practise, ensuring the alignment of AI literacy across all 6 colleges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what recent discussions she has had in relation to devolution and reform of the Apprenticeship Levy.
answer_text: The Apprenticeship Levy is a reserved matter. While the Fiscal Commission recommended that there is a strong case for the devolution of the Apprenticeship Levy, the Commission also stated that this levy should only be devolved in conjunction with income tax, given the likely administration costs of pursuing devolution of this tax in isolation. I have had several recent discussions with stakeholders in relation to the Apprenticeship Levy, including CBI, Skills Council and other leaders in business and industry. My officials have ongoing engagement with other jurisdictions on apprenticeship matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what discussions she has had with the UK Government in relation to the newly launched TechFirst programme.
answer_text: My officials have initiated discussions with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology to determine the scope and funding arrangements for the TechFirst programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what consideration has been given to providing additional childcare to (i) home; and (ii) and international students, enrolled in (a) part-time; (b) postgraduate; and (c) PhD courses, in our higher education institutions.
answer_text: My Department provides a means-tested childcare grant to help towards the costs of childcare for eligible full-time undergraduate home students. The grant is not available to international, part-time, postgraduate or PhD students.I recognise the rising cost of childcare and the impact this has on parents and families who are already struggling with the cost of living. Officials are currently finalising the draft terms of reference for the review of Higher Education Funding and will brief me in the coming weeks. The review is likely to involve three specific strands of work including a review of the package of support that is available for students. Financial support for approved childcare may also be available, depending on household circumstances.  Further information can be found on nidirect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has assessed the long-term economic impact of failing to provide sufficient (i) English language education; (ii) training; and (iii) qualifications, to young asylum seekers and refugees.
answer_text: My Department recognises the critical role that skills including language proficiency play in driving economic growth. This aligns with my ‘Good Jobs’ economic priority which focuses on:quality employment opportunitiesupskilling the workforce ensuring inclusive access to jobs across the North of IrelandThere is a wide breadth of English language provision available through Further Education colleges. Additionally, in recent years my Department has secured funding from the British Home Office to deliver targeted programmes equipping asylum seekers and refugees with key skills needed to progress in education and employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of (i) asylum seekers; and (ii) refugees, aged between 16 and 24, who are currently enrolled in courses in further education colleges in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Due to the UK Statistics Authority’s rules regarding the use of published statistics, we are unable to provide the statistics for the current 2024/25 academic year until formal publication by the Department's Analytical Services Division in December of this year. Data is available for previous years and can be found in the ‘Further Education College Statistics’ section of the Department’s website which can be accessed here - https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/publications/further-education-ad-hoc-tables.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what coordination exists between her Department and other Executive departments, to ensure young asylum seekers and refugees, aged between 16 and 24, can access appropriate and timely education and skills development, in line with the Draft Refugee Integration Strategy.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of a coordinated approach to support young asylum seekers and refugees in accessing education and skills development opportunities.  I am committed to working with other Executive Departments to ensure the Refugee Integration Strategy is implemented effectively.  My officials have actively contributed to the development of the Refugee Integration Strategy, from initial planning stage through to the development of the outcomes for the delivery framework.As part of the delivery framework, my Department is currently leading the development of an English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) policy to align with the Refugee Integration Strategy.  Executive Departments and relevant sectoral partners are represented on the Policy Advisory Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration she has given to conducting a review of all the components of the skills eco-system, as recommended within the Green Skills Action Plan.
answer_text: The Green Skills Action Plan recommended a review of the skills ecosystem. The Public Accounts Committee’s Report on Developing the Skills for Northern Ireland’s Future was published on 29 May 2025 and made a number of recommendations which relate to this issue, and my department is currently considering its response to that report. When this response is finalised, I will consider how and when this work will be progressed, dependent on the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will commit to revising the Skills Strategy for Northern Ireland, to explicitly include the needs of (i) refugees; (ii) asylum seekers; and (iii) English for speakers of other language learners, in line with the Draft Refugee Integration Strategy’s priority outcomes on inclusive access to education and employment pathways.
answer_text: I do not intend to revise the Skills Strategy as my focus is on the development of the Skills Action Plan which I intend to publish shortly.  However, my Department recognises that the ability to speak the English language is a key enabler for integration into society and as such offers free ESOL provision via the further education college network to a number of vulnerable groups, including refugees and asylum seekers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the development of policy related to post-19 special educational needs provision; and (ii) to outline what work will be undertaken by the UK Research and Innovation policy fellowship, hosted by her Department in relation to this policy area.
answer_text: My officials have recently submitted a detailed report to me following their extensive review into the education, skills and training provision, and associated legislative protections, for school leavers with special educational needs.  I am currently considering the findings and intend to make a Statement to the Assembly later this month on the next steps.The Fellowship application process closes in mid-July and the 18-month role commences next February. It involves an initial inception phase, during which time the successful fellow will codesign the scope of the work in collaboration with my Departmental officials.  We anticipate this involving research using the novel Longitudinal Education Outcomes for Northern Ireland (LEO NI) and expert longitudinal analysis on the barriers faced by school leavers with Special Educational Needs and the education pathways taken into training, further and higher education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to the use of Artificial Intelligence to analyse roads for defects and potholes.
answer_text: My Department is currently investigating a number of emerging technologies that interpret and categorise road defects, such as potholes, from images collected on a camera fixed to a moving vehicle.   The proof-of-concept trials are underway to validate the solution accuracy and repeatability to determine if it can provide efficiency benefits within the current road safety inspection and maintenance strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21422/22-27, whether any further (i) trials; (i) proof of concepts; and (iii) pilot projects related to the use of artificial intelligence in the health service are currently (a) underway; and (b) under consideration by his Department or any of the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My department continues to assess how artificial intelligence (AI) can be used to manage resources more effectively, ensuring that clinical resources are targeted where they have the greatest impact. Areas which are being considered include the use of AI within Radiology departments and the application of  AI to support demand analysis to allow better planning and targeting of healthcare resources. Other areas include the use of AI to support pre-operative assessment services to ensure better assessment of surgical risk.  However robust testing and evaluation will be necessary to ensure the safety and effectiveness of AI in healthcare.Looking ahead, we are committed to expanding AI capabilities through strategic partnerships, investment in digital infrastructure, and workforce training. These efforts are part of a broader vision to deliver a more responsive, data-driven, and patient-centred health service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21422/22-27, to provide an update on the outcome of the formal audit and evaluation of the use of artificial intelligence to improve the detection of fractures.
answer_text: The work to introduce Artificial Intelligence (AI) to improve the detection of fractures in Emergency Departments and Minor Injury Units in the Northern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust ran for a two-month period from 20 January 2025 to 20 March 2025.  The evaluation of the pilot found that this new service model had significant potential and it was agreed that the utilisation of this Artificial Intelligence would be extended in the Northern HSC Trust for 2025/26.  A process is currently underway to scope how this initiative will be rolled out across the region, enabling the remaining HSC Trusts to use AI for the detection of fractures during 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has given any consideration to creating an online portal for the reporting of damaged cycling infrastructure.
answer_text: Currently the existing NI Direct public portal system caters for defects within the road and footpath network. Rather than creating a new online public portal solely dedicated to cycle ways and or greenways, it may be beneficial to install a damaged cycle way category option and/or drop-down bar within the existing NI Direct public portal system to report such a defect.I have asked my officials to consider the introduction of such a category.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18399/22-27, whether she has given any consideration to (i) providing more robust cycling infrastructure in Upper Arthur Street, Belfast; and (ii) improving enforcement of damage to cycling infrastructure.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering future priority schemes within the Belfast Cycling Network and will consider the existing layout in Upper Arthur Street as part of that review.Whilst my department will look to recover the costs of damage to any of its infrastructure, in practice this is can difficult and time consuming as the perpetrators are often not easily identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on when the independent final evaluation of the JobStart 50+ scheme will be published.
answer_text: The final evaluation is expected to be completed by end of July 2025 and forwarded to HM Treasury to review and consider timeline for publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18399/22-27, whether the broken and missing cycling wands in Upper Arthur Street, Belfast, can be replaced.
answer_text: The location of the cycling wands in Upper Arthur Street, Belfast are a continued problem with damage caused on a regular basis by poor or inconsiderate driving.  I am advised that my officials have visited the location and identified six cycling wands requiring repair or replacement. A works instruction will be passed to the contractor to programme the replacement of the missing cycling wands, and it is anticipated that the work will be completed in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement he has had with his counterparts in the (i) UK Government; (ii) Scottish Government; and (iii) Welsh Government in relation to improving the processes for transferring medical records when people move from (a) Great Britain to Northern Ireland; and (b) Northern Ireland to Great Britain.
answer_text: My Department’s Chief Digital Information Officer (CDIO) is a member of a four UK jurisdictions forum which considers the receiving and sharing of primary care medical records for patients moving from GB to NI and vice versa.It is anticipated that this issue will be raised at its next meeting in March 2026.For records transferring from Scotland, it may be possible to provide an interim solution, with records transferred in PDF format.  BSO is currently engaging with counterparts in Scotland to deliver this interim solution.In recent weeks, staff of the BSO have been in discussions with counterparts in England, who also manage a hub in Wales, to determine if the same interim solution could be extended to their regions. Colleagues in England have advised that they would prefer a UK wide solution and have agreed to undertake factfinding before feeding back.Under current arrangements when patients or service users move permanently to another UK jurisdiction, there are established processes in place between BSO and their UK counterparts to securely transfer GP and other records.When a patient registers with a GP practice in Northern Ireland and the patient has been identified as having relocated to NI from GB, an electronic ‘deduction’ request is processed by the HSC Business Services Organisation (BSO), i.e. a notification is sent to the relevant GB health authority which requests the medical records of the patient to be forwarded to the BSO. Records are transferred from England and Wales to the BSO by courier and from Scotland via a postal delivery service. When records are received into BSO they are forwarded to the new GP practice on the next available weekly delivery via BSO courier. When a patient registers with a GP practice in England a request is made to remove the patient’s details from the Business Services Organisation (BSO) registration system and provide information about the area of England in which the practice is situated.The patient’s GP practice in Northern Ireland is notified to send the medical records via BSO courier service. Once processed by BSO, within 4 to 5 days, records are delivered via BSO courier on a weekly basis to the Primary Care Support England centre in Darlington where they are distributed to the new GP practice in England.In relation to secondary care medical records, these can be obtained by the receiving hospital/consultant requesting a copy of a patient’s medical records, with the patient’s consent, in writing, from the relevant Trust under the purpose of ‘continuity of care’.Alternatively, patients can request a copy of their medical records by making a Subject Access Request (SAR) to the relevant Trust.With the introduction of the encompass system if a patient takes unwell elsewhere in the UK (outside NI) and presents to hospital the hospital can seek consent via EPIC for the patient’s encompass records to be accessed.  Patients who have the My Care app can also share their records with medical staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how (i) GP; and (ii) other, medical records are transferred from (a) England; (b) Scotland; (c) and; Wales, when someone moves to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: When a patient registers with a GP practice in Northern Ireland and the patient has been identified as having relocated to Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom. a notification is sent by the Business Services Organisation to the relevant health authority (in the United Kingdom) which requests the medical records of the patient to be forwarded to BSO. Records are transferred from England and Wales to BSO by courier and from Scotland via a postal delivery service. When records are received by BSO they are forwarded to the new GP practice by BSO courier.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work is underway to improve the systems for transferring medical records from England.
answer_text: My Department are currently exploring electronic solutions such as extending the GP2GP functionality across and between regions of the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 18488/22-27, for an update on the delivery of a bespoke skills action plan for the tourism industry.
answer_text: This will be taken forward by a Tourism Skills Delivery Group which is being established as a sub group of the Skills Council, the advisory body which provides strategic advice on issues pertaining to the development and implementation of skills policy. The Terms of Reference and proposed membership of that Group are currently being agreed and it is planned to hold its inaugural meeting in early summer. The Group will take forward three phases of work:Phase 1: Identify needs of tourism sector and research;Phase 2: Skills Gap Analysis and Quantification;Phase 3: Implementation / DeliveryUpdates on the work of the Group will also be provided to the Tourism Partnership Board who will meet twice a year to monitor and advise on implementation of the Tourism Vision and Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration she has given to developing the agritourism sector as a means of promoting regional balance and supporting rural communities.
answer_text: Agritourism presents a valuable opportunity to support regional balance and promote the sustainable development of our rural communities. Tourism NI actively supports numerous agritourism-related businesses through tourism development programmes and initiatives. Regional balance is a key priority for Tourism NI and its teams work closely with regional councils to identify and support businesses, including those in rural communities and the agritourism-related sector, who can confidently enhance the destination proposition and confidently underpin the wider development of tourism. DAERA delivered the Rural Tourism Scheme under the 2014-2020 Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme. This scheme, co-funded by the EU, invested almost £9 million in tourism and heritage related capital infrastructure projects across the north, with the aim of increasing footfall and economic prosperity for rural areas. In developing a new rural policy, DAERA is engaging with other government departments to explore future opportunities for supporting rural communities and to help identify how DAERA can work more effectively with them in the future to help deliver better outcomes for our rural people and places.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the outcomes of the employment support programmes offered by her Department within the last 12 months, broken down by (i) those with a section 75 category; (ii) the working-age population; and (iii) 16 to 25 year olds, with (a) long-term illness; (b) long-term health conditions; or (c) disability.
answer_text: My Department does not have the policy remit for employment support – this is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.  My Department is responsible for Vocational, Further and Higher Education and Skills policy, with the aim of equipping people to continue with their learning at higher levels or to move into employment.While not an employment support programme, Assured Skills Academies provide pre-employment training courses for individuals aged 18 and over. The training is industry-recognised and on completion of the academy a job interview is guaranteed for existing vacancies with the participating companies. No Section 75 data is gathered by the Department at employment stage.To enhance disability access to full time skills and education programmes, as part of the development of the new Skills for Life and Work 2025 programme a Public Consultation launched in July 2024 on the proposal to integrate additional disability support within the new programme from September 2025. The consultation was open for 13 weeks, closing on 18 October 2024. During the consultation period, discussions took place with stakeholders and engagement sessions with young disabled people who are currently on training programmes were facilitated by Stranmillis University College. The Procurement for an integrated disability support model was launched in May 2025.In addition, My Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis, provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers. This service is available to all, including those with a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 22808/22-27, whether the answer provided remains her current position.
answer_text: I have been assured by Invest NI that it does not provide support towards the manufacture of arms or components used by the Israeli Defence Forces.I have asked Invest NI to review its investments to make absolutely sure that this is the case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total number of 16 to 25 year-olds with a (i) disability; (ii) long-term illness; or (iii) health condition, who have been supported into employment as a result of programmes offered by her Department, within the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department does not have the policy remit for employment support – this is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.  My Department is responsible for Vocational, Further and Higher Education and Skills policy, with the aim of equipping people to continue with their learning at higher levels or to move into employment.While not an employment support programme, Assured Skills Academies provide pre-employment training courses for individuals aged 18 and over. The training is industry-recognised and on completion of the academy a job interview is guaranteed for existing vacancies with the participating companies. The consultation was open for 13 weeks, closing on 18 October 2024. During the consultation period, discussions took place with stakeholders and engagement sessions with young disabled people who are currently on training programmes were facilitated by Stranmillis University College. The Procurement for an integrated disability support model was launched in May 2025.In addition, My Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis, provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers. This service is available to all, including those with a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total number of 16 to 25 year-olds who have been supported into employment as a result of programmes offered by her Department, within the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department does not have the policy remit for employment support – this is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.  My Department is responsible for Vocational, Further and Higher Education and Skills policy, with the aim of equipping people to continue with their learning at higher levels or to move into employment.While not an employment support programme, Assured Skills Academies provide pre-employment training courses for individuals aged 18 and over. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the Section 75 participation within the employment support programmes offered by her Department, over the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department does not have the policy remit for employment support – this is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.  My Department is responsible for Vocational, Further and Higher Education and Skills policy, with the aim of equipping people to continue with their learning at higher levels or to move into employment.While not an employment support programme, Assured Skills Academies provide pre-employment training courses for individuals aged 18 and over. The training is industry-recognised and on completion of the academy a job interview is guaranteed for existing vacancies with the participating companies. The Procurement for an integrated disability support model was launched in May 2025.In addition, My Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis, provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers. This service is available to all, including those with a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline (i) all programmes funded by her Department that support people into employment; and (ii) which of these programmes have supported people with a (a) disability; or (b) long-term health condition, within the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department does not have the policy remit for employment support – this is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.  My Department is responsible for Vocational, Further and Higher Education and Skills policy, with the aim of equipping people to continue with their learning at higher levels or to move into employment.While not an employment support programme, Assured Skills Academies provide pre-employment training courses for individuals aged 18 and over. The training is industry-recognised and on completion of the academy a job interview is guaranteed for existing vacancies with the participating companies. No Section 75 data is gathered by the Department at employment stage.To enhance disability access to full time skills and education programmes, as part of the development of the new Skills for Life and Work 2025 programme a Public Consultation launched in July 2024 on the proposal to integrate additional disability support within the new programme from September 2025. The consultation was open for 13 weeks, closing on 18 October 2024. During the consultation period, discussions took place with stakeholders and engagement sessions with young disabled people who are currently on training programmes were facilitated by Stranmillis University College. The Procurement for an integrated disability support model was launched in May 2025.In addition, My Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis, provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers. This service is available to all, including those with a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on when the business case to close the non-domestic RHI scheme is expected to be approved.
answer_text: The business case to close the non-domestic RHI Scheme is subject to both Department of Finance and Treasury review. My department has reviewed the business case and has submitted it to Treasury for their assessment. We await the outcome of Treasury’s review of the business case.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide to outline when the Investment Strategy will be brought forward for Executive approval.
answer_text: We are currently considering a final draft of the Investment Strategy and we hope to be able to bring it to the Executive for approval in the near future. The Strategy will stabilise and transform investment infrastructure in key areas such as our schools, hospitals, transport, and digital connectivity, over the next 30 years. At the same time, it will recognise the need for regional balance across all the priorities. We want to grow our economy and create a society where everyone leads healthy and productive lives. That is why it is important that the final document is closely aligned with the Programme for Government, and wider Executive priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the outcome of the support for low carbon heat in the residential sector consultation.
answer_text: My department consulted on future support of low carbon technologies in residential properties, which closed 10th January 2025. The views provided will support my department in the development of policy to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat. Officials are currently analysing the responses provided and my department plans to communicate a feedback report on the consultation through the department's website and social media platforms this summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline when the regulations to implement the Health and Social Care (Control of Data Processing) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 will be brought forward.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care (Control of Data Processing) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 Act received Royal Ascent on 11th April 2016. Following unavoidable interruptions, planning resumed in 2022 for the introduction of the required regulations to enable the sharing of identifiable health data for secondary usages, beyond the limited and specific conditions available at present. The process to draft regulations included a full review of the Act (2016) with guidance from the Department Solicitors office. This review identified a substantive issue in the wording of the 2016 Act, regarding the scope of applicable data which could have encroached on the original intent of the legislation.My Department is currently progressing work to bring a "Bill of Amendment" to address the deficiencies identified.  Although I am seeking to introduce the Bill before the Summer recess, advancement is dependent on the NI Assembly legislative timetable and processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what consideration has been given to the aligning of school finishing times across all primary school year groups.
answer_text: Schools have the autonomy and flexibility to set their own start and finish times.  In determining these times, all schools must take account of the statutory requirements on the minimum number of hours that pupils must be under instruction each day.  This differs for children aged below and above the age of eight.The school’s individual context may influence finishing times such as the need to take account of the availability of transport or traffic congestion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education if any consideration has been given to the mandating of allergy and anaphylaxis training for staff in schools.
answer_text: All Education Authority Catering Service staff receive mandatory annual training on allergy management. This training includes the processes that are to be followed by catering staff to manage the risks posed by allergens in food to children and young people who use the school meals service.My officials have met with parents and organisations representing pupils with medical needs including allergies and are considering the information shared as part of the review of the departments current guidance, ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline: (i) how many schools have a dedicated allergy policy; and (ii) what work is being undertaken to ensure all schools develop such a policy.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it does not collect this information.  My department is progressing an update to the ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’ Guidance, which includes a section on Allergic Reactions/Anaphylaxis, in collaboration with the Department of Health and the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 26556/22-27, to detail what engagement there has been with (i) parents of children with allergies; and (ii) children with allergies, as part of the update to the 'Supporting pupils with Medication Needs' Guidance.
answer_text: My officials have met with parents and organisations representing pupils with medical needs including allergies and are considering the information shared as part of the review of the departments current guidance, ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what consideration has been given to developing a comprehensive food allergy policy to cover all schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) current policy for the management of food allergies in the provision of school meals is that following a child’s parents making a request for a special diet, the parents, school and the EA will work together to agree the arrangements for the child’s special diet provision, including allergen management. A means of identifying children on allergen diets is part of this process.EA catering staff will identify any allergens contained in each dish on the menu in accordance with the Food Standards Agency recommended process. EA catering staff then use this information to ensure children with allergies do not receive foods containing potentially harmful allergens.The EA Catering Service’s special diet management process is currently being reviewed with a view to making further improvements to the process of managing the provision of special diets for children and young people, including those with food allergies.My officials have met with parents and organisations representing pupils with medical needs including allergies and are considering the information shared as part of the review of the departments current guidance, ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what what considerations she gave to the environment when approving mineral prospecting licence applications (i) CA1/25; (ii) CA3/25; (iii) DGA3/25; (iv) DG4A/25; (v) KDRA1/25; (vi) KDRA4/25; and (vii) OMA4/25.
answer_text: My Department assessed each Mineral Prospecting Licence application to identify and consider the potential environmental impacts on the protected sites and their qualifying features within each proposed licence area. The result of that assessment for each of the seven applications was made available to help inform the public consultation process. Once a licence is in place, a Licensee is required to inform the Department of any exploration activity that it proposes to undertake.  My Department, as the recognised Competent Authority, undertakes the necessary environmental assessments in line with the requirements of the Habitats and Birds Directives and associated domestic legislation, namely the Conservation (Natural Habitats etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995 (as amended), The Environment Order (Northern Ireland) 2002 and the Wildlife and Natural Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 and where appropriate, in consultation with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency. A Licensee is required to obtain additional appropriate permissions from other regulatory authorities including, but not limited to, Local Councils, Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) and the Health & Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI).  This cross-Departmental legislative framework aims to protect the natural heritage and minimise environmental impact of exploration activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what consideration was given to the 99.5 per cent of consultations respondents who opposed the granting of mineral prospecting licence applications of  (i) CA1/25; (ii) CA3/25; (iii) DGA3/25; (iv) DG4A/25; (v) KDRA1/25; (vi) KDRA4/25; and (vii) OMA4/25.
answer_text: In line with the requirements of the current legislation, the Mineral Development Act (NI) 1969, my Department completed a public consultation exercise on the Department’s intention to grant the seven mineral prospecting licences,. On 8 May 2025 the Department published its Consultation Summary Report to these applications. This report, which can be found on our website, outlines in detail the Department’s response to each of the issues raised throughout the consultation. A significant majority of respondents made it clear that they were opposed to the gold exploration being undertaken by some of the applicant companies. The Minerals Development Act (NI) 1969 lists the minerals vested in the Department and gold and silver are exempted from that list. Permission to carry out exploration for gold exploration is provided by the Crown Estate through Mines Royal Options and not through a mineral prospecting licence that issue from my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 23667/22-27, to outline when the childcare business support scoping study will be published.
answer_text: The cross-Departmental Steering Group has received the draft Final Report on the Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers and is considering its findings. The report will be finalised as soon as practically possible and published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to clarify why mineral prospecting licence applications (i) CA1/25; (ii) CA3/25; (iii) DGA3/25; (iv) DG4A/25; (v) KDRA1/25; (vi) KDRA4/25; and (vii) OMA4/25 were approved by her Department.
answer_text: Under the Mineral Development Act (NI) 1969, my Department has a legal responsibility to consider any application for a mineral prospecting licence.  Having met the  necessary legislative requirements, the seven applications were deemed to be valid and where subsequently issued for public consultation.  Having considered all the issues raised by the respondees to the consultation, it was concluded that there were no lawful barriers to prevent my Department granting all seven mineral prospecting licences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the review of the minerals licensing policy and the associated regulatory framework; and (ii) to outline when this will be finalised.
answer_text: The review of mineral licensing policy, which is currently enshrined in the Minerals Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969, is ongoing. The next steps are a Strategic Environmental Assessment and a Call for Evidence. My Department is proceeding with the review as quickly as possible

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16354/22-27, to provide an update on the work undertaken by the Careers Advisory Forum within the last six months.
answer_text: The Careers Advisory Forum has not yet been re-established.  However, stakeholder engagement activity remains a key priority with the partnerships created providing essential support to early careers reform projects. Most recently, a careers portal stakeholder event was held on 12th May 2025, bringing together over 50 representatives from across the careers landscape.  This event followed a wide range of evidence gathering, focus groups and a formal discovery process to validate findings. Officials in my department have worked closely with counterparts in the Department of Education to develop proposals aimed at strengthening the careers ecosystem. This includes setting out mechanisms to enhance system level engagement and collaboration to inform the effective and joined up delivery of careers services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her initial assessment of the affordability of paid carer's leave as detailed in the Introducing Carer's Leave in Northern Ireland briefing paper.
answer_text: It will be for the Executive to decide on the affordability of paid carer’s leave once its budget is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20499/22-27, for an update on the progress of the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12.
answer_text: The first stage of the review of the Minimum Standards, a public consultation on adult-child ratios and the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration countersigned by their GP, was launched on Monday 24 March 2025.Following the close of the consultation on 20 June 2025, all responses will be collated for review by the Department, and a consultation report will be produced. If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these changes will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers. Following the consultation a piece of work will be taken forward with relevant stakeholders to draft a new set of Minimum Standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline whether the Screen Industries Sectoral Action Plan will be updated to incorporate the impacts of Artificial Intelligence on the sector.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence will be a strong theme within the Screen's strategy for 2026-30, currently under development.  The Screen Industries Sectoral Plan will then be updated to reflect the new 2026-30 strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what consideration she has given to providing enhanced research and development support for micro and small businesses.
answer_text: Invest NI Grant for R&D support is available to all company sizes.   micro, small and medium sizes companies are eligible for enhanced support of up to 70% support  for a small sized company and up to 60% for a medium sized company. In September 2024 Invest NI launched a ‘Boosting R&D’ initiative for SMEs which had not received an offer of R&D support from Invest NI in the previous 3 years. Businesses applying through this initiative can receive  a fixed support rate of 70% for up to £100k.  Invest NI also has a range of innovation support available to small to medium enterprises (SMEs). Its Business Innovation Grant (BIG) also offers grants of up to £20,000 to support SMEs in Northern Ireland with innovation projects. Invest NI also provides both advice, guidance and financial support to SMEs for innovation and digitalisation projects, product development, process improvement, product certification, and global quality management systems. Invest NI financial support is also available to enable SMEs to access expertise from universities, colleges, Institutes of Technology and other research bodies throughout the North and the South of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any ongoing work between her Department and local councils to support the development and implementation of productivity strategies, particularly in geographic lower-performing areas.
answer_text: My officials continue to work alongside local councils on the establishment of Local Economic Partnerships as outlined in the Sub-regional Economic Plan.  Supported by a £45m Regional Balance Fund, these will focus on identifying and developing the economic priorities of each area with one aim being to improve productivity. My Department is also responsible for £800 million of City and Growth Deal funding across over 40 projects throughout the North which have been identified and prioritised by councils.  These will contribute to increasing productivity particularly through investment in the innovation centres which will provide world-class spaces to innovate and boost productivity within key sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any work being undertaken by her Department to support students from disadvantaged socio-economic backgrounds in accessing high-quality work placements.
answer_text: My Department is focused on ensuring that everyone in our society has access to skills pathways to access Good Jobs.  We work closely with our delivery partners, stakeholders and other government departments to understand and address the barriers that those from disadvantaged socio-economic backgrounds face – including accessing high-quality work placements.    Work placements for students are taken forward through arrangements between the students place of study the employer. Our higher education providers offer support for students from disadvantaged socio-economic backgrounds as part of Widening Participation activities. Further, there are work placements incorporated into a number of programmes available through my Department which are available to those from disadvantaged socio-economic backgrounds, for example:Skills for Life and Work Programme which focuses primarily on Entry Level and Level 1, for young people aged 16–17 who have left school with low or no qualifications. NI Level 2 Traineeships helps young people move into level 3 education, apprenticeships, or employment and contributes to tackling educational and skills inequality in NI.The Breaking Barriers programme has funded the Open University (OU) in conjunction with local employers to support internships, with the aim of widening participation. The Graduate Boost programme specifically targets those who are neurodiverse. The formal work-based learning experience combined with a post graduate accredited module enables graduate students to further enhance employment opportunities. The Women Returners Programme specifically targets those economically excluded from the workforce, provides a work placement and access to employment opportunities linked to qualification studied.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) metrics; and (ii) measurements, her Department applies to track the productivity of the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: The metrics against which we are tracking the productivity of the North are set out in the 2024/25 DfE business plan (Delivering the Economic Vision - A three year forward look & 2024/25 Action Plan).  The top line measure is ‘Output per hour worked’ with a suite of supporting indicators listed in Annex A of the business plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 22136/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23190/22-27, to outline when there will be more detail on the future changes to the financial support provided to students on pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by his Department.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the work her Department is undertaking to increase export intensity.
answer_text: There is a strong, positive relationship between exporting, innovation and productivity – and I am working with Invest NI, InterTradeIreland and others to ensure we harness our unique dual market access arrangements to grow the export base wider and deeper. We are making good progress in that regard: in the year ending December 2024, the North increased the value of its exports while Scotland, England and Wales experienced decreases. Invest NI is the main provider of export support in the North. Since the start of this year, it has delivered 12 international trade missions and exhibitions, collectively supporting 87 businesses across multiple sectors such as Food and Drink, Materials Handling, Aerospace, and Cleantech to accelerate export success in Europe, the Americas, and IMEA. Invest NI also provides tailored trade support to help local businesses to develop their footprint overseas and to increase export intensity. Support programmes such as Grow Beyond and Going Dutch have been tailored to the needs of early-stage exporters and SME’s. The current uncertainty around tariffs is unwelcome and creates additional complexity for businesses trading internationally. I am engaging with business representatives to understand the issues at play and shape my asks of the British and Irish Governments as the situation evolves.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work she is undertaking to increase the uptake of lifelong learning.
answer_text: My officials have been working with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning to develop approaches that will embed a culture of lifelong learning. In April, we launched ‘Measuring the Impact of Adult Education in NI: Current Practice and Ways Forward’ (MeasuringtheImpactofAdultEd2025.pdf) . We are currently considering how best to progress the recommendations in partnership with the Alliance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how treatments for perimenopause and menopause will be incorporated into the Women's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: Work to develop a Women’s Health Action Plan is ongoing, which will include the wide range of policy and service development initiatives currently in progress and outline any actions that can be delivered within current resources over the next three years to improve health services and deliver better outcomes for women.  I launched a survey in November 2024 with the aim of hearing directly from women about their health priorities and barriers to accessing services.  Early findings, including any menopause feedback from this survey, are due at the end of May 2025 and will be considered as work develops on the Action Plan.My Department also recently hosted a number of online engagement events with women's representative groups and healthcare professionals, input received will be reviewed over the coming weeks to help inform the priorities in the action plan.In 2023, my Department commissioned the Getting it Right First Time Team (GIRFT) to undertake an independent, external rapid review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland.  The aim of the GIRFT review was to identify areas where improvements could be made in the extensive waiting lists for gynaecology services, including those for menopause, and to ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.The report, which was published in January 2024, set out recommendations designed to maximise capacity, reduce waiting times, improve patient care and outcomes. A Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and PHA representation, has been established to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level.  Although work is at an early stage of development and will require additional funding, this will include mapping clinical pathways from presentation at GP services into secondary and tertiary care, which will benefit both patients and clinicians.  This work includes a specific task and finish group to consider menopause care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what support is available to patients who are seeking to access hormone replacement therapy prescriptions; and (ii) any work that is ongoing to educate GPs around treatments for perimenopause and menopause.
answer_text: GPs are often the first point of contact for patients seeking to access Hormone Replacement Therapy.  Following a consultation GPs are best placed to advise on resources. This may include online resources that will educate and inform patients on perimenopause or menopause and any associated treatments recommended including hormone replacement therapy tailored to their individual circumstances.  As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including management of perimenopause and menopause.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient. These initiatives cover perimenopause, menopause and HRT prescribing. GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2024/25 is £50.5k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners. Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.  GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.The Northern Ireland formulary maintained by the Regional Pharmacy and Medicines Management Team with the support of Panels for Prescribing Advice (PPA) provides evidence-based guidance on Hormone replacement therapy which is available to GPs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given the increasing importance of artificial intelligence in the workplace and the need to improve its adoption, to detail any consideration he has given to the development of training programmes to upskill and reskill officials in (i) the Northern Ireland Civil Service; and (ii) other statutory bodies and agencies.
answer_text: We have tasked the Chief Scientific and Technology Adviser (CSTA) with chairing a Task-and-Finish Group to scope out the role of an Office of AI and Digital. This stakeholder group includes representatives from government departments, industry and academia. The contributions thus far have been invaluable in identifying the challenges and opportunities associated with adoption of AI across NI’s public sector.  The CSTA Office will progress the task of drafting the key elements for the Office of AI and Digital, and share this with the stakeholder group for consultation and input.The AI skills required to upskill and reskill officials will be considered as part of the key elements for the Office of AI and Digital. This structured and delivery-focused approach will ensure that the Office of AI and Digital is founded on a strong evidence base, is aligned to Executive priorities on efficiency and reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22513/22-27, when the improvement works at Cairnshill park and ride facility will be completed.
answer_text: I am pleased to report that the installation of bollards has now been completed. Height restriction barriers are currently being manufactured off-site.  Depending on contractor availability these are expected to be erected within the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration she has given to the need for upskilling and reskilling in the economy due to the increasing prevalence and use of Artificial Intelligence in the workplace.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of upskilling and reskilling in order to harness the benefits of AI in the workplace. My department, along with InvestNI are funding (£16.3m) the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre. This is a five-year programme delivered by Ulster University and Queen’s University Belfast. During 2024/2025 the Skill Up programme approved over 100 training places related to AI.  These were fully funded courses from level 2 to level 7 delivered through further and higher education providers. Higher-Level Apprenticeship (HLA) programmes include IT-related qualifications from level 5 through to Level 7, which contain modules on AI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how Global Citizenship Education is being incorporated and considered as part of the ongoing review of the Northern Ireland curriculum.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan will consider all areas of learning across the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the research on paid carer’s leave will be published.
answer_text: I have published the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) briefing paper on carer’s leave. It can be accessed via this link: Introducing Carer’s Leave in Northern Ireland briefing paper.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the rationale for reducing the recognition threshold for trade unions from 21 to 10 employees; and (ii) any engagement she has had with trade unions and businesses on this issue.
answer_text: I am committed to increasing the number of employees who have access to the support and guidance that a trade union can offer. To that end I have decided to reduce the statutory recognition threshold from 21 to 10, as proposed in the consultation document issued in July 2024. I, my predecessor and departmental officials have engaged with trade unions, leading business representatives and other stakeholders on numerous occasions in relation to the totality of the good jobs agenda, including this change. This engagement has included introductory meetings with relevant officials, Ministerial led engagement forum meetings and three workshop style meetings prior to consultation launch. It has also included official attendance at four Labour Relations Agency Engagement Forum meetings comprised of union and business representatives. My predecessor met with trade unions and business representatives during the consultation period, and numerous trade unions and businesses were invited to attend an academic event run by my Department prior to the consultation closing. My officials gave presentations, heard views and answered questions about the proposals at several meetings and conferences organised by business organisations and attended by hundreds of participants. I also took account of the 192 formal responses to the consultation in making my decision on this issue. My officials and I will continue to liaise with key stakeholders as the planned Bill passes through the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when she intends to bring forward the (i) costings; and (ii) investments associated with delivering the measures intended to be included in the forthcoming Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: My Department carried out individual Regulatory Impact Assessments on the proposed updates to our employment law framework, including a Small and Micro Business Impact Assessment. These impact assessments, which are available on the Department’s website and formed part of my Department’s consultation, will be assessed and reviewed as we continue to develop further our policies and legislation. Additional measures (such as extended paternity leave or paid carer’s leave) could have legislative, administrative and financial implications which would require careful consideration by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill way forward, how her Department is seeking to address the concerns of small and micro businesses in relation to the implementation of any changes.
answer_text: In addition to individual Regulatory Impact Assessments on the proposed updates to our employment law framework, my Department carried out a Small and Micro Business Impact Assessment. These impact assessments, which formed part of my Department’s consultation, will be assessed and reviewed as we continue to develop further our policies and legislation. While workers are generally entitled to the same framework of employment rights regardless of employer size, my proposals contain some mitigations for smaller businesses. For example, businesses with less than £45,000 in employer national insurance contributions are allowed to claim 108.5% of any statutory paid leave entitlement such as neo-natal care leave and pay, rather than the rate of 92% which applies to larger employers. This is in recognition of the additional administrative overhead for businesses of that size. While my proposals aim to expand the number of workers who can be represented by a formally recognised trade union, I have set the statutory recognition threshold at ten, which excludes micro businesses. In addition, my Department, in conjunction with the Labour Relations Agency, will consider what training and support could be provided to businesses during this transition period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress that has been made on improving access to NHS dentistry for children in South Belfast.
answer_text: General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and, unlike General Medical Practitioners, are under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they will treat under Health Service arrangements.  Access to Health Service dentistry is currently very difficult, particularly for unregistered patients, and this is likely to be the case for some time.  In regards to the steps taken to improve access to dental care there was a £9.2m investment in dental access initiatives for the 2024/25 financial year.  This investment included £1m to fund an Enhanced Child Examination Scheme (ECE), £4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments, and £3.9m to fund a Dental Access Scheme for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients.  Over 37,000 children aged 10 and under have been registered under the ECE since it came into effect in June 2024 with 2,214 patients in the South Belfast constituency. A total of 36 practices are currently participating in the Dental Access Scheme with over 500 patients currently accessing care each week.  There are ten participating practices in the Belfast Trust area and two in the South Belfast constituency.In addition, a 6% pay uplift for dentists was back-dated to April 2023 and is in line with the recommendation from the Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body.  The uplift came with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses, with a 3% recurrent award in future years and represents an investment of £7m for 2023/24 and £5.3m for future years. Investments for the 2025/26 financial year will be confirmed in the near future though a 6% pay uplift has already been approved which will be back-dated to April 2024 in line with the recommendation from the Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body.  The uplift will come with a 2.8% increase to non-staff expenses and represents an investment of £6.45m.These investments aim to bolster support for dental practices and protect public access to health service dental care across Northern Ireland including in your constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to upgrade the Four Winds roundabout, in light of the anticipated increased volume of traffic related to new housing developments in the area.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to upgrade the Four Winds Roundabout, however, planning approvals for two major residential developments in the area require the developers to undertake improvement works at Four Winds roundabout before occupation of 90 residential units across the two development sites.
