I would encourage anyone who believes that a person may be providing unregistered childcare to contact their local Health and Social Care Trust, who will investigate further. In accordance with Part XI, Article 132 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order, anyone found to be providing unregistered childminding by the Trust is served with a one year enforcement notice. Should they be found to be providing unregistered childminding again during that period, they will be guilty of an offence and liable to a fine on conviction of up to level 5 on the standard scale.My department continues to promote the benefits of using registered childcare and raise awareness of the risks associated with unregistered childcare, through the www.familysupportni.gov.uk website.A further element to addressing this is the delivery of affordable and accessible childcare which is a commitment of the Northern Ireland Executive and effective legislative and standards frameworks will be a key to delivering on that.  As such, I have made a commitment to undertake a full review of the Minimum Standards.  The first stage of the review of the Minimum Standards, a public consultation on adult-child ratios and the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration countersigned by their GP, was launched on Monday 24 March 2025.Following the close of the consultation on 20 June 2025, all responses will be collated for review by the Department, and a consultation report will be produced. If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these changes will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers. The full review of the Minimum Standards is expected to be completed in 2026.In addition, the developing Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will also assist in tackling the issue of unregistered childminders, by promoting the value of registered childminding, as well as making childcare more affordable and accessible for parents who need it. The Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) aims to reduce the childcare costs of eligible working parents by 15 per cent for all qualifying children.  It is delivered through registered childcare providers, including daycares, playgroups, childminders, and Approved Home Childcarers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to his announcement on an increase to maximum student maintenance loan entitlement, to detail when the broader review of student support will be completed.
answer_text: My predecessor agreed in principle to proceed with a Review of Higher Education Funding.  Officials are currently engaging with the sector as regards to the potential scope and approach to the Review and will brief me in coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to his announcement on the increase to maximum student maintenance loan entitlement for full-time students, whether he will introduce similar maintenance support for part-time students.
answer_text: Currently, eligible part time students can apply for a means-tested tuition fee grant of up to £1,230 and course grant of up to £265. Students can also apply for a non-means-tested fee loan of up to £3,532.50.My predecessor agreed to proceed with a review of Higher Education Funding and I will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available to higher education students, including those studying part time, going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the current status of the proposed Belfast Stories project as part of the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: Currently, Belfast City Council is developing concept designs and a draft outline business case on the detail of the proposal for Belfast Stories.A second phase of public consultation for the project commenced on the 19th of November 2024 and will remain open until the 23rd of February 2025. In parallel, Tourism NI has initiated market testing for the project in six key visitor markets and has facilitated workshops with relevant Executive Departments with an interest in the delivery and outcomes of the proposition. It is anticipated that the Outline Business Case (OBC) will be submitted for consideration in Spring 2025. If funding is approved, the project aims to open to the public in 2030. 	My officials will continue to work closely with Tourism Northern Ireland, Belfast Region City Deal and Belfast City Council to develop the OBC for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans he has to incentivise part-time learning as a means of improving the inclusiveness of higher education and to attract a more diverse range of students, including those who (i) are employed; (ii) are facing financial hardship; and (iii) have caring responsibilities.
answer_text: Currently, eligible part-time students can apply for a means-tested tuition fee grant of up to £1,230 and course grant of up to £265. They can also apply for a non-means-tested fee loan of up to £3,562.50.My predecessor agreed to proceed with a review of Higher Education Funding and I will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available to higher education students going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any provisions that exist within the Encompass system to (i) incorporate additional referral mechanisms, if the need should arise; and (ii) connect with data held by other public bodies to give a broader picture of the health and well-being of patients.
answer_text: The encompass platform is designed to support multiple referral mechanisms and can accommodate additional referral pathways if a need arises. The encompass system will hold information collected during episodes of care in the Acute, Community and Mental Health sectors. As with other health related information sources, access to both patient identifiable information and anonymised health information is strictly governed. Currently, encompass data is not connected to other public body data and any requests to do so will be subject to strict information governance considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) (i) ceased to operate; and (ii) contracted out, the fast-track contact service for GPs to access DVA medical advisors.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not provide and has not previously provided a service which permits GPs direct access to DVA medical advisors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any mechanisms in place for GPs to contact Driver and Vehicle Agency medical advisors.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not provide a service which permits GPs direct access to DVA medical advisors. DVA may request medical information from various medical professionals to support the assessment of a driver or driving licence applicant’s fitness to drive and provide this information to their medical advisors as appropriate. The requests for and the provision of this information is done by post. Where there is a concern around an individual’s fitness to drive, anyone, including medical professionals, can report such concerns to the DVA. The DVA will investigate these reports and if required refer these to their medical advisors for assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15836/22-27, to detail why the system used by the Driver and Vehicle Agency is not able to easily identify the total number of cases where doctors or other medical professionals have disclosed information about a patient’s lack of fitness to drive.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland driver licensing system is designed primarily to record information relating to the identity and driving entitlements of persons who make applications for Northern Ireland driving licences. This information is essential in the issue or withdrawal of a licence.  The system is also required by law to record details of any endorsements or disqualifications.  The system was designed to enable the easy extraction and reporting of key business information and data to support the effective operation of the business. The ability to readily access other information which does not fall into this criterion, such as that highlighted in the question, is not an essential part of the driver licensing system and was not included in the design specification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the delivery of the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route.
answer_text: I had planned to make a statement on the Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 (BRT2) project on 28th January 2025, however this was postponed due to the severity of Storm Eowyn and its consequential impact on citizens in the North.A revised date for announcement will be agreed in the near future, and I would not wish to pre-empt the contents of this statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 19617/22-27, for an updated timeline for the publication of the 2024-2027 Apprenticeship Action Plan.
answer_text: There are a significant range of actions currently underway in the area of apprenticeships. This includes an Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund, Apprenticeship Week and the development of new apprenticeship and higher-level apprenticeship programmes for September 2025. In parallel, officials are developing an Apprenticeship Action Plan that will include these actions and others that will be delivered over the next two years.  The Apprenticeship Action plan is in the final stages of development and will be published in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department will (i) make all Ordnance Survey Northern Ireland (OSNI) admin boundary maps publicly and freely available as raster images, including (a) post-1973 maps held by OSNI; and (b) 1921-1973 maps held by PRONI; and (ii) publish these boundaries in GeoJSON/Shapefile format.
answer_text: (i) 	a. 	The post-1973 administrative boundaries referred to (ie, Local Government District boundaries, Wards Boundaries and Parliamentary Constituencies boundaries) and held by OSNI, are already available under Open Government License (OGL) via the Open Data NI portal, Spatial NI and/or through direct contact with OSNI.		b. 	The 1921-1973 maps held by PRONI are not within OSNI’s remit.		(ii) 	The digitised administrative boundary datasets OSNI hold are already available in shapefile format from 1993 onwards under OGL.  All other administrative boundary datasets within OSNI’s remit are only available as scanned images of historic paper maps (and also available under OGL) and therefore can only be disseminated using the appropriate raster formats (ie, not shapefile).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they intend to bring the Refugee Integration Strategy to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: We are currently considering a draft Refugee Integration Strategy and associated Delivery Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20405/22-27, to detail when he expects the (i) trial period to end at Antrim Area Hospital; and (ii) formal evaluation to be concluded.
answer_text: Following intensive preparation and shadow testing, the trial of an AI tool to improve the detection of fractures went live on 20 January 2025 and its scope includes the Emergency Departments at the Antrim and Causeway Hospitals as well as Minor Injury Units across the Northern Trust area.   The trial period will run until 20 March 2025.Formal audit and evaluation of the trial will commence at the mid-point of the trial period and will run simultaneously to conclude on 20 March 2025 in line with the trial end period.  This is aimed at ensuring timely decision making and seamless continuation and roll out across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18400/22-27, for an update on work to review the financial support available for students on pre-registration Allied Health Professional degree courses commissioned by his Department.
answer_text: Work is ongoing on this issue.  My officials are currently developing possible options for my consideration and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20042/22-27, whether he will provide more details of the Education Authority's response to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill consultation.
answer_text: I will not be providing any more details of the Education Authority’s response to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill consultation at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work to progress the development of a new model of delivery for further education colleges.
answer_text: My Department will be taking forward a co-designed programme of work in conjunction with Further Education Colleges over the next 24 months to review the Further Education delivery model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the projected costs that the implementation of the Electronic Travel Authorisation Scheme will have on the tourism sector over the next five years.
answer_text: The full impact of the introduction of the Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme will not be known for some time. Tourism has grown substantially in recent years and there is considerable potential for further growth. The introduction of the ETA Scheme, however, threatens that progress.  That is why, on behalf of the industry, I continue to lobby the British Government for a solution.With 67% of our overseas holiday makers arriving via the South, seamless all-island travel is key to the success of our tourism sector.  The ETA introduces a financial and bureaucratic barrier for tourists to travel into the north. This will undoubtedly put off some people from visiting here, which will in turn impact on expenditure by international visitors in the local economy. I was disappointed at the Home Office decision earlier this month to increase the cost of the ETA from £10 to £16. This significant 60% price increase will only serve to heighten the financial barrier for tourists wanting to visit the north of Ireland and makes it even more important that a pragmatic solution must be found to protect out tourism sector. I am advised that the introduction of the ETA will result in the North of Ireland being excluded from the itinerary of many all-island tour operators. £210m was contributed to the local economy by international visitors (who will now require an ETA) in 2023. It is this expenditure that could be impacted. Additionally, £7.5m of associated expenditure from international visitors (on day trips from the South) may also be threatened.  A recent Sentiment Tracker Survey undertaken by Tourism Ireland indicated that 1 in 5 people (coming from North America) and 1 in 4 (coming from Europe) are likely not to travel due to an ETA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when the Open Data Strategy for Northern Ireland 2020–2023 will be updated.
answer_text: A review of the Open Data Strategy (2020–2023) is underway and is scheduled to complete by the end of March 2025. The findings from this review, will help inform a new strategy which will be developed and published in the 2025-26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how Artificial Intelligence is being incorporated into the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The reform and transformation of public services is a key element of our proposed Programme for Government.  Critical to that, will be the need to identify opportunities for leveraging AI to enhance decision-making and improve efficiency. We are establishing an AI Unit, and our Chief Scientific and Technology Advisor is already working with a range of key stakeholders to develop a Regional Strategy for Science and Technology to drive this work forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the role of a Chief Digital Information Officer is expected to be established; and (ii) the primary responsibilities of the role.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work he is undertaking to grow the software skills pipeline.
answer_text: In June 2024, I launched my Department’s Software and Cyber Sectoral Plan, which includes a number of actions focused on improving skills within the sector. Following this in September 2024, I then launched my Digital Skills Action plan which was developed in collaboration with industry and educational experts.Officials are working with our delivery partners across industry and academia to grow the skills pipeline for the software sector, to ensure it has the people with the skills it requires.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how his proposed Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill (i) aligns; and (ii) differs from similar legislation being brought forward by the UK Government.
answer_text: Final policy decisions have not yet been made on the content of my Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.  Once I have finalised my policy decisions, the policy content of the Bill will be subject to Executive agreement.  At that point, any similarities and differences between my Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill and the legislative position in Britain can be clarified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) roles; and (ii) responsibilities of the Head of the Policy Profession in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: ANSWERThe roles and responsibilities of Heads of Professions in the Civil Service are currently under review, but the role of the Head of the Policy Profession currently includes supporting the development of those working in policy teams, ensuring effective communications about the policy function, setting standards for professional skills, and embedding professional standards in HR processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the definition of inclusive education used within his Department.
answer_text: As a member of the European Agency for Special Education and Inclusion since 1996, the department has subscribed to an agreed definition of inclusive education systems as those within which ‘all learners of any age are provided with meaningful, high-quality educational opportunities in their local community, alongside their friends and peers.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report stating that there should be a comprehensive review of how NI Water is funded, to outline the basis on which he considers NI Water’s funding model to be fit for purpose.
To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20136/22-27, to detail the (i) terms of reference; and (ii) membership of the Civil Service AI Working Group.
answer_text: (i) The AI Working Group referenced in AQW 20136/22-27 is an informal group of departmental representatives, established to share early learnings and best practice in terms of harnessing opportunities for AI to be potentially used across the Civil Service. There is no official Terms of Reference for the group who have no decision making powers. (ii) The membership of the AI Working Group is made up of ICT Level 8 staff from across departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his work to improve the quality of care in care homes.
answer_text: When a person moves to a care home it remains their home in their community. A key focus is therefore to ensure a secure environment where the quality of care provided to all residents is safe and effective.The Social Care Collaborative Forum is working to implement proposals stemming from the public consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care in Northern Ireland. One of the proposals is Enhancing Care in Care Homes: which focuses on access to safe, high quality and person-centred care and implementing the findings of the CPEA and COPNI reports and the, Enhanced Clinical Care Framework recommendations.I accept that the Care Home Standards need a comprehensive review to ensure they are fully reflective of best practice, learning and guidance and are relevant to the current environment in which the care sector operate. A Working Group, has been established to undertake a review of the Standards.   My Department has commissioned a five-year regional roll out of the My Home Life (MHL) Programme. MHL is an international initiative that promotes quality of life and delivers positive change in care homes for older people. The Programme was specifically designed to meet the unique needs of care home managers by supporting them to improve quality of life for residents, relatives and staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7473/22-27, when the public consultation will take place.
answer_text: I can advise that Onslow Parade and Ravenhill Park, Belfast have been assessed for the introduction of traffic calming measures.  Following the assessment, preparation of a feasibility design is now underway. Unfortunately, this has been delayed due to my limited staff resources being redirected to urgent and priority work, such as the Belfast Grand Central Station and associated junction improvements, service works, legislation, Sydenham Bypass, McKinstry Road/ Derriaghy Road/ The Cutts improvement scheme, Environment Improvement Schemes such as Hillsborough Public Realm, Blackstaff Square/ 5 C’s, major utility works by Phoenix Gas , NI Water and NIE together with our traffic management, collision remedial, pedestrian schemes and the processing of 6/7000 lane and road closures per year. It is now anticipated that a formal consultation could take place mid-2025. Further progress on this matter will be subject to funding and satisfactory completion of the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures being taken to address transport challenges for rural schools accessing Dream Space at W5 LIFE.
answer_text: I fully recognise the significant educational value that learning experiences such as Dream Space bring to children and young people, offering excellent opportunities for schools to enhance their curriculum delivery. It is regrettable that, given the challenging budget circumstances, additional earmarked funding for transport to support school visits to such venues cannot be provided. Nevertheless, I would like to highlight the many free online learning opportunities available to schools through Dream Space, including a wide range of professional learning programmes and resources to help teachers upskill in STEM and Digital Technology, as well as many online offerings for pupils. These resources can be used as teacher-led activities in the classroom or for independent learning. I strongly encourage all schools to make full use of these online facilities to enhance teaching and learning experiences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of funding received to support the work of her Department's Innovation Lab in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Innovation Lab was mainly funded by the Department of Finance, which was supplemented by additional income generated from work for other departments. The budget and income received for the Innovation Lab for the period 2020/21 to 2023/24 is detailed below. Please note information for 2019/20 is not available as the budget for the Innovation Lab was not separated out from other Departmental functions at this time.Innovation Lab2020/21£0002021/22£0002022/23£0002023/24£000Final Outturn572639800849Income-105-105-124-94Total Funding467534676755Following an organisational review, the Innovation Lab merged with Business Consultancy Services (BCS) in April 2024 to form Innovation and Consultancy Services (ICS). Therefore, there is no separately identifiable 2024/25 funding allocated for the Innovation Lab.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the current work being undertaken by her Department's Innovation Lab.
answer_text: Following an organisational review, the Innovation Lab merged with Business Consultancy Services (BCS) in April 2024 to form Innovation and Consultancy Services (ICS). The services offered by ICS now include those previously available within the Innovation Lab and these are detailed in the ICS 2024/25 Service Brochure which is available at: Innovation and Consultancy Services (ICS) | Department of Finance.ICS supports the public sector to deliver better outcomes for citizens through the provision of independent advice and insights in relation to setting direction; evaluation & design; and strengthening governance. To date in 2024/25, ICS has completed 18 projects for clients in these areas, with a further 44 projects currently underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16137/22-27, to provide an update on this research.
answer_text: The Department of Finance recently announced plans to commission a study into the cost of doing business. There was potential for overlap between this study and our planned research into the night-time economy. It is important to avoid duplication and ensure value for money. My officials, together with Tourism NI and the three Business Improvement Districts in Belfast, have therefore refined the specification of the research and expect work to commence in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 1138/22-27, for an update on the progress of the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12.
answer_text: I will be seeking views on adult: child ratios through a public consultation exercise in the coming weeks. Work will also be undertaken in partnership with a stakeholder reference group to update the wider Minimum Standards document, with a view to consulting on and implementing a new set of Standards by December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of litigants in person that have (i) won; and (ii) lost their case in employment tribunals for people with disabilities in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Office of the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal are unable to provide this information as they do not collect personal information regarding a person’s disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of complaints made by litigants in person in relation to employment tribunals for people with disabilities, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department has not received any formal complaints made by litigants in person in relation to employment tribunals for people with disabilities, in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any regulatory frameworks being developed to ensure the responsible use of artificial intelligence by businesses.
answer_text: The regulatory frameworks to govern artificial intelligence are the responsibility of the British government and potentially the EU.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration he has given to updating regulations to allow taxi drivers to work with multiple operators, similar to the model in parts of England, to increase driver income and improve service availability.
answer_text: Under section 3(7) of the Taxis Act (NI) 2008 it is not possible for a taxi driver to be affiliated to more than one licensed operator, though it is possible to switch driver affiliation between operators at a cost of £5.I currently have no plans to update this section of the Taxis Act (NI) 2008.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support available to help businesses prepare for and harness artificial intelligence technology.
answer_text: My Department, in collaboration with Invest NI, has dedicated teams focused on maximizing the potential of AI and digital technologies. Invest NI has funding available to support adopting digital technologies through initiatives such as the Agri-Food Investment Initiative, along with grants for R&D, Technical Development Incentive and help to access alternative funding through Innovate UK. The City and Growth Deals investments and initiatives also offer access to funding, such as the Digital Transformation Flexible Fund (DTFF) which is funded through the Executive’s Complementary Fund. The AI Collaboration Centre (AICC) provides assistance to SMEs in AI adoption, including AI training programmes. In partnership with my Department, the AICC is delivering 390 postgraduate scholarships in AI-related courses offered by Ulster University and Queen's University.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to the use of artificial intelligence to address waiting lists.
answer_text: My Department is working with the Chief Digital Information Officer to ascertain if AI technologies can be utilised to help reduce current waiting times. Progress has been made in this area, including a trial which is underway in Antrim Area Hospital with an AI application to improve the detection of fractures in the Emergency Department.  Following formal evaluation of this trial period, a decision will be made as to its full implementation into other areas.  Other areas being explored include the increased use of AI tools on scanners to reduce sequence time, hence reducing scanning time and increasing throughput. There are future plans to consider testing AI algorithms to assist with referral/ decision making, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage of reporting, and results acknowledgement/alerts.My Department is also working with Trusts and the Digital Health and Care Northern Ireland (DHCNI) team to address patient waiting list duplications through the development of a new electronic application which helps identify duplicate referrals within the same Trust and across Trusts. All these initiatives had reduced the administrative input and have helped improve productivity and efficiency and ultimately helps reduce waiting times.Another area where my Department utilises smart technology is in its use for waiting list validation. The roll-out of text reminders and the use of QR codes, which are placed on patient letters, are scanned and patients are directed to a survey which they are prompted to complete. The results of the survey help the relevant clinician determine whether the patient needs to remain on the waiting list.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current length of the waiting list for the treatment of dystonia.
answer_text: Treatment of dystonia is primarily managed by the Regional Neurology Service at the Belfast HSC Trust.Dystonia is not a single diagnosis but relates to a number of genetic and acquired disorders resulting in the movement disorder called dystonia.  Depending on the suspected cause, patients may be graded as routine or urgent.  Any suspicion of a potential underlying cancer diagnosis would result in a 2 week wait.  In general terms movement disorders such as dystonia would fall within the urgent waiting time of 8 months for the regional movement disorder clinic.  Most patients would receive treatment at the first appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any initiatives being developed to address skills gaps around the use of Artificial Intelligence in the public sector.
answer_text: ANSWERThe NICS has a growing interest in exploring the effective and appropriate use of artificial intelligence and emerging technologies, which may present opportunities for enhancing public service delivery.  I want to ensure the NICS is a digitally enabled workforce. Within my department, we are already exploring use of AI in our service delivery, and NICSHR’s Learning & Development team is beginning to reference AI within its own relevant learning interventions. As the understanding on the effective use of AI in the NICS develops, so too will initiatives to develop skills in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how Artificial Intelligence is being integrated into (i) teaching; (ii) learning; and (iii) administration in the education system to improve outcomes.
answer_text: In respect of integrating AI into teaching, the Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in Northern Ireland deliver Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes aligned with the Northern Ireland Curriculum, preparing future teachers for effective curriculum delivery.  All HEIs introduce students to the topic of AI during ITE under ‘Digital Skills’ within the curriculum.  ITE course coverage includes exploration of the impact and advancements in AI, as well as the opportunities and the challenges it brings. The Education Authority is working alongside a cohort of 94 teachers and school leaders to pilot AI (Microsoft Copilot).  The pilot, which is being independently assessed by Ulster University, will ascertain the benefits and disadvantages, whilst ensuring appropriate safety measures are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any pilot programmes planned to demonstrate the potential of generative Artificial Intelligence in the public sector.
answer_text: The adoption and use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) within the Department of Finance offers many opportunities to examine how processes and procedures could be improved to drive efficiencies and effectiveness across the Department. To investigate this new technology, while being cognisant of the risks, my Department is running a few small-scale Proof of Value (PoV) projects to examine the use of AI in a controlled and measured way to understand how this technology can deliver value in the public sector.One of these PoVs is to investigate how a chatbot can reduce complexity, save time, and make interactions with a government service simpler and faster. The technology responds to user questions using existing published information. Another PoV involves the use of an AI coding assistant that helps our developers become more efficient in writing code, quickly identifying and rectifying errors, increasing efficiency and productivity. I am also aware that some other departments are embarking on similar projects to assess the value of harnessing the potential of generative AI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he intends to bring a paper on Artificial Intelligence to the Executive.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering the AI landscape in the North and will bring forward policy recommendations that focus on the impact on the workforce and business support to encourage AI as a tool for growth.	The impact of AI will be felt across society and, other departments will wish to consider their own remits and how AI can contribute to the improved delivery of public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how the Education Authority ensures children with severe allergies have access to support in school.
To ask the Minister for the Economy when he intends to publish an updated Innovation Strategy.
answer_text: I have outlined, in my economic vision, my four economic priorities of creating good jobs, increasing productivity, promoting regional balance and decarbonisation. Innovation is a key driver across all these priorities, playing a particular role in driving productivity across our economy. While there are currently no plans to publish an updated Innovation Strategy, my department remains committed to driving innovation through strong collaboration and co-design with stakeholders across our innovation ecosystem. Activities are focused on three priorities of accelerating Research and Development (R&D), delivering more and better Innovation Driven Enterprises (IDEs) and supporting innovation across the wider business base.  Supporting innovative growth will form part of a wider programme of work, taken forward by my department, to deliver my economic vision.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on what their Department is doing to support victims and survivors of historical clerical abuse.
answer_text: We recognise how important the issue of clerical child abuse is, and we are aware of the devastating impact it has had on many people. Work is progressing on the three separate pieces of research that we have commissioned: one of which will be recording experiences and expectations of victims and survivors. This will include the views of victims and survivors on the support services available and will highlight any deficits in the current service provision.Currently counselling support is provided at all Interdepartmental Working Group Historical Clerical Child Abuse Reference Group meetings.  Additionally, Listening Ear support is available from qualified counsellors to the victims and survivors taking part in the research.We look forward to the conclusion of the research projects in Spring this year, and subsequently seeing the final project reports and recommendations from the Interdepartmental Working Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) membership; (ii) forward work plan; and (iii) meetings that have taken place during the last 12 months of the Equine Assisted Therapy and Learning Stakeholders Group.
answer_text: The Equine Assisted Therapy and Learning stakeholders group consists of representatives from DOH (Office of Social Services), DAERA, DE, DOJ, PHA the Health and Social Care Trusts, One Equine Trust, RDA, Ulster University, and Queens University (PhD research student).The workplan to-date has focussed on supporting research and awareness raising activities and the development of pilot programmes. A workplan for 2025/26 is under development with the Group. Stakeholder group meetings are held quarterly. In the last 12 months meetings were held on the respective dates: 22 January 2024, 25 March 2024 and 1 October 2024. A meeting is scheduled for 14 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19716/22-27, to provide details of the evaluations undertaken following the pilot programmes.
answer_text: The OSS is completing a scoping exercise to review evaluations of Equine Assisted Therapy/Learning programmes in each of the Health & Social Care Trusts. The findings from the evaluation report received from the Belfast Trust based on a pilot programme to support children in stable foster care who have experienced trauma, loneliness and isolation, has been very positive and highlights that the children have benefitted from this intervention. The Western Trusts ‘early help’ pilot programme for families who have been subject to domestic violence is underway.  Early indications are that the children and parents are greatly benefitting from this type of intervention.  A fuller evaluation is planned on completion of the programme which will help inform the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any measures being implemented to ensure the ethical use of artificial intelligence in public policy development.
answer_text: The Head of the Policy Profession in the Civil Service has commissioned work to  consider how the policy function in Departments can use generative artificial intelligence effectively and appropriately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any strategies in place to increase Ministerial engagement with (i) digital technology; and (ii) artificial intelligence.
answer_text: I have overseen a number of strategic initiatives by my Department and Invest NI to increase engagement with digital technologies and Artificial Intelligence (AI) across the North.These include an investment of £16.3m in the AI Collaboration Centre and the £8m Digital Transformation Flexible Fund. These investments will drive engagement with digital technology and AI and help local businesses to invest in digital infrastructure.  I recently engaged with the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology prior to the publication of the British Government’s AI Opportunities Action Plan. I am keen to maximise the opportunities for the North as this plan is implemented.A range of planned significant investments will further boost engagement with digital technologies and AI, such as the £15m SMART/Digital City programme in Derry or the £35m Digital Twin Centre in Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps being taken to improve data sharing between government departments.
answer_text: Data and evidence are critical to good policy-making. Appropriately governed data sharing between government departments can enable cross-cutting analysis to identify more effective, more efficient ways of delivering services. With that in mind I recently approved the NISRA 2025-2029 Corporate Plan. This Plan emphasises the importance of sharing data assets across Departments, to provide cross-cutting analysis to support joined up policy making and operational delivery. NISRA will formally launch their Corporate Plan in February and will actively promote and support the delivery of this important issue. NISRA are partners in the Administrative Data Research UK (ADR UK) which aims to facilitate the linkage of administrative data held by government and public bodies and make it available to approved researchers in a secure way. The NI partnership (ADR NI) includes NISRA, Ulster University and Queen’s University Belfast, and has successfully collaborated with departments to create several cross-departmental data linkages in the areas of secondary educational attainment; business and the economy; income and benefits; post custody mortality and farming life. Detailed information from the successful Census 2021 programme is also now available for research use at the secure facilities in Colby House. The recently launched longitudinal Educational Outcomes Linkage (EOL) which focuses on secondary education, is the first stage to producing a NI Longitudinal Educational Outcomes dataset which will bring together data from the Department of Education, the Department for the Economy, as well as DWP and HMRC to assist in the understanding of educational pathways to employment. Securing new data assets to facilitate research is an ongoing priority for the partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps being taken to enhance the understanding of artificial intelligence in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: The adoption and use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) offers many opportunities to examine how processes and procedures could be improved to drive efficiencies and effectiveness across the Civil Service and the wider public sector. A number of departments are experimenting with this new technology while being cognisant of the risks. My department is running a few small-scale Proof of Value (PoV) projects to examine the use of AI in a controlled and measured way to understand how this technology can deliver value in the public sector.One of these PoVs is to investigate how a chatbot can reduce complexity, save time, and make interactions with a government service simpler and faster. The technology responds to user questions using existing published information. Another PoV involves the use of an AI coding assistant that helps our developers become more efficient in writing code, quickly identifying and rectifying errors, increasing efficiency and productivity.However, these opportunities must be considered in the light of the challenges such as the availability and suitability of data, data protection, data privacy and cyber security alongside the wider issues with AI such as usage monitoring, bias, incorrect information, data poisoning, incorrect predictions, and hallucinations.The Civil Service has established an AI working group made up of digital staff from across departments to share learnings from current initiatives and disseminate guidance and updates from other jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of short respite breaks that have been availed by families of children with complex needs since his announcement of investment in services in October 2024.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 13929/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 13930/22-27, for an update on the review of the paediatric autism spectrum disorder pathway.
answer_text: My officials met with all Trusts individually to map current paediatric children’s ASD and ADHD referral, assessment and intervention pathways. Following this mapping exercise, Department officials are working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Child Health to develop a regionally consistent and defined neurodevelopmental pathway that complies with NICE guidelines. This pathway will take into consideration, best practice, emerging evidence and the recognition that workforce planning is critical to meeting the needs of children and young people in a timely manner. A consistent pathway will inform future investment across all HSC Trusts to provide equitable and effective pathways, and reduce waiting times. This work is being overseen by the Joint Children’s Planning and Performance Team chaired by two Deputy Directors, one from my Department and one from the PHA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 15112/22-27, for an update on his assessment of the implications of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill on (i) teachers; (ii) supply teachers; and (iii) other temporary staff within the education system.
answer_text: The Education Authority has provided comprehensive feedback as part of the public consultation on the Good Jobs Employment Bill, including the reliance of the education sector on the requirement for short term cover at short notice for a range of posts including substitute teachers.To date, there has been no feedback provided on the public consultation process, nor has a draft Bill been published. When a draft Bill is published and details are available on any proposed changes, it is anticipated that an education working group will be established to assess the impact of the proposals on all services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the current budgetary position of the Arts Council of Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering working with Belfast City Council and the PSNI to expand the Park with us in Mind scheme to other areas.
answer_text: My Department is collaborating with Belfast City Council and the PSNI on the ‘Park with us in Mind’ campaign and will consider any request from Belfast City Council or PSNI seeking parking enforcement support in the expansion of the scheme as far as resources will permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact of the Park with us in Mind scheme.
answer_text: My Department is collaborating with Belfast City Council and the PSNI on the “Park with us in Mind” scheme, currently in the initial stages of a 18-month pilot. Following Council leafleting and Traffic Attendant enforcement, the report from Belfast City Council indicates a reduction in the numbers of complaints and missed bin collections. My Department has also been advised, through feedback from our enforcement contract supplier, that a reduction in the amount of poorly parked vehicles has been observed by Traffic Attendants when conducting their enforcement activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the progress made on implementing any actions from the 2022 Inconsiderate Pavement Parking Options Paper.
answer_text: My Department sought the public’s views on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking. My officials are currently developing the next steps for my consideration and a formal response will issue when I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the number of (a) road; and (b) lane closure approvals that have been issued retrospectively by his Department in the last 12 months; and (ii) to detail the proportion of total approved (a) road; and (b) lane closures within that period that were issued retrospectively.
answer_text: My Department process over 22,000 road restrictions application each year from a range of stakeholders. Article 7 of the Road Traffic Regulation (NI) Order 1997 stipulates that the Department may give authorisation to restrict or prohibit the temporary use of a road by traffic. It is illegal for a road to be obstructed without my Department’s authorisation, with the exception of emergencies, or, for under a 15 minute duration.  In principle, approvals should be sought and approved before an obstruction is put in place on a road. However, there can be a small number of instances when retrospective approvals are granted. Such examples include, a follow-up approval after an urgent verbal request by a stakeholder requiring a restriction to be put in place, or, a late notification seeking an extension to an existing closure. Since 1 April 2024, my Department has implemented a new online application process that allows such information to be extracted.  Regrettably, it has not been practical to undertake a manual check of applications before April 2024 due to the volume of applications held in a manual system.  However, I can advise that between 1 April to 16 December there were 15,401 approved applications of which 445 (2.9%) were retrospective approvals.  The table below splits these between road and lane closures. Closure typeTotal number of approvals(1-April to 16 December 2024)Retrospective approvalsPercentageLane Closure10,9802881.9%Road Closure4,4211571.0%Total15,4014452.9%I would also advise that the enforcement of temporary road restrictions is the responsibility of the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) adults; (ii) and children living in (a) relative poverty; and (b) absolute poverty in the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Official measures of absolute and relative poverty for NI are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). Due to the uncertainty around estimates at lower levels, the Department only publishes poverty rates at local council area level for the whole NI population, and not specifically for adults.Further information regarding the Family Resources Survey and NI poverty statistics can be found at the link below.https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/family-resources-survey-and-poverty-analysisThe Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) annually release official statistics for “Children in low income families: local area statistics”. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Equine Assisted Therapy and Learning pilot programmes being undertaken by (i) his Department; and (ii) individual Health and Social Care Trusts in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Equine Assisted Therapy and Learning Programme being undertaken by the Department;Northern and Belfast Trust Equine Facilitated Learning - Multi-Disciplinary TeamsTwo pilot programmes were developed between the Department and the Northern and Belfast Trust(s).  These aimed to enhance partnership and relationships between professional staff in the multi-disciplinary teams and leads in community organisations.  However, both pilots have been paused due to the sudden death of the facilitator.  Discussions are underway with the Trusts to identify how the pilots can progress.   The Equine Therapy programmes undertaken by the Trusts in the last 12 months are:Belfast Trust – Equine Facilitated Learning for Children in stable foster care. This programme aimed to provide children who have experienced adverse childhood experiences- including being exposed to domestic abuse with support and learning tools that will help them cope with moving forward after experiencing trauma, and improving their mental health and wellbeing. The programme commenced in summer 2023 and completed in spring 2024.   Western Trust – Equine Facilitated Learning ‘Early Help’This programme has been designed as an ‘early help’ intervention with the aim of supporting families in this geographic Trust area who have been subjected to violence and domestic abuse.  The programme was launched on 10th December the final day of ‘16 Days of Action Against Domestic and Sexual Abuse’ and ‘World Human Rights Day’ to demonstrate the Department’s commitment to tackling and preventing domestic and sexual abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the budget allocated to Equine Assisted Therapy and Learning by (i) his Department; and (ii) each Health and Social Care Trust in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: (i) The budget allocated by the Department for Equine Assisted Therapy and Learning in the 2024-25 financial year is £30,000. This is to cover the costs associated with pilot programmes and their evaluation. (ii) The budget allocation to each Health and Social Care Trust: Western Trust – Equine Facilitated Learning for Early HelpThe sum of £19,000 was allocated to the Western Trust to cover the costs of an Equine Facilitated Learning Programme. This has been designed as an ‘early help’ intervention with the aim of supporting families in this geographic Trust area who have been subject to violence and domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 18119/22-27, to detail (i) when his Department expects to receive the independent review into paid carer's leave entitlement; and (ii) when the final report will be published.
answer_text: The Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) are currently undertaking an independent review of available cost and financial benefit modelling for a paid carer’s leave entitlement. It is hoped this review will be completed in late Spring 2025. Consideration will be given to the publication of the final report in due course thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment his Department can make of the impact of traffic congestion in Belfast city centre on the local economy.
answer_text: While no formal assessment has been made of the economic impact, I have been in touch with the Minister for Infrastructure to discuss the matter and am fully supportive of his efforts to work with local businesses and their representative bodies on this issue. I and my officials continue to keep the situation under review, in liaison with relevant business and policy stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to the economic cost of traffic congestion in Belfast, particularly in relation to (i) micro-businesses; (ii) small businesses; and (iii) independent businesses.
answer_text: I recognise the impact congestion can have on the local economy. I have had informal discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure and continue to engage with him on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the Apprenticeship Action Plan will be published.
answer_text: My ambition is to expand the pipeline of future talent for business, as well as skills and employment opportunities for citizens through delivery of a three-year Apprenticeship Action Plan.  The purpose of the new Apprenticeship Action Plan is to clearly outline the key activities and outputs for the 2024-2027 period that will support the delivery and development of our apprenticeship provision. Officials are finalising the Apprenticeship Action Plan for my approval in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will answer AQW 18718/22-27.
answer_text: A response was provided on 12th December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how sustainable tourism itineraries are being promoted to overseas visitors to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Tourism Ireland highlights sustainable tourism itineraries and how overseas visitors can enjoy the North sustainably via a range of platforms, including publicity, advertising, social media and on its international website, Ireland.com. These focus on sustainable through both attractions and ways of travelling about the region.Current examples of this include; A new TV series called Fred’s Tour De Ireland, co-produced by Tourism Ireland and fronted by TV presenter Fred Sirieix, which features the Mournes, Strangford, Belfast, the Causeway Coast and Derry promoting sustainable exploration of Ireland by overseas visitors.The Tourism Ireland team in Spain shared details of train journeys on with Condé Nast Traveler, including the Derry to Coleraine train and the vintage train from Downpatrick to Inch Abbey. The website has an average of 2 million unique users monthly. 	In June 2025, Tourism Ireland will run a ‘Slow Tourism’ month promoting car-free travel itineraries to encourage sustainable exploration of the regions of Ireland. Tourism Ireland regularly shares sustainable tourism story ideas with its database of 20,000+ international travel and lifestyle journalists and on Tourism Ireland’s international website, there are pages specifically about how overseas visitors can enjoy Ireland sustainably.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether contractors working on behalf of NI Water attended (i) Onslow Parade; and (ii) Ravenhill Park, South Belfast, on 6 November 2024.
answer_text: The question you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that their contractors BSG Civil Engineering and Blockbusters were working in the vicinity of Onslow Parade and Ravenhill Park on Wednesday 6 November. Should you have any further queries with regards this you can contact NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the potential to provide additional support to help small businesses and micro-businesses with digital transformation.
answer_text: The £7.5m Digital Transformation Flexible Fund, supported under the Executive’s Complementary Fund, was launched in November 2023 to help SMEs overcome the barriers and challenges in developing their digital transformation plans. The Fund gives micro, small and medium sized businesses the opportunity to improve their resilience, develop new digital capabilities and drive-up levels of digital engagement and innovation.  This in turn will help drive productivity and competitiveness, delivering tangible benefits for the business itself, and for the wider economy. Three calls have already taken place resulting in 169 offers of support being made, with a further four calls planned.Invest NI also continues to provide advice and financial support through existing support mechanisms to help increase adoption of digitalisation within export focused businesses across the North of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any consideration they have given to the development of an Executive Data Strategy.
answer_text: We are assured that data is shared and used extensively across the Northern Ireland Civil Service within the boundaries of information law, to inform policy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration he has given to the development of a small businesses and micro-businesses strategy.
answer_text: Support for small and micro businesses is already provided within existing strategies and programmes.A major source of support is the council led Go Succeed Service that provides masterclasses and funding for those seeking to start or grow their own businesses.Invest NI offer a range of supports, including Ambition to Grow, Innovation Vouchers, Founder Labs, Business Innovation Grant and the Cluster Acceleration Programme. Support available through InterTradeIreland includes the Acumen Programme, Innovation Boost, Seed Enterprise Investment Scheme, and the Business Explorer Programme amongst others. Our Universities support the creation and growth of new businesses through programmes such as InnovateHer and Enactus through Queens University, and the Explore Enterprise Course and Enterprise Placement Year through Ulster University. The Further Education Colleges provide support through their Entrepreneurship Hub and their Entrepreneurship Club. The colleges, in partnership with my Department, also deliver the SME Productivity Booster, equipping businesses to innovate, grow, and create jobs. In summary, there is an extensive range of support available to micro and small businesses, and I would recommend that anyone interested in learning more, should visit nibusinessinfo.co.uk in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how their Department shares data in order to better inform the policy development process.
answer_text: The Department’s Data Protection Officer works with business areas to ensure that Data Sharing Agreements are in place, where required, to inform the development of various policy areas.  This work is guided by the Information Commissioner's Office’s code of practice, which ensures that, where data sharing is required, it is done in accordance with the law and in a safe and compliant manner.Several key strategic policy areas of the Department have benefited to date, including:addressing the needs of refugees and asylum seekers, where TEO works collaboratively with, for example, other NICS Departments and Agencies, the Home Office, local Councils, and charitable organisations;the Public Appointments process;		the Truth Recovery Process here in respect of Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries, and Workhouses;Historical Institutional Abuse; andthe Victims & Survivors Service.TEO has been working with NISRA to bring improvements in the availability and accessibility of equality data. As part of the draft PfG, NISRA statisticians have developed a “Wellbeing dashboard” to inform future iterations of the PfG, which uses the latest NISRA data to show the wellbeing of people as individuals, communities and as a society across ten domains of wellbeing.TEO has been involved in the development of guidance, published by NISRA, aimed at improving the harmonisation of collection and reporting of equality data; and has been working with NISRA on the early stages of the development of an equality data tool. The objective is to provide access to readily consumable data visualisations, for use by officials, stakeholders and the general public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide details of the feedback provided by students who have completed the English for Speakers of Other Languages course through further education colleges in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My officials have been advised by the Further Education colleges that they employ robust and rigorous feedback processes.  To provide a student-centred experience, colleges have in place established processes for capturing the student voice.  This includes post induction survey, focus groups with students and pre-exit surveys. Each programme will also elect a student representative who will be engaged with during each semester to capture the views of the student group.  This feedback is reviewed by the programme team and used as part of the evaluation process.		Further to this, my Department conducts a Survey of Further Education College Leavers.		The 2022/23 survey shows that 94% of English for Speakers of Other Languages students were satisfied with their Further Education experience, compared to 92% for all Further Education leavers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans he has to address the inequality in postgraduate loan provision between Northern Ireland and England, with a maximum loan of £6,500 for those normally resident in Northern Ireland and a maximum of £12,471 for those normally resident in England.
answer_text: Following a public consultation the maximum postgraduate tuition fee loan amount available here rose from £5,500 to £6,500 for academic year 2023/24.  Alongside this, there was a commitment to review this after three years.  I have committed to a review of Higher Education Funding, including the financial support provided to students and have instructed my officials to bring forward detailed proposals for this review.  I will be considering whether the issue of postgraduate tuition fee loans is addressed through this proposed Funding Review or separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how the English for Speakers of Other Languages course is evaluated; and (ii) how the views of course participants are sought (a) during; and (b) after the completion of a course.
answer_text: My officials are advised by the Further Education colleges that evaluation of English for Speakers of Other Languages programmes follows a set process used across all programmes delivered by the Further Education colleges.  The annual evaluation of the English for Speakers of Other Languages includes a review of enrolment and achievement levels and quality of teaching and learning including learner experience. The learners experience is captured through induction surveys and student voice surveys which are undertaken throughout the academic year and on exit of the programme.Further to this, my Department conducts the Survey of Further Education College Leavers. The most up to date results for the 2022/23 academic year shows that 94% of English for Speakers of Other Languages students were satisfied with their Further Education experience, compared to 92% for all Further Education leavers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 17473/22-27, whether the review of further education student support will include a review of the level of loans available to postgraduate students.
answer_text: The departmental review of Further Education student support does not include a review of the level of loans available to postgraduate students. However, I have committed to a review of Higher Education Funding, including the financial support provided to students and have instructed my officials to bring forward detailed proposals for this review. I will be considering whether the issue of postgraduate tuition fee loans is addressed through this proposed funding review or separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking in relation to the removal of flags on lamp-posts in the Carryduff area.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the problem of illegal flags and emblems on street furniture. It is a persistent problem that requires a comprehensive and lasting solution.Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of our primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a hazard to road users, we will seek to remove that danger. Where there is no such danger, we will liaise closely with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution.In this particular case, my officials have contacted the PSNI and other partner agencies (local council, NIHE, DfC etc)  and taken the appropriate action based on the advice received. Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to his mitigations to ease traffic congestion in Belfast city centre, to detail the improvements he will make in relation to services to and from Cairnshill park and ride facility.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that as part of the mitigations to ease traffic congestion in Belfast, peak time frequency on the 652 service from Cairnshill Park and Ride was enhanced from 13th May this year. This increased the service to provide 10 departures from Cairnshill between 7.00am and 9.20am Monday to Friday at the following times:0700071507450800081008200840090009100920Translink has recently revealed that over 400k additional passenger journeys have been made on public transport in the last two months compared to the same period last year.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration he has given to extending childcare support grants to part-time students.
answer_text: I recognise the rising cost of childcare and the impact this has on parents and families who are already struggling with the cost of living. I am committed to a review of Higher Education Funding, including childcare support provided to students.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide details of the permit that facilitated the closure of Baronscourt Road, South Belfast, on 20 November 2024.
answer_text: On 13 November 2024 my Department received an application for a road closure from NIE on the Saintfield Road to Baronscourt Link Road to facilitate a new connection.  The closure request was for one day on 20th November 2024. Officials in my department discussed the proposed closure with the Temporary Traffic Management Contractor and stressed the necessity of completing the works with minimal inconvenience.  Following these discussions, officials provided verbal agreement for the closure, however permit number 2024/LICA/26381 was issued retrospectively for the road closure on 22 November 2024My Department process in excess of 25,000 applications for road or lane closures annually and has recently introduced a new computerised system. Unfortunately, due to staffing resource issues, the permit for the road closure was issued retrospectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how road closures in local constituencies are notified to (i) residents; (ii) MLAs; and (iii) local councillors.
answer_text: My Department does not notify road closures directly to residents, MLAs and local councillors. Officials process between 25,000 and 30,000 road or lane closure applications annually on a recently developed system. This system sends an email notification of each approved work permit in advance of planned roadworks to a council-based distribution list which includes the local council, blue-light services, coastal and transportation services. Upcoming roadworks on the busiest routes are also advertised in the main press.Given the volume of closures, and in line with the Limited Service principles being implemented because of funding pressures, communication with road users in relation to upcoming roadworks are focused on the busiest routes, primarily the 'A' Class network.Communications include the use of advanced notification signage or Variable Messaging Signs on the impacted route in advance of the works, as well as notification on TrafficWatchNI as resources permit. Priority is given to advertisement of those roadworks that affect the public the most.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the housing developments in the area, to detail any plans he has to deliver improved active travel facilities along (i) Ballymaconaghy Road; and (ii) Manse Road, South Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise there is committed development for two large housing schemes on the northside of the Ballymaconaghy Road. Active travel measures associated with these include new 2m footways the length of the realigned road, a controlled pedestrian crossing near Knockbracken Manor / Laurel Grove Avenue and improvements to the roundabout at the Newton Park/Manse Road/Ballymaconaghy Road junction (Four Winds roundabout)As these are developer led schemes, my Department does not yet have a timeframe for construction. My Department’s Active Travel programme includes provision for a new section of footway along Manse Road connecting the existing public footways on Ballygowan Road and Manse Road and extending the existing footway facilities at Lagan College. Construction of the new footway is subject to the successful completion of detailed design and land acquisition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department will deliver (i) safe walking; and (ii) cycling routes for children and young people to access (a) Lagan College; and (b) Loughview Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a £227,000 project on Church Road in Castlereagh to providing new footway along the eastern side of Church Road, linking the existing public footway at Grey Castle Manor to the Henry Jones playing fields. This footway will provide a safe route for parents and children walking to and from Loughview Integrated Primary School. Officials are also developing plans for a new section of footway along Manse Road, Castlereagh that would connect the existing public footways on Ballygowan Road and Manse Road, beyond Lisnabreeny Road, extending the existing public footway on Manse Road, making it safer to walk to Lagan College.My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan that includes an update of our policies and design guidance for Active Travel as well as network plans for our larger towns and cities, including the Metropolitan Castlereagh area. Developing safe walking and cycling routes to schools is proposed as a key priority within that plan. Public consultation on the draft plan runs until 28 February 2025. I would encourage all those with an interest in development of high quality, safe pedestrian and cycling infrastructure to engage with this consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the progress that has been made on the review of terms, conditions and workload for further education lecturers.
answer_text: In April 2024 I approved a pay remit business case submitted by the Further Education Colleges, as employers, for lecturers that aligned to the pay settlement provided to teachers in the North of Ireland for 2023/24. Conditions agreed between the employers and lecturing trade unions within the pay agreement included a commitment to teacher pay scale parity to be informed through an independent examination of the pay, all terms (including addressing policy review); to develop an industrial relations model that will transform and modernise the existing pay, reward, and conditions of service for all academic staff within regional Further Education Colleges; and for key deliverables and timescales to be agreed.		Due to the cessation of the College Employers’ Forum, I commissioned an independent review on the collaborative/structural working arrangements between the Further Education Colleges in respect of staff terms and conditions, including pay.  I am currently reviewing the final draft of this  collaboration review, with a view to share with College Governing Body Chairs and Principals/Chief Executives. This will enable a structured forum to be formed within the sector to deliver the conditions specified within the 2023/24 pay offer for lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure who is responsible for enforcing the clearing of debris created by site traffic around new housing developments.
answer_text: Article 7 of the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 places a duty on District Councils to ensure that all roads within their area, with the exception of designated roads, are, so far as is practical, kept clear of litter and detritus. My Department is responsible for keeping Designated Roads, such as the motorways, clear of litter and detritus.Other deposits on the road which are likely to cause an obstruction or danger are offences under Articles 94(1) or 95(1) of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 and are dealt with by the PSNI.  However, such deposits on the road must be deemed by the PSNI to constitute a danger to persons using the road before proceedings are initiated.While the responsibility for any prosecution would lie with the PSNI, the objective of enforcement is, however, not so much to secure convictions and to impose penalties, as to restore the condition of the road, and/or to discourage other potential offenders.  Where my Department’s interests can be protected by persuasion, rather than prosecution, so much the better.To this end my officials monitor roads on a regular basis and, when they can be clearly identified, any offenders are instructed to clean a road when it is unduly dirty.  The PSNI are also informed, and if the offenders do not co-operate, they may consider taking action.In certain circumstances, particularly in the interests of road safety, my Department may carry out whatever operations are necessary to restore the road condition and may subsequently seek to recover costs from the offender if possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of an electric vehicle strategy.
answer_text: Switching fuels to low or zero emission alternatives is a key tenet of our approach to transport decarbonisation with the electrification of surface transport playing a pivotal role in reducing emissions in the Transport Sector.I have made clear my commitment to delivering greener, cleaner, sustainable transport. Modelling undertaken by my Department has shown that a focus on removing car emissions through the transition to zero emission vehicles will have the most significant impact on reducing carbon emissions from the transport sector. The introduction of the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes legislation in the North will support this transition by further accelerating the number of zero emission vehicles entering the new car market and building stock to enter the second-hand market. There are now over 30,000 EVs on our roads, this figure having more than doubled in the past two years. Encouragingly, strong growth in EV uptake is evident across all constituencies here.Through the EV Infrastructure Task Force my Department has worked with other stakeholders to help facilitate the expansion of the charge point network. With a publicly accessible charge point network of over 640 chargers now in place here, this represents growth in EV charging provision of 65% since April 2023.
