The Rapid/Ultra Rapid charging public network, which offers the fastest, most convenient method of charging has grown by over 300% here in the same period.My officials also continue to engage with officials from the British Government’s Office for Zero Emission Vehicles on grants and funding available for EV Infrastructure here. I am pleased to confirm that the North has now had it’s 1,000th workplace charge point installed through the Workplace Charging Scheme. These charge points provide additional options for charging to EV owners, and will further compliment the growing publicly accessible charge point network.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any plans to consider further adaptations to Parliament Buildings to improve accessibility.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work ongoing within his Department to scope the potential for converting lamp-posts into electric vehicle charge points.
answer_text: I have made clear my commitment to delivering greener, cleaner, sustainable transport and switching fuels to low or zero emission alternatives is a key tenet of my Department’s approach to transport decarbonisation. My Department installs its own underground cable network to provide electricity to its street lights, which is designed to accommodate only the relatively low electrical load required to power these lights. There is no additional capacity that would support any substantial additional load, such as that required for electric vehicle charging.While no formal study has been undertaken, to upgrade my Department's street lighting network to support electric vehicle charging, would require the replacement of all of its underground cable network with mains supplies from a Distribution Network Operator to each street light.Due to the high costs and levels of disruption involved, it is not considered practicable or affordable to upgrade the network for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is considering the development of a universal 0-to-3 learning framework.

To ask the Minister for the Economy for an overview of the engagement of his Department with the Executive Office in the development of an updated Executive international relations strategy through (i) the Tourism Partnership Board; and (ii) other forums.
answer_text: My Department engages as appropriate with the Executive Office on areas of mutual interest, including how and where we showcase our capabilities to an international audience.The Tourism Partnership Board, established by my Department, and other forums, including the Executive Office’s International Relations Working Group, are useful groups in facilitating collaboration and information sharing on international policy matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of the lack of an updated international relations strategy has on the ability of the Executive to promote Northern Ireland as an attractive tourist destination.
answer_text: The North is successfully promoted as an attractive tourism destination overseas by Tourism Ireland and closer to home by Tourism NI.Whilst I look forward to the publication of an updated International Relations Strategy, both of the agencies that promote the North to potential visitors have bespoke strategies for identifying target customers, with sustainability at the heart of their approach.For overseas visitors, resources are focused on delivering ‘revenue per carbon footprint’, prioritising visitors with value adding tourism traits. This approach also encourages sustainable tourism itineraries for our overseas visitors, including active travel, rail travel and ways to get out into nature.  Officials responsible for the development of the Executive’s International Relations strategy also sit on the Tourism Partnership Board, to ensure that common themes can be identified and maximised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the delivery of a bespoke skills action plan for the tourism industry.
answer_text: The Tourism Partnership Board is currently developing an innovative Action Plan which will include a range of interventions to grow our tourism offering over the next 10 years and contribute to the delivery of my Economic Vision. Once agreed by the Board, I will consider their recommendations, with a view to publishing a Tourism Vision and final Action Plan before the end of the year.Addressing skills gaps and improving career pathways is crucial for sustainable growth in our tourism industry. My officials will continue to engage with the Tourism Partnership Board to support the ongoing skills needs within the industry and ensure it utilises the existing skills provision which contribute to upskilling for the industry, including:Skill UpSkills Focus, InnovateUs, Apprenticeships NI Level 2 Traineeships and Further Education courses

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any additional support measures he can introduce to help micro-businesses and small businesses take on apprentices.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of support to help all employers, including micro-business and small businesses, employ apprentices. This includes: Making the case for and overseeing significant budget to fund the delivery of apprentices – this includes recent increases in budget to support all-age apprenticeships.Establishing a high-quality and local network of providers to deliver the off the job learning.  Training is delivered by further education colleges, higher education institutions, the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise and non-statutory contracted training suppliers. Facilitating the engagement of stakeholders and employers through 19 Sectoral Partnerships to ensure apprenticeship pathways and content align with the needs of our economy.Delivering apprenticeship promotional campaigns, and the annual Apprenticeship Week and Awards which help to highlight the benefits of apprenticeships to both potential apprentices and employers. In addition, I recently launched an Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund which will support innovative ideas from the skills and employer community on how to address barriers to apprenticeships.  Finally, I will be publishing an Apprenticeship Action Plan which will set out wider actions that will be delivered this mandate to further develop our apprenticeship system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work he is undertaking to increase the volume of conferences being held in Northern Ireland as part of generating greater tourism spend in the local economy.
answer_text: To determine the potential to generate tourism revenue into the local economy from conferences and business events, Tourism NI led on the development of an Integrated Strategy for Business Events.Tourism NI is currently conducting research to update the revenue potential for business tourism since the pandemic.Tourism NI works with Belfast City Council and Derry City & Strabane District Council and their Destination Management Organisations, Visit Belfast & Visit Derry to undertake an annual programme of sales and marketing activity. This supports local businesses and the region to secure international conferences and achieve growth - increasing the North’s ability to attract high value, year-round, midweek business events to the region. Tourism NI, Belfast City Council & Derry City & Strabane District Council also provide funding for conference support to attract conference business into the North. Tourism NI also coordinates and leads delegations of local industry attendance at national and international Business Tourism trade shows such as IMEX Frankfurt, IMEX America, IBTM World, The Meetings Show and in-market activity in the business tourism markets of North America, Britain & Germany. On foot of this activity, in 2023-24 conferences here generated £41 million of direct economic expenditure into the local economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he has had with the UK Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology on the deployment of Artificial Intelligence in government departments.
answer_text: I have met and discussed Artificial Intelligence with Lord Patrick Vallance MP, Minister for Science, Innovation and Technology on 2 September and 13 November and I will continue to engage on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress in relation to changes to the financial support available for students on pre-registration Allied Health Professional degree courses commissioned by his Department.
answer_text: I am currently exploring with my officials possible future changes to the financial support provided to students on pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department. While this work is currently in its early stages, it could, potentially, impact on the Allied Health professions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why the fixed cycle lane separators on Upper Arthur Street, Belfast, were removed; and (ii) whether he has any plans to reinstate them to improve safety for cyclists.
answer_text: My Department has not instructed the removal of cycling infrastructure from Upper Arthur Street, Belfast. The cycling wands in Upper Arthur Street are however vulnerable to vehicle damage, particularly where vehicles drive over the wands causing them to split and fall apart. Officials are currently liaising with our external contractor regarding the programming of works to reinstate the missing cycling infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any improvements that can be made to (i) cycling; (ii) pedestrian; and (iii) wheelchair access at Adelaide Train Station, South Belfast.
answer_text: I am committed to promoting access for all public transport passengers and my officials and Translink continue to engage with key stakeholders regarding improvements for integrating walking, wheeling and cycling into all public transport journeys. Translink have recently completed a £3.8m upgrade of the platforms at Adelaide station that has significantly improved access to all platforms, and which also now permit the stoppage of six car trains. Work has also been completed on a new spectator walkway linking the station with the National Football Stadium at Windsor Park.  My Department are also engaging with Translink and Belfast City Council to explore options that would improve pedestrian and cycling linkages between Adelaide train station and the wider Belfast Cycling Network proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 17330/22-27, to detail the funding provided to further education colleges for the provision of English for speakers of other languages in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department provided the Further Education colleges with £1.7 million as part of their overall resource budget allocation in 2020/21 for the provision of English for Speakers of Other Languages, and this continues to be carried forward in their budget baseline.In addition to this, the Further Education colleges have received £1.7 million in additional funding over the last five years, details in Table 1, through successful bids made by my Department to the Home Office and the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government. Funding was utilised to support with the provision of English Language training and to remove barriers to English Language training, including, childcare, travel, and IT equipment.Table 1 - Funding for English Language Provision 2020/21-2024/25Financial Year Funding Amount 2020/21£17,2392021/22£15,9542022/23£768,0612023/24£451,3792024/25£461,551

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any bids he submitted for October Monitoring Round allocation.
answer_text: My Department submitted bids to the Department of Finance as part of the October Monitoring Round as follows:Non-Earmarked Resource DEL of £13.9 million;Earmarked Resource DEL of £1.8 million;Depreciation and Impairment of £36.7 million;Non-Earmarked Capital DEL of £19.7 million; andFinancial Transactions Capital of £0.2 million.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 5568/22-27, to detail (i) the work that has been carried out on the development of a new international relations strategy since February 2024; and (ii) when a draft strategy will be brought to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: We have asked officials to consider a new strategy that will support the delivery of priorities as set out in the draft Programme for Government.As part of that work, they have engaged at official level across Departments to deepen our understanding of the existing partnerships and opportunities that exist. This has included discussions with our overseas offices and delivery partners, as well as key stakeholders such as Academia and Local Government on emerging priorities in the current global context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to improve public transport services along Saintfield Road towards Carryduff, if the proposed North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route is not introduced within the expected time frame.
answer_text: Work on the feasibility of extending to Glengormley and Carryduff has been finalised and I commissioned work on the delivery options that could be implemented, utilising the £35m funding envelope from the Belfast Region City Deal.  The Outline Business Case is being finalised in line with these feasibility reports and delivery options. I welcome the £35m contribution from Belfast Region City Deal and recognise that this significant investment will bring enhancements to the city centre and wider public transport network as we seek to promote sustainable travel options and improve public transport journey time reliability.Translink have also confirmed that they are working with Lisburn and Castlereagh Council, Planning Service and developers in the Carryduff area to enhance the public transport offering for residents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the policies and procedures governing the disposal of surplus public land and property.
answer_text: The disposal of public sector assets is subject to a range of policy, guidance and legislative requirements. The transfer of surplus assets must comply with the Stormont Regulation and Government Property Act (NI) 1933, which states that any sale, exchange or lease of land must be 'at the best price, best rent or best terms'. All public sector bodies are expected to adhere to the Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property guidance when disposing of their surplus public sector owned assets, and by doing so must comply with the requirements of Managing Public Money NI.Land & Property Services’ (LPS) Central Advisory Unit (CAU) manages, oversees and provides advice in relation to the disposal (or D1) process.There are two pathways to disposal, the internal market and the external market. CAU first circulates the D1 to the internal market which is all public sector bodies, Councils and third sector organisations (TSO) represented by Development Trusts NI (DTNI). The market value of the property is provided by LPS valuers.If a potential purchaser is identified through the internal market process, a timeline is agreed between the parties to bring the transfer to conclusion. There are specific requirements if the property is to be transferred to a TSO as a Community Asset Transfer. Should an internal market transfer not occur consideration of any legal requirement to offer the asset back to a former owner will take place. An offer back to former owners may be necessary if the asset meets the criteria set out within the Land Clauses Consolidation Act 1845 (LCCA) or because of the application of the Crichel Down rules as applied in Northern Ireland. A summary of these is contained in the LPS disposal guidelines.An asset owner may also consider a sale to an adjoining owner, for example, when portions of surplus land have no marketing potential such as “small plots of publicly owned land that may be of interest to adjoining homeowners to provide additional garden.”Only when all the above disposal options have been discounted will the asset move to an open market for disposal, and these are also managed by CAU.   Links to relevant documents are listed.Stormont Regulation and Government Property Act (Northern Ireland) 1933Disposal of surplus land and property - publications | Department of FinanceCommunity Asset Transfer: Guidance for Asset Owners | Department for CommunitiesCommunity Asset Transfer policy framework | Department for CommunitiesBetter Business Cases NI Supplementary Guidance | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework will support local food and drink producers.
answer_text: I was delighted to be able to launch Northern Ireland’s first ever Food Strategy Framework on 14 November.  The NI Food Strategy Framework, which was approved by the Executive on 18 July 2024, is a new strategic collaborative approach around food that will help to improve economic, environmental, health and social outcomes for Northern Ireland.  It will set the direction of travel for cross-departmental working around food.I am very aware of the value and importance of the agri-food sector to Northern Ireland’s economy, with the agriculture and food processing sectors employing around 80,000 people. This is particularly significant in our rural and coastal areas.  My department is dedicated to improving the sustainability, productivity and resilience of the agri-food sector in Northern Ireland and continues to operate a range of existing programmes to support the sector. This includes the Northern Ireland Regional Food Programme, which supports the promotion of NI produce.  However, no single Northern Ireland department has all the policy tools required to deliver the Framework’s vision and ambition.  Work is therefore ongoing to develop a cross departmental action plan, which I aim to bring forward to the Executive in early 2025.  It is anticipated that this action plan will set out both interventions which can be quickly made, as well as the steps which will prepare for longer term initiatives.  Delivery of the Framework will require significant leadership, strong partnerships, a shared understanding of needs, the combination of resources, and effective governance arrangements.  With these factors in place, it will be a great success.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on a renewed Race Relations (Northern Ireland) Order 1997.
answer_text: Following an extensive 12-week consultation on the Review of the Race Relations Order, a report on the feedback and responses was published on 5 August 2024. Our officials are working to bring policy changes to the Executive with the intention of presenting them to the Assembly in the coming year.This will move us a step closer to providing the best possible legal protection against discrimination.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 14548/22-27, to detail any progress made on further modelling and refinement of the cost of implementing an entitlement to paid carer's leave.
answer_text: The Good Jobs consultation closed to responses on 30 September 2024. Consultation responses are currently being analysed. I have made clear my desire to introduce paid carer’s leave, subject to funding being agreed at an Executive level. The issue of cost implications, including the financial and societal benefits of a paid entitlement, will be important considerations for the Executive.Engagement with various stakeholders, including CarersNI, the Carers Poverty Commission and a cross departmental working group on the issues impacting unpaid carers, is ongoing. Engagement with the Department for Business and Trade to discuss their plans for a review of carer’s leave in Britain has also commenced.Independently, the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) has been commissioned to undertake an independent review of available cost and financial benefit modelling for a paid carer’s leave entitlement. HMRC have also been asked to impact the options and costs for the potential implementation of paid carer’s leave.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent flooding in the area, to detail NI Water's plans to replace and upgrade the storm drains in (i) Ravenhill Park; and (ii) Onslow Parade, South Belfast.
answer_text: My Department’s officials are not aware of any recent flooding in the Ravenhill Park and Onslow Parade area.  NI Water has confirmed it does not own any dedicated storm drains in the area as both Ravenhill Park and Onslow Parade are served by a combined sewerage system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any improvements that can be made to the lighting and safety features of car park 8 Broadway that is used by staff at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised that car park 8 at Broadway currently has CCTV coverage which is monitored 24/7 by security staff, pedestrian and vehicle access gates which are access controlled out of hours and adequate lighting that meets all UK standards for car-parking lighting. In late October 2024 a fault with the car park lighting was reported to the car park services team. Northern Ireland Electricity recently reviewed the fault, and the Trust is currently waiting on their report / recommendation.  It is hoped that remedial work to rectify the fault will commence  in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 14548/22-27, to detail any work to assess the ongoing cost of not implementing an entitlement to paid carer's leave.
answer_text: According to Census 21, there are over 183,000 unpaid carers of working age in the north of Ireland. Of those, almost 14,000 work fewer than 15 hours per week and 30,000 work between 16 and 30 hours per week. There are 61,620 unpaid carers of working age who are unemployed. Of these unpaid carers, 16,670 reported as having never worked and 44,950 reported as unemployed.High levels of economic inactivity undermine our economy and adversely impact the living standards of individuals. I am mindful that rates are particularly high for those with caring responsibilities, which can lead to some unpaid carers having to reduce their working hours, decline promotion opportunities or leave the workforce altogether.Research undertaken by Queen’s University at the request of my Department reported that the majority of claimants of Carers' Benefits are female and of working age (65% female compared to 35% male in the case of Carer's Allowance).  Of all claimants in this category, 70% were of working age.A regulatory impact assessment of the cost of an unpaid entitlement to carer’s leave was published alongside the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill consultation. The issue of cost implications, including the financial and societal benefits of a paid entitlement, continue to be explored by officials and the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre on behalf of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the decision of the Welsh Government to make the non-domestic rates relief for childcare premises permanent, (i) to provide her assessment of this decision; and (ii) to detail any consideration she has given to implementing a similar scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 17520/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the GP referral pathway for asylum seekers.
answer_text: The Department, supported by the Business Services Organisation and Health and Social Care Trusts, has piloted a managed pathway to GP registration for people seeking asylum as close to the point of entry to Northern Ireland as possible. The pilot scheme started on 1 August 2024. A review of the scheme is ongoing and is intended to complete by the end January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12703/22-27, to detail any plans his Department has to upgrade and resurface footways on Locksley Park, South Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. Whilst there are many roads that would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Unfortunately there are no plans to upgrade and resurface footways in Locksley Park as part of the 2024/25 resurfacing programme.Details of planned resurfacing works for 2024/25 are provided in the Department’s Annual Report to Belfast City Council which will be presented to Council on 25th November 2024.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of Locksley Park and to repair defects in accordance with the Department’s current maintenance policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to his Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill, to detail the support that can be provided to parents with multiples.
answer_text: The Good Jobs consultation closed to responses on 30 September 2024. Consultation responses are currently being finalised. Policy decisions will be taken in due course.The range of employment law measures to support working families, both existing and proposed, is available to parents of children from multiple births on the same basis as all parents.  In relation to my proposals for neonatal leave and pay, I am aware that multiple children are more likely to require neonatal care.  The right to request flexible working enables the making of a wide variety of types of requests reflecting specific individual and family circumstances.Maternity pay policy and support from benefits are matters for the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide clarification on the Child Maintenance Service’s policy of pursuing payments missed by paying parents.
answer_text: Where a parent with care reports missed payments by the paying parent, the Child Maintenance Service can take action to move the case to the Collect and Pay Service so payments can be monitored and where necessary, the Child Maintenance Service can take enforcement action to re-establish compliance. The Child Maintenance Service will support parents on the Direct Pay Service with periodic text message reminders to make their child maintenance payment or report missed payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale behind the policy of the Child Maintenance Service to only pursue missing payments if recipients of child maintenance use the pay and collect process.
answer_text: The Collect and Pay Service for child maintenance payments is a default option used when the non-resident parent agrees to the arrangements, or the Child Maintenance Service is satisfied that without the arrangement’s, child maintenance is unlikely to be paid in accordance with the child maintenance calculation.  This could arise when child maintenance payments have been missed under the Direct Pay Service.Under the Direct Pay Service, the Child Maintenance Service is only responsible for calculating child maintenance payments with the non-resident parent making the maintenance payment directly to the parent with care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the policy for Child Maintenance Service to only pursue missing payments if recipients of child maintenance use the pay and collect process was introduced.
answer_text: The Child Maintenance Service was introduced in 2012 to encourage parents to make their own arrangements where it is safe and appropriate to do so, with the statutory scheme as a fallback for those who could not do so. As part of the statutory scheme the Direct Pay and Collect and Pay Services were introduced. For cases on Collect and Pay, the Child Maintenance Service can take enforcement action to re-establish compliance on missing payments.Under the Direct Pay Service, the Child Maintenance Service is only responsible for calculating child maintenance payments with the non-resident parent making the maintenance payment directly to the parent with care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work of the Trading Standards Service in preventing the sale of vape products to children.
answer_text: This is a matter for the Department of Health given its responsibility for the health impacts of tobacco and vapes.  The legislative framework here provides that District Councils are primarily responsible for the enforcement of age of sale restrictions in respect of tobacco and nicotine vapes.   The Department (via the Public Health Agency) funds Tobacco Control Officers to carry out test purchasing exercises and engage with retailers to support compliance with existing legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of businesses that have faced action from the Trading Standards Service in the last 12 months as a consequence of selling vape products to children.
answer_text: District Councils enforce underage sales breaches in relation to tobacco and nicotine vapes in Northern Ireland. The most recent available data for Northern Ireland for breaches of underage sale of nicotine vape products shows that in 2023-2024, there were:61 fixed penalty notices 18 written warnings 2 investigations ongoing1 no further action2 Restricted Premises Orders

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with the Minster for the Economy in relation to data sharing around special educational needs and support requirements for relevant people moving from schools to further or higher education.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that young people with statements of special educational needs (SEN) are supported in their transition from school into adulthood, including progression into institutions of further or higher education.   To further this work, my department and the Department of Health (DoH) jointly chair a Transitions Working Group supported by officials from the Departments for the Economy (DfE) and Communities (DfC).  The purpose of the group is to understand the experiences and perceptions of the transitions planning process to ensure that there are clear and appropriate pathways for young people with SEN and/or disability across further and higher education, training or employment.Officials have been working closely to ensure structures are in place to share data that is captured across the system and explore how this can be better used to inform planning of provisions for children and young people with SEN and disabilities. My department is also working closely with colleagues from the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) on the Education Outcomes Linkage project. As the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN concludes and we move forward into the delivery phase, the capturing of data to monitor improved outcomes for children and young people with SEN will be critical and I will be engaging with Executive colleagues as this progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department is working with schools to prevent children from accessing and using vape products.
answer_text: Issues around vaping are addressed as part of the statutory curriculum.  At primary level, pupils are taught about the importance of making good choices and being aware of the effects of drugs and alcohol on their own health and others.  At post-primary level, pupils explore the concept of health as part of the development of a whole person and investigate the effects on the body of legal and illegal substances, and the risks and consequences of their use.All schools are required to have a drugs policy in place and the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment has published Drugs Guidance for Schools in Northern Ireland.  The guidance was developed in consultation with the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.Schools also have the flexibility to work directly with external organisations, such as the PHA and the Police Service of Northern Ireland, to provide learning opportunities in the delivery of these important aspects of the curriculum.	As recognised during the debate on the recent Assembly motion on vaping in schools, this is an issue that goes beyond the school gates, and it is therefore a matter that requires a collective response.  As such, my department will work closely with the Department of Health, the Public Health Agency and, as necessary, the Department of Justice and the PSNI, following appropriate guidance and regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to develop and implement a public health campaign around the dangers and potential harm of children using vaping products.
answer_text: Due to the significant budgetary challenges faced by my Department a decision was taken to freeze all campaign advertising for 2024/25. The budgetary pressures that led to the freeze on advertising spend remain severe. The Public Health Agency (PHA) continues to promote health issues and advice via social media and through its website.The PHA, working with partners, has also taken forward a number of relevant initiatives:‘Vaping - the Facts’ training – this was provided region-wide for professionals in healthcare, social care, education and youth work.  This training is now available online and has been distributed through CCEA to all post primary schools, SEN schools and regional colleges.  The resources have also been made available for onward distribution to parents/carers.  A stop smoking/vaping pilot service is being undertaken in schools in Belfast and South Eastern areas.  An extensive piece of research, shortly to conclude, which seeks to understand the use of vaping among young people. The Tobacco and Vapes Bill, recently introduced at Westminster, provides a landmark opportunity to create a smoke-free Northern Ireland and address the increasing use of vapes particularly among younger people. In respect of vapes (both nicotine and non-nicotine) the Bill will:Prohibit advertising and sponsorship agreements for vaping and nicotine products.Ban the sale of non-nicotine vapes and nicotine products to under 18s. The Bill will also ban the purchase of these products on behalf of someone under 18. Ban vending machines for the sale of vaping or nicotine products.Ban the free distribution of vaping and nicotine products to people of all ages.Provide regulation making powers to:Regulate the contents and flavours of vaping and nicotine products – and any accessories to vaping products which impact flavour. Regulate packaging and product requirements of vaping and nicotine products. Regulate the display of vaping and nicotine products in retail premises. The Bill strengthens enforcement activity to support the implementation of the above measures including extending the current registration scheme for retailers of tobacco to vaping products and nicotine products.  The Bill also provides powers to introduce a licensing scheme for the retail sale of tobacco, herbal smoking products, cigarette papers, vapes and nicotine products following consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the educational and training provision for prisoners.
answer_text: Rehabilitation is a key priority for the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS).  Those committed to its care by the courts are supported and challenged to change and address their offending behaviour.  Through a wide and progressive curriculum, NIPS offers individuals the opportunity to develop skills and attain qualifications.  This will help give them the best chance to secure gainful employment on release, which evidence shows is a key factor in desisting reoffending.  Delivery of the curriculum is provided by Belfast Met and North West Regional College, adopting the Further Education delivery model.Cognisant of the diverse needs of the prison population a varied curriculum offers accredited training from Entry Level 3 to Level 3 in educational, vocational and employability skills.  This includes Essential Skills training in Literacy, Numeracy and Information Computer Technology; construction skills such as bricklaying, joinery and tiling; hospitality skills such as barista training, catering, and food hygiene; as well as training in barbering, hair and beauty.  Soft skills such as interview techniques and CV writing are also offered as part of the range of employability skills programs.  The curriculum is kept under review to respond to the changing needs of the prison population, emerging industries within the labour market and labour market shortages, including current and future skills gaps.  Belfast Met, as the lead College acting on behalf of the college partnership, recently conducted a substantive curriculum review, making recommendations which will be used to help inform the strategic direction of learning and skills over the next 5 to 10 years.  In addition to the core learning and skills provision all persons in NIPS care have access to higher education, currently offered through the Open University.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12.
answer_text: The department developed and published the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12 in 2012, to provide families in Northern Ireland with assurance of a consistent level of quality in childcare services and to ensure a regional approach to registration and inspection across HSC Trusts. The Standards supplement and support the legal requirements set out in The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 and associated Guidance and Regulations (Volume 2).The childcare landscape has changed significantly since 2012, and I am fully aware of the range of challenges currently facing the sector. Work is underway across Departments, led by the Department of Education, to support the development of a new Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy with the dual aims of supporting child development and enabling parental employment. The delivery of affordable and accessible childcare is a commitment of the Northern Ireland Executive and effective legislative and standards frameworks will be a key to delivering on that commitment. I have made a commitment to undertake a full review of the Minimum Standards. A wide range of stakeholders have already expressed an interest in coming forward to assist the department with the review, and my officials will be contacting those individuals in the coming weeks to formally commence the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14633/22-27, to detail the level of priority that will be given to the resurfacing scheme on the A24 Ormeau Road and Saintfield Road in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: The resurfacing of the A24 Ormeau Road is recognised as a high priority within my Department’s current programme of works. However, the progression of this scheme will be subject to the availability of the necessary financial resources within the 2025-26 financial year

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14633/22-27, to detail the pre-works engagement he intends to undertake with local (i) residents; and (ii) businesses in relation to the resurfacing scheme on the A24 Ormeau Road and Saintfield Road.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of effective engagement with local communities to ensure that any planned works are carried out with minimal disruption and maximum cooperation. Prior to the commencement of the resurfacing scheme, my officials will provide advance notification to local residents and businesses including information on the nature and timing of the works, any potential traffic management arrangements, and alternative routes, where applicable.Engagement will take the form of written notifications, local media announcements, and updates on associated online platforms. Where necessary, direct consultation with key stakeholders, including local businesses and residents’ groups, will be undertaken to address specific concerns or requirements.My Department is committed to maintaining open lines of communication throughout the planning and delivery phases of this scheme to ensure that all stakeholders are informed and feedback is considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will bring proposals for minimum unit pricing for alcohol to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: It is my intention to bring the proposals for Minimum Unit Pricing to the Executive in the very near future. Subject to Executive agreement, I aim to progress the development of the required legislation and to bring to the Assembly within the current mandate. Given the competing primary legislation demands on Assembly time, I anticipate this will be considered by the Assembly in the final year of the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he expects to bring legislation to the Assembly in relation to minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: It is my intention to bring the proposals for Minimum Unit Pricing to the Executive in the very near future. Subject to Executive agreement, I aim to progress the development of the required legislation and to bring to the Assembly within the current mandate. Given the competing primary legislation demands on Assembly time, I anticipate this will be considered by the Assembly in the final year of the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14633/22-27, to detail the timeline for delivery of the resurfacing scheme on the A24 Ormeau Road and Saintfield Road.
answer_text: The A24 Ormeau Road/Saintfield Road scheme is currently placed highly on my Department’s priority list for resurfacing and it is currently anticipated that it will be delivered in the 2025-26 financial year, subject to the availability of the necessary financial resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail what consideration they have given to establish the High Street Taskforce on a permanent basis.
answer_text: On October 10th, we wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance and Infrastructure asking them to consider the recommendations of the High Street Task Force report and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to recommendation five of the Delivering a 21st Century High Street report, to detail any ongoing work to develop and implement an investment programme for blue-green infrastructure.
answer_text: The new Transport Strategy along with a suite of Transport Plans, currently in preparation, will set a framework for making transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035. Active Travel and Placemaking will play a key role within these plans which aspire to better connect neighbouring communities to towns and city centres by facilitating healthy and sustainable travel choices and minimising the use of the private car.The Living With Water in Belfast Plan includes proposals for investment in blue/green infrastructure including Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS) and Natural Flood Management (NFM). My Department will also be consulting on a draft Living With Water in Derry Plan. These Plans are primarily to help address flood risk, drainage and environmental issues and their geographical scope extends beyond the urban centres to include residential and outlying areas. Going forward consideration will also be given to nature-based solutions for improved water management in urban and other road drainage.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to recommendation seven of the Delivering a 21st Century High Street report, to outline what consideration has been given to appointing a lead Minister to champion Smart High Streets.
answer_text: The scope of the 14 recommendations span the responsibilities of a number of departments. On October 10th, we wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance and Infrastructure asking them to consider the recommendations and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to the High Street and Town Centre Rejuvenation motion which was agreed, as amended, on 4th November 2024, what consideration he has given to bringing forward a comprehensive strategy for the rejuvenation of our high streets.
answer_text: I am committed to cooperating with my Executive colleagues to tackle the issues facing high streets and town centres.My department will also be working with councils through local economic partnerships to identify and address specific regional economic issues. This approach leverages the particular insights and expertise provided by our councils, who are best positioned to offer informed perspectives on regional challenges and potential solutions for local economic growth, including on our high streets.		Finally, I have identified High Street Task Force Recommendation 8: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’ as sitting within the direct remit of my department. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my priority on Good Jobs, and my department is already actively working on initiatives that will support its implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 17051/22-27, to clarify whether he has considered the development of an artificial intelligence literacy strategy for schools.
answer_text: The Department has not considered developing an individual strategy around building pupil competence and knowledge of Artificial Intelligence (AI).  As announced in my statement in response to the recommendations from the Independent Review of Education, I have commissioned an independent review of the curriculum and I intend to develop a new strategy on literacy and numeracy as a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to recommendation two of the Delivering a 21st Century High Street report, to detail what engagement he has had with local councils to implement a Living High Streets approach to high street regeneration, working in partnership with local communities to design the vibrant spaces that they need.
answer_text: The Ministerial Advisory Group (MAG) for Architecture and the Built Environment led on the development of the Living High Streets approach and both NILGA and SOLACE took part in this.To assist the use of the craft kit, MAG published a complementary starter pack in February 2024, which includes additional guidance, presentation slides and guidance notes for use in workshops. A training session was held on April 2024 to train placemaking facilitators to guide the process in different places and help deliver using the Living High Streets Craft Kit locally. Representatives from councils were invited and several attended this session.The first pilot, in Downpatrick, was conducted between November 2022 and September 2023 with the close involvement of the local council and resulted in the launch of the Downpatrick Living High Streets Framework.DfC is also working with Belfast City Council, the Greater Shankill Partnership, BUILD, other key statutory organisations and stakeholders in the Greater Shankill area to take forward a further pilot of the Craft Kit to demonstrate its use in a setting such as an arterial route into Belfast. MAG facilitated a workshop in September and provided a training session for all those involved in October.MAG are also planning a further training session for placemaking facilitators in January 2025 in response to a request from Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council.  This is to ensure that councils are upskilled in the delivery of the Living High Streets Craft Kit. It is hoped that other Councils who haven’t previously participated in the training, will also take part.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 9863/22-27, to provide an update on his departmental review of Further Education student support.
answer_text: The departmental review of Further Education student support is ongoing.  Further to the recommendations made in the report commissioned from the Strategic Investment Board, my officials are liaising with the Departmental Solicitors Office for advice to ensure that were these recommendations  to be taken forward, they would be legally compliant and in accordance with current legislative provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the establishment of the Chemicals Stakeholder Forum.
answer_text: My officials are carrying out a scoping exercise to review the viability of establishing a North of Ireland Chemicals Stakeholder Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14473/22-27 in relation to correspondence from the First Minister and deputy First Minister on 10 October 2024, to detail any actions she has taken in relation to the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce and determining a way forward.
answer_text: My Department was responsible for the rating elements within the report and has undertaken a number of actions in response to the recommendations, including extending the Small Business Rate Relief scheme for 2024/25, a measure which now provides support to some 30,000 businesses here and the reinstatement of the Back in Business scheme to incentivise the use of long-term vacant retail space by helping new ventures across our councils in their vital first years in business. The High Street Task Force was a cross-cutting initiative, however, and business rates formed just one aspect within a much wider set of recommendations across Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 14473/22-27 in relation to correspondence from the First Minister and deputy First Minister on 10 October 2024, to detail any actions he has taken in relation to the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce and determining a way forward.
answer_text: I have considered the report of the High Street Task Force and have identified Recommendation 8: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’ as sitting within the remit of my department.		Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my priority on Good Jobs. Ongoing work within my department on the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter will serve to support implementation of the recommendation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to their responsibilities for integration, to detail the support they are putting in place for relevant people in Northern Ireland who will be moved from physical residence permits to an eVisa system under new Home Office rules.
answer_text: We are aware that Home Office has funded organisations here to provide free help and information to vulnerable people who need to create a UK Visas and Immigration (UKVI) account and access their e-Visa. Officials have already engaged with Advice NI, the initial organisation funded by Home Office, to understand how it will assist refugees to access their e-Visa.  Following a further announcement on 18 October 2024 of two additional organisations to be funded by Home Office here, officials will also now engage with North Down YMCA and South Tyrone Empowerment Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14473/22-27 in relation to correspondence from the First Minister and deputy First Minister on 10 October 2024, to detail any actions he has taken in relation to the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce and determining a way forward.
answer_text: I can advise that I responded to the First Minister and deputy First Minister on the 23 October and indicated that I would be happy for my Department to be represented on any future cross-departmental group and to explore the opportunities for progressing the recommendations contained in the Task Force Report. I have, therefore, nominated my Department’s Chief Planner and Director of Regional Planning Policy and Casework, to represent the Department. You will be aware, however, that my Department continues to face ongoing pressures on budgets and resources, and the difficult reality is that this has an impact on my ability to take on new and drive forward every workstream I would like to. Certain outcomes may only be achievable over a longer period than I would want.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14473/22-27 in relation to correspondence from the First Minister and deputy First Minister on 10 October 2024, to detail any actions he has taken in relation to the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce and determining a way forward.
answer_text: The High Street Taskforce Report contains 14 recommendations, with short, medium and longer-term time frames. Several of the recommendations have a pragmatic focus on existing delivery mechanisms, whilst others seek fundamental reform of key policies such as rating and planning or call for significant additional investment.Of the 14 recommendations in the report, three could be identified where DfC would be seen as a significant lead organisation.  These are Recommendations 2, 5 and 12.  Both 5 and 12 require Executive endorsement and in the case of Recommendation 5, a significant Capital Budget to allow for implementation.Recommendation 2 DfC undertook to carry out up to three Pilots of the “Living High Streets Craft Kit”.  Due to limited resources only one pilot has been completed to date.  This pilot was completed in Downpatrick between November 2022 to September 2023 and resulted in the launch of the Downpatrick Living High Streets Framework.DfC is also working with the Greater Shankill Partnership, BUILD, other key statutory organisations and stakeholders in the Greater Shankill area to take forward a further pilot of the Craft Kit to demonstrate its use in a setting such as an arterial route into Belfast.Recommendation 5 An Outline Business Case for a Shaping Sustainable Places (SSP) Programme is currently in development by DfC in partnership with DAERA and DfI. The vision is to implement a strategic and integrated approach to tackling the challenges facing our village, town and city centres. The proposals will be consulted upon in early 2025 and the overall Programme will require Executive approval.Recommendation 12 SOLACE is currently engaged in work to set out the local government position on this matter and will report when this is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the tendering process used to appoint providers to deliver English for speakers of other languages courses.
answer_text: English for Speakers of Other Languages courses delivery is primarily provided by the Further Education colleges, and as Non-Departmental Public Bodies, Further Education colleges follow Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy for tendering processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of the Careers Portal.
answer_text: I refer the member to previous answer given in AQW 16808/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any additional support he can provide to further education colleges to deliver additional English for speakers of other languages courses.
answer_text: My Department currently provides £1.7 million per annum to the Further Education colleges for the provision of English for Speakers of Other Languages.£0.4 million additional support to this annualised provision has been allocated to Further Education colleges within this financial year through successful bids made by my Department to the Home Office and Ministry of Housing Communities and Local Government under the Full Dispersal Fund, the Homes for Ukraine Fund and the Refugee Integration and Employability Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration he has given to Recommendation 3 of the Independent Review of Education to give everybody the opportunity to access basic education throughout their lifetime free of charge.
answer_text: The recommendations from the Independent Review of Education are still under consideration. I am looking forward to the Education Minister bringing forward a paper to the Executive in relation to the review and I look forward to further engagement on this.Currently, Further Education Colleges provide Essential Skills courses, in numeracy, literacy and information and communication technology, free to to those engaged in education, training or employment as well as those adults wishing to access education to improve their life skills.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration he has given to developing a single college governance model with fully integrated shared services but operating via local campuses to replace the current regional colleges, as recommended by the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: The recommendations from the Independent Review of Education are still under consideration. I am looking forward to the Education Minister bringing forward a paper to the Executive in relation to the review and I look forward to further engagement on this.	My officials have commenced work on a co-designed Further Education Tranformation programme, and whilst developing a single college governance model is not one of the 25 key recommendations, all recommendations will be considered in conjuction with that work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration he has given to recommendation 22 of the Independent Review of Education to promote a cost effective, thriving and value for money college sector.
answer_text: The recommendations from the Independent Review of Education are still under consideration. I am looking forward to the Education Minister bringing forward a paper to the Executive in relation to the review and I look forward to further engagement on this.	My officials have commenced work on a co-designed Further Education Tranformation programme and recommendation 22 will be considered in conjunction with this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the work of DfI Roads to develop a plan for road access at the proposed new build for Forge Integrated Primary School; and (ii) to detail a timeline for resolving these issues.
answer_text: I can advise that my department has been consulted on a Pre-Application Discussion (PAD) with proposals to develop the site of the former Knockbreda High School.  My officials have given full consideration to all the options presented, including any potential effect on road safety and traffic progression, and have provided without prejudice, advice and recommendations to the planning authority.  My department has engaged positively with the Department for Education during the PAD process and welcomes the development of the site.At this time, the Department has not been consulted upon an any further proposals through the Planning Authority. However, officials will continue to assess any design proposals and provide advice and recommendations on road safety and traffic progression, as required, as part of the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will publish the Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14 to 19 Education and Training - a Framework to Transform 14 to 19 Education and Training Provision Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department is jointly responsible for delivery of the 14-19 Education and Training Framework along with the Department of Education. The Framework was published in 2022. At the time, officials developed an underpinning draft action plan to assist with implementation and resource allocation. The draft action plan was not intended for publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons for the delay in finding a solution to road access issues for the proposed new build for Forge Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: My Department has engaged in ongoing discussions regarding this matter as part of the Department’s statutory consultee role since July 2020.It is acknowledged that Departmental staffing resource pressures may have contributed to some delay in providing a timely response in particular when a change in staffing between January 2021 and July 2022 resulted in a delayed response.  However, the main concern for my Department is the proposed access from the development onto the A55, which is a ‘protected route’.My Department has engaged positively with the Department for Education, identifying and discussing the Department’s concerns since 2020. My officials have given full consideration to all the options presented and considered any potential effect on road safety and traffic progression, and responded without prejudice, with advice and recommendations to the Planning Authority throughout the statutory consultation process.  To-date the developer has not yet provided a proposal which appropriately addresses road safety and traffic progression concerns associated with the proposed development.My Department will continue to assess any design proposals that we are consulted on, providing the Planning Authority with advice and recommendations on road safety and traffic progression with our primary focus to ensure the safety of all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether salt boxes can be provided for roads that have not yet been formally adopted by his Department.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,600 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met. Salt bins are not provided by my department on private and unadopted roads, where responsibility falls to the landowner or developer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered the development of an artificial intelligence literacy strategy for schools.
answer_text: I have committed to bring forward a new literacy and numeracy strategy.  I will consider a range of options as the strategy is developed in order to ensure that our teachers are supported and guided on best practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any progress that has been made in relation to the creation of an independent workforce development agency for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are no current plans to develop a bespoke workforce development agency for the North of Ireland.  However, I will continue to keep current arrangements under review to ensure we have structures that help develop the skills we need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any engagement he has had with the UK Government in relation to the abolition of the two-year qualifying period for unfair dismissal as part of the proposed employment rights Bill; and (ii) whether he is minded to reduce or remove the qualifying period within Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I will be scheduling a meeting with my Ministerial counterpart in the Department of Business and Trade to discuss all aspects of the British Government’s Employment Rights Bill. My officials regularly engage with their British counterparts on all matters relating to employment law.The qualifying period for protection against unfair dismissal in the North of Ireland is one year, by virtue of the Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order (Northern Ireland) 1999, not two years as in Britain. Whilst the British Government’s Employment Rights Bill seeks to remove the two-year qualifying period for unfair dismissal, it also plans to consult on introducing a statutory nine month probation period for workers. My position will be set out as I take forward the Employment Rights Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 14548/22-27, how the additional needs of young carers are being incorporated into the agenda and forward work plan of the cross-departmental working group on issues impacting carers, including access to (i) school; (ii) further education; (iii) training; and (iv) employment.
answer_text: My Department is represented on the Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group for Carers by officials within the skills and education policy remit, in recognition of the need for young carers to be supported through their journey through education, training and employment.  Access to Further Education is key for addressing equality of opportunity and creating pathways to good jobs. This is particularly important for those who experience additional barriers to participation, such as people with caring responsibilities. To that end, a range of financial support is currently available including FE Grants, Hardship Fund, Care to Learn (NI) childcare scheme and the Additional Support Fund.  Part-time delivery models are also currently being piloted for certain Assured Skills Academies to support those who have other commitments such as caring or health conditions/disability. In addition to this a recent consultation regarding policy proposals to support unpaid carers in employment, including young carers closed on 30 September 2024. Analysis of consultation responses, engagement with CarersNI and the cross-departmental working group is ongoing to inform policy options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to improve road surfaces in the Finaghy area of South Belfast.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all roads in a reasonable condition.Whilst there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year are included in the Department’s Annual Report to Belfast City Council which will be presented to the Council in the coming weeks.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in the Finaghy area in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14548/22-27, how the voices and experiences of young carers and young adult carers are being included in the cross-departmental working group on issues impacting carers.
answer_text: The Department of Economy have transferred this question to my Department for response as the Department of Health  currently chairs  the recently established Cross Departmental Senior Official Group for unpaid carers. I am grateful for and value the collaboration with senior officials from a range of other Departments in the recent formation of this group.  Many issues faced by carers are cross cutting and interconnected, this includes issues such as wellbeing, financial hardship, housing, isolation, participation in education, employment and leisure activities. Carers, including young carers and young adult carers, often face daily challenges associated with the responsibilities of caring and the impact of caring on their own lives. As part of my Department’s Social Care Collaborative Forum a Supporting Carers Workstream has been established. This workstream is  jointly chaired by my Department and Carers NI. Barnardo’s and Action for Children are core members, providing valued representation about the needs of young carers and young adult carers. Earlier this year a series of regional engagement events with carers were held, including two dedicated events with young carers and young adult carers. These events provided a welcome forum for the workstream to directly engage with young carers and young adult carers.The Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group for unpaid carers has been formed as an outcome  of the work of the Supporting Carers Workstream. I welcome that Carers NI  have agreed to act in an advisory role to this group and will attend meetings by invitation. This will provide a conduit for the views and experiences of carers. In addition, other groups and carer representatives may also be invited to attend or provide input,  which offers further opportunities for the inclusion of the voices of young carers and young adult carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to reduce traffic congestion at the Finaghy crossroads in South Belfast given the road safety concerns.
answer_text: The current traffic signal sequencing at Finaghy crossroads is complex, with up to 6 traffic movements and 4 pedestrian movements that need to be controlled efficiently but more importantly, safely, within the limited road space available.The Department has introduced advanced dynamic traffic signal control called MOVA (Microprocessor Optimised Vehicle Actuation).  MOVA gathers flow information, on each movement, from detector loops buried in the carriageway surface and amends signal timing according to the prevailing flow.The benefits of MOVA control vary from site-to-site but delay savings are typically between 10-20%. I can also advise that an investigation of the history of personal injury collisions currently available to the Department has not highlighted a particular issue at the junction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action he will take to improve the cycling infrastructure in the Finaghy area of South Belfast.
answer_text: Cycling infrastructure improvements in Belfast are being delivered in accordance with the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan. My Department is currently developing proposals in the Finaghy area as part of the West Belfast Greenway Phase 2. When complete, this project will connect Blacks Road, Finaghy Road North, and Kennedy Way. Preliminary design is in the early stages and public engagement will be arranged when the proposals have been suitably developed.West Belfast Greenway Phase 1 will connect Kennedy Way to the existing Forth Meadow Greenway and the existing facilities along the Westlink to provide a connection to Belfast City Centre. Detailed design proposals are being developed for that scheme with a view to delivery by the end of 2025, subject to satisfactory completion of the planning process and availability of future funding. Delivery of West Belfast Greenway Phase 2 will follow completion of Phase 1.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14548/22-27, to detail the membership of the cross-departmental working group on issues impacting carers.
answer_text: My Department established a cross departmental senior officials group to provide an opportunity to bring together departments, other stakeholders, and carers to build recognition and strengthen cross government approaches to carers issues. Membership of the Group comprises of relevant NICS Departments in relation to their remit pertaining to carers issues.The current membership of the group is:RepresentativeTitle/RoleDepartmentMark McGuicken (Chair)Director of Disability and Older PeopleDepartment of HealthTony SimpsonDirector of Strategic Policy and ReformDepartment of FinanceRicky IrwinEquality Rights and Identity DivisionThe Executive OfficeDavid TarrActing Director for Social Security, Policy, Legislation and Decision-Making ServicesDepartment              for CommunitiesClaire McClellandDirector of Raising Aspirations, Supporting Learning, and Empowering ImprovementDepartment of EducationMichael LeonardHead of Additional Educational Needs TeamDepartment of EducationGraeme WilkinsonDirector of SkillsDepartment for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of sewage on water quality.
answer_text: I am very aware of the impact of sewage pollution on water quality. The Northern Ireland Environment Agency undertakes an extensive water quality monitoring programme within Northern Ireland’s rivers and lakes. NIEA’s river water body monitoring, indicates that 51% of river water bodies are impacted by agriculture, 26 % by sewage related pressures and a further 22 % impacted by both by sewage pressures and agricultural land use. Research led by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute was published in October 2020. This research examined substance flow analysis for phosphorus in the Northern Ireland’s food system. Of all the phosphorus lost to water, and estimated 36% was due to sewage, of which 24 % was attributed to wastewater and a further 12 % to septic tanks. I am committed to working with the DfI Minister and with Executive colleagues to address the funding constraints that are having a significant impact on NI Water’s ability to invest and upgrade its wastewater infrastructure. The recently approved Environmental Improvement Plan sets out my commitment to improving water quality in Northern Ireland, including the requirement to publish the third cycle River Basin Management Plan and associated Programme of Measures. Work to improve water quality will require collective action across government, the public and private sectors and wider society.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work of the Careers Portal project.
answer_text: The development of a new Careers Portal is an ambitious and complex IT enabled system transformation project. Successful delivery will help every part of the careers system to work together and improve access to high quality careers information and services.A small project team was established in December 2023 with an initial focus on research and engagement to identify priority needs of the wide range of end users.The engagement work to date has included:Establishing a network with colleagues in the English, Irish, Scottish and Welsh governments who have worked on similar projects.Focus groups with careers advisers, careers educators and young people in the 14-19 age bracket from a range of educational backgrounds and geographical locations.11 interactive workshops in nine different locations with the Careers Service including careers advisers, operations managers, support staff and policy staff. In-depth interviews with a range of adults at different stages of their career journeys as well as parents/carers, including special educational needs representation. A cross-cutting in-person stakeholder event, facilitated by Dr Deirdre Hughes, which was attended by 31 key stakeholders representing education, government, business and the voluntary and community sector.An online Citizen Space survey which received 1,229 responses from a diverse range of users and stakeholders.The project has now moved into a Discovery phase, bringing the more empathetic user-focused engagement and research together with IT specialists to identify the best technical solutions. In conjunction with developers, 16 workshops are scheduled to take place over the next six weeks. These sessions will be attended by representatives of multiple groups including young people, teachers, careers professionals, parents and the voluntary and community sector, each representing a different user persona on a career journey. This co-design approach will be key to ensuring that the product taken through to development after the Discovery phase best meets the needs of users and stakeholders. Once the product is fully defined, it is anticipated that it will take between 18 and 24 months to deliver the project, subject to the availability of resources. This is in line with other capital projects of this scale and complexity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the importance placed on diaspora engagement by Invest NI, to detail the engagement he has had with the First Minister and deputy First Minister in relation to the development of a diaspora strategy as part of the development of an updated Executive international relations strategy.
answer_text: On my recent international trips to the United States, Canada and Germany, I have seen at firsthand the passion, commitment and energy of our diaspora community. It is important that we harness that energy in a focussed way aligned with our strategic economic agenda.   The establishment of both Net Zero and Life and Health Sciences Advisory Boards in recent months is an excellent example of how we are working through Invest NI and its NI Connections programme to tap into that pool of expertise.The role of diaspora engagement to boost trade and investment is recognised in Invest NI’s 2024-2027 Business Strategy, which is currently out for consultation. I and my officials will continue to engage with TEO and others to tell the positive story of our diaspora and the role it can play in driving forward our economic agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16219/22-27, why the running costs of Invest NI offices in GB and Europe increased by over £800,000 between the 2022-23 and 2023-24 financial years.
answer_text: Following the ease of Covid restrictions, eight vacancies in the British and Europe team were filled and their employment commenced during the first few months of the 2023-24 financial year. In addition, five vacancies were filled during the second half of the 2022-23 financial year, they were in post the entire 2023-24 financial year and therefore, the expenditure reported for 2023-24 includes their annual salary costs in full. As a result of an increase in headcount, office overheads incurred in the 2023-24 financial year were also higher. The above were the main reasons leading to an increase in the running costs of Invest NI offices in Britain and Europe between the 2022-23 and 2023-24 financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16219/22-27, why the running costs of Invest NI in the (i) Asia Pacific; and (ii) India, Middle East and Africa regions declined between the 2022-23 and 2023-24 financial years.
