answer_text: The pricing of tickets is an operational matter for Translink. The Enterprise service is operated as a joint venture by Irish Rail and Translink. Each company has its own ticketing, systems, fare structures and special offers. In April 2022, the Irish government discounted public transport fares by 20% across the board, which includes Enterprise tickets for journeys originating south of the border.  Translink have confirmed that they are carrying out a review of prices with Irish Rail. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. Consequently, a similar discount is not available for Enterprise tickets for journeys originating in the North. The value of our public transport services cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the childcare subsidy scheme will still be ready for implementation from September 2024.
answer_text: As stated in my Ministerial Statement on 23rd May, while it will be challenging, my aim is to have the childcare subsidy scheme operational for September.  My officials remain committed to working to this timescale.The Executive has agreed that the focus over the coming months should be on implementation of the early learning and childcare measures announced for 2024/25. Evaluation of these measures and analysis of the data due to be collected from parents and providers in 2024/25 will inform the development of the longer-term Strategy and a feasible timeline to deliver it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department will publish a Frequently Asked Questions update to detail how the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will work.
answer_text: A Frequently Asked Questions for the package of measures announced for an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy in 2024/25 financial year are currently being finalised and will be published as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional support he can provide to the Child and Adolescent Autism Service to ensure (i) waiting lists are reduced; and (ii) children and young people can receive a more timely diagnosis.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. As you will be aware the Health Budget is under considerable pressure. In order to support HSC Trusts in the current fiscal challenges officials in my department are currently reviewing the paediatric ASD pathway. Officials meet with Trusts on a monthly basis as part of this review and to inform the development of assessment pathways that are consistent and equally accessible across the region. The outcome of the review will be to provide the most effective care pathway to meet the needs of children and young people in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people currently on the Child and Adolescent Autism Service waiting list; and (ii) the average waiting time for those who have been referred to the service.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider introducing a scheme to provide baby boxes to every baby born in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the aim of the baby box scheme is to reduce inequality and give every baby the best start in life. However, in 2018, when the baby box was being introduced to the UK, concerns were raised by medical professionals and cot death charities about the lack of evaluation around whether baby boxes do actually reduce the risk of infant death, compared to using a crib or a Moses basket.In light of this, I am not intending to introduce a baby box scheme at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assurances he can provide to parents and pupils that the Deanby Centre will be operational by September 2024.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is undertaking a programme of works in July and August at the site of the former Our Lady’s Primary School.  Works include refurbishment of eight classrooms and corridors, the creation of sensory, therapy and hygiene rooms and external safeguarding works. The recruitment process for all staff required for the Deanby Centre Special School for September 2024 is underway in line with the EA’s recruitment framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the tendering and procurement processes in relation to the provision of taxis for pupils with a statement of special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, including the procurement of taxis for pupils with special educational needs. The EA is a Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) and it is responsible for its own tendering processes. It would therefore be for the EA to determine if a review of the tendering and procurement processes in relation to taxis for pupils with a statement of special educational needs is necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on when the final version of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review will be published.
answer_text: Since the public consultation closed in September last year work has been ongoing to consider the outcomes of the consultation and finalise the Review. 	I expect to receive the final Review for my approval in the coming weeks, after which, the Review will be shared with my Executive colleagues.	My officials are currently engaging with their colleagues in the Department of Transport regarding the processes and timeframes for agreement of the Review by the Irish Government and to agree a date for its publication. 	It is anticipated the final Review will be published in the summer.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when their Department will appoint a Permanent Secretary.
answer_text: The role of the Permanent Secretary at the Executive Office is being filled on an interim basis by David Malcolm, following his success in a NICS-wide temporary promotion exercise open to all substantive NICS Grade 3s. David took up the post on 17 June 2024, and joins TEO from his former position as a Deputy Secretary with the Department for the Economy. This temporary promotion arrangement is expected to last for six to twelve months.  During this time a review of the most recent substantive Permanent Secretary recruitment competition will be undertaken, and a new recruitment competition to fill the TEO Permanent Secretary vacancy on a substantive basis will be progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the consideration he has given to the Lived Experience Influencing Change report published by SEN Reform NI in June 2024.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to consider the report published by SEN Reform NI within the context of the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when Good Relations funding will be distributed.
answer_text: Allocations across Good Relations Funding Programmes have now been finalised. All organisations have been notified of the outcome of their applications and the distribution of funding is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the safeguards that will be put in place to ensure that savings from the childcare subsidy scheme will be passed on to parents.
answer_text: Work to confirm the operational detail of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme is ongoing.  ‘Frequently Asked Questions’ (FAQs) will be made available in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the administration process; and (ii) the costs expected to be borne by childcare providers to administer the childcare subsidy scheme.
answer_text: Work to confirm the operational detail of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme is ongoing.  ‘Frequently Asked Questions’ (FAQs) will be made available in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to reform the school curriculum.
answer_text: I intend to commission a review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum to ensure we have a curriculum which is based on international best practice and is proven to reduce inequality in education and schools. That review will inform options for curriculum reform including how it is monitored, reviewed, and developed on an ongoing basis to ensure that it meets and exceeds the learning needs of our children and young people.  Further details will be provided in September.In parallel, my officials, together with their counterparts in the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment, will continue to engage with schools to ensure there is a joined-up approach to how the curriculum is delivered.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 5569/22-27, for an update on work being undertaken to develop a new Racial Equality Strategy.
answer_text: Officials are preparing a submission, for our consideration, of the final report of the independent review of progress on the implementation of the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025, which will support our deliberation on the strategic approach to racial equality post 2025.It is our intention that further engagement and close collaboration with the wider minority ethnic sector will inform this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to reform Home to School transport.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 11330/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 17 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what plans his Department has to better understand the causes of anxiety among children in pre-school settings; and (ii) what interventions he can take forward to support those young children who are experiencing anxiety.
answer_text: Early Years foundations are set through early years programmes such as the Infant Mental Health Framework for NI; the ‘Getting Ready’ Suite of Programmes; Sure Start; the Pre-school education programme and Healthy Child, Healthy Future (HCHF). A Refresh of the DoH Healthy Child Healthy Future framework is currently underway with my officials representing the Department on the Programme Board in addition to being a member of the Healthy Child Healthy Future Pre-School Group which are both helping to shape the future of the framework for the universal Child Health Promotion Programme in Northern Ireland.In relation to the Children & Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework (the Framework), while mainly focused on children and young people in primary and post primary schools, the underlying principles equally apply to early years settings.  In fact the Framework takes a lead from the cross government policies for early years which prepare children for life, support them to gain a desire for learning and to be ready for school, and builds upon these at each stage of a child’s journey throughout education.  My department also funds the Getting Ready to Learn (GRtL) programme which is available to all funded pre-school settings.  This includes a ‘Happy Healthy Kids’ theme designed to support the physical and emotional development of pre-school age children.  Parents are also provided with material to help them build on this learning at home.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to improve the support, financial or otherwise, provided to those in the Employment and Support Allowance support group.
answer_text: All Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) claimants undergo a Work Capability Assessment to determine their capability for work. Claimants with an illness or disability that severely restricts their ability to work will be placed in the Support Group. Those placed within the Support Group receive an increased rate of ESA. While claimants in the Support Group are not asked to take part in any work-related activity as a condition of receiving benefit, they do have access to Work Coach support and all of the services available at their local Jobs and Benefits Office. This includes information and advice on training/employment opportunities and employment programmes available to help manage an illness and disability at work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the findings of the On A Mission: Religious Practices in NI Primary Schools report published by Parents for Inclusive Education NI.
answer_text: I have noted the report from Parents for Inclusive Education Northern Ireland and my officials will issue a response in due course.  I visit many schools as it is important, as the Minister of Education, to engage with our school leaders and teachers.  Schools, in turn, should engage with their community as appropriate when inviting external visitors to engage with pupils.  I find schools to be a welcoming and inclusive environment for all.  It is important that we continue to give schools the flexibility to decide their own approach with regard to the delivery of the curriculum including Religious Education.  I do believe however, that Christianity, like all faiths, has a significant role to play in the ethos of our schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Hidden Homelessness report published by the Simon Community NI.
answer_text: I noted with interest the research published by the Simon Community.  I am confident that such research will inform future work in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when a decision will be made about the development proposal submitted by Cairnshill Primary School to transform to Controlled Integrated Status.
answer_text: I approved Development Proposal 719 ‘Cairnshill Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status, with effect from 1 September 2024, or as soon as possible thereafter.’ on 10 June 2024.  My decision and the evidence on which it is based is published on the Department’s website at: Development Proposal Decisions 2024 / Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what guarantees he can provide that funding for Windsor Women's Centre will be extended beyond June 2024; and (ii) when a decision on this funding allocation will be communicated to recipients.
answer_text: Now that my department’s budget allocation is known, I have tasked my officials to work at pace to ensure that all funded community groups are updated as soon as possible on their indicative grant funding allocations for the 2024/25 year.  These allocations remain indicative as they are subject to the outcome of the Department’s budget consultation exercise which launched today.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the guarantees he can provide that the childcare subsidy scheme will be operational by September 2024.
answer_text: As stated in my Ministerial Statement on 23rd May, while it will be challenging, my aim is to have the childcare subsidy scheme operational for September.  My officials are working to this timescale.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to address the increasing number of unregistered childminders as part of the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care.
answer_text: Through the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care, my Department will assess whether the approach to registration and inspection of childminding is proportionate, not unnecessarily bureaucratic nor a barrier to registration. In addition, my Department will continue to raise awareness of the risks of using unregistered childminding and draw attention to the tax and other benefits of using registered childminding.  HSC Trusts will also take action when they receive any reports of unregistered childminding, using their powers under the Children (NI) Order 1995. The developing Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will also assist in tackling the issue of unregistered childminders, by promoting the value of registered childminding, as well as making childcare more affordable and accessible for parents who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7473/22-27, for an update on when this formal consultation will take place.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department hope to consult on the proposals early 2025. This will be subject to staff resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to improve access to NHS dentists in South Belfast.
answer_text: The commissioning of General Dental Services is undertaken on a regional basis, rather than within HSC Trust boundaries.The absence of Executive Ministers had meant that it had not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.  Practices in South Belfast will benefit from this additional investment.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Practices in South Belfast will benefit from all three of these new funding streams.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 8799/22-27; (ii) AQW 9555/22-27; and (iii) AQW 10655/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Finance when she will answer AQW 10465/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 10465/22-27 was answered on 5 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12196/22-27, when he will make a decision on the dissemination of the scoping study report.
answer_text: The report is available on my Department’s website at Final Scoping Study Report | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk). I wrote to MLAs and other stakeholders on 13 June to advise of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the proposed North-South Glider route; and (ii) to detail whether the route will serve Carryduff.
answer_text: The estimated cost of the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route is currently in the range of £142m to £148m. £35m of funding has been secured through Belfast Region City Deal. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues and the Belfast Region City Deal Partners to ensure that the appropriate level of investment is made available to deliver the project.In my previous tenure as Minister for Infrastructure I announced the North to South Belfast Glider route from Carryduff in the south, along the Antrim Road to Glengormley. To ensure the correct decision is made, officials are currently finalising feasibility studies to determine the viability of extending the routes into Glengormley and Carryduff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the consideration his Department has given to the Independent Review of Education; and (ii) for a timeline of when that assessment will be published.
answer_text: Departmental officials have now completed an internal consultation with DE officials, officials in other departments potentially impacted by the recommendations and with NDPB’s and other stakeholders.  A paper is now being produced for my consideration outlining the findings of the internal consultation process. I hope to receive and consider this paper in the near future and will communicate my decisions in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what guarantees he can provide that the Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 will be fully funded.
answer_text: The estimated cost of the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route is currently in the range of £142m to £148m. £35m of funding has been secured through Belfast Region City Deal. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues and the Belfast Region City Deal Partners to ensure that the appropriate level of investment is made available to deliver the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans to improve road safety on Lisburn Road, South Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has recently completed a resurfacing scheme on the Lisburn Road between Balmoral Ave and Cranmore Gardens which included the upgrade of three pelican crossings to puffin crossings.While no further works are programmed at this time, the local roads division has preliminary proposals to resurface the section of the Lisburn Road between Balmoral Avenue and Balmoral Golf Club.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards applications submitted in the last twelve months, broken down by (a) Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) whether they relate to children or adults; and (ii) how many applications include a specific reference to the need to use restraint.
answer_text: The number of Deprivation of Liberty Trust Panel applications (in the community) submitted from 1st April 2023 to 31st March 2024 are as follows:TrustTrust Panel DoL ApplicationsNumber of which were AdultsNumber of which were Children (16-17 year olds)Belfast7277189Northern47446113South Eastern5195136Southern20719611Western4234176Total2350230545The number of Short-Term Detentions (in hospitals) from 1st April 2023 to 31st March 2024 are as follows:TrustShort Term Detention AuthorisationsNumber of which were AdultsNumber of which were Children (16-17 year olds)Belfast5045040Northern2922920South Eastern2472470Southern1631630Western1261260Total133213320The Health and Social Care Trusts do not record data in relation to cases where there is specific reference to the need to use restraint.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the recently announced subsidy scheme for childcare support will be applicable to families that use multiple providers for different children.
answer_text: The subsidy will be paid directly to registered childcare providers to reduce childcare bills for all eligible parents using their provision. Parents can use more than one provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans to upgrade and resurface footways on Locksley Park, South Belfast.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations, my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including resurfacing works within the South Belfast constituency.	Details of these plans will be presented in the annual report to Belfast City Council, scheduled for Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what action he is taking to increase the number of children who walk or cycle to school.
answer_text: My Department regularly reviews statistics in relation to children’s travel methods to and from school. The latest results for 2022-23 found that 31% of primary school children and 21% of post-primary school children regularly walk or cycle to school and that more than half live close enough to their school for walking or cycling to be a viable option.Provision of high-quality infrastructure is one of the main things my Department can do to encourage walking, wheeling, and cycling. I am committed to increasing investment in active travel to enable more people to make that choice for many of their everyday journeys, including to and from school. I can confirm that routes to schools are considered in the active travel network plans currently being developed for our largest towns and cities.My Department also works in partnership with schools on the delivery of a number of road safety and active travel programmes, such as child pedestrian road safety training and the Cycling Proficiency Scheme. My Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) jointly fund the Active School Travel programme which is delivered by Sustrans. This programme also provides our young people with the necessary skills and confidence to feel safe when making active travel choices.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what plans they have to widen the scope of the Central Good Relations Fund.
To ask the Minister of Education whether the needs of deaf children have been taken into account as part of the proposed package of interim measures for early learning and childcare.
answer_text: I would refer the member to the answer to AQW 12291/22-27 which provides the current position. The answer is contained in the Weekly Answers Booklet for week ending 31 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans for supporting children with special educational needs and disabilities and their families through the proposed package of interim measures for early learning and childcare.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement of 23 May 2024 sets out my plans for early learning and childcare measures during 2024/25. These include support for children with	special educational needs and disabilities, the detail of which will be informed by further stakeholder engagement and communicated in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether developers can begin construction work, at their own risk, before they have been granted plan approval from Building Control.
answer_text: The Building Regulations do not prevent developers from commencing construction work at their own risk prior to their plans being passed by the district Council.  Failure to provide notification to the district Council prior to commencement of work or to submit a plans application where required, however, would be a contravention of the Building Regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether people in receipt of childcare vouchers will be eligible to access support from the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: I advised in my statement of 23 May that I intend to have the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme in place for September 2024.  Work to confirm the operational detail is ongoing, including through engagement with stakeholders, and further information will be communicated in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, to detail the system that will be used by childcare providers to access emergency support.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 12512/22-27, which provides the current position.The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 31st May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will operate in practice.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 12512/22-27, which provides the current position.The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 31st May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the work his Department has completed in relation to the standardisation of pre-school since the announcement was made by Minister McIlveen in September 2022.
answer_text: I would refer you to my answer to AQW 11153/22-27 which provided this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on what engagement she has had with UK Treasury on making improvements to the tax-free childcare scheme.
To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact of the free hours model of childcare support in England on parents of multiples.
answer_text: The impact of childcare support available in England on parents of multiples is not an issue on which I have made an assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to ensuring parents of multiples are fully supported as part of the upcoming Early Learning and Childcare strategy, considering the extra financial burden they face.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 set out my plans for early learning and childcare support measures during 2024/25.  Work to confirm the operational detail is ongoing and this will be communicated in due course.  In terms of the Childcare Subsidy Scheme, all eligible children will be able to benefit from the discount proposed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the processes in place to communicate with parents and carers upon the closure of a childcare or early years setting.
answer_text: Where a pre-school provider participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) is withdrawn from the programme the approach taken by the Education Authority (EA) to inform and support parents and carers will vary depending on the circumstances.  Primarily it involves the EA Admissions Service contacting parents by email or telephone to advise of the setting’s withdrawal and make them aware of alternative options.  Where no email or phone number is available, parents will be contacted in writing.   For registered childcare providers, it is the responsibility of the provider to inform parents of their closure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to improve and upgrade the childcare sections of the Family Support NI website.
answer_text: The public register of childcare providers sits within the childcare directory on Family Support NI.Anyone using the childcare search for registered providers, can search by postcode to find childcare nearest them and then filter search results by: -VacanciesExperience of Disability / Complex NeedsSchool Pick-ups/drop offsAccepts Childcare VouchersFlexing Working HoursTax Free ChildcareFSNI also hosts useful related articles for Working Parents including ‘Working Parents – Financial Assistance with Childcare Costs’‘Finding Childcare’‘Benefits of Registered Childcare’The Family Support NI team have run several advertising campaigns (funded by Brightstart) to promote-FamilySupportNI as the public register of childcare providers for Northern Ireland.  Government financial assistance available for working parentsBenefits of using Registered Childcare provision.In 2024 to encourage better engagement from childcare providers on this platform (particularly regarding vacancies) a Self-Service Portal was added to the website.  This was launched in March 2024.This portal enables all registered providers to log into their own account and populate/update services available.At 31st March 2024 we had 3353 Childcare providers on the register.  To date (at 22 May 2024) we have had 428 (13%) log into the Self-Service portal to update their details.  These figures indicate the need for greater engagement from registered childcare providers to update their information to make it more up to date and useful for parents. Future Developments for FSNI: -FSNI are proposing to expand information for parents looking for childcare.  This will include adding fields to the database/website which will provide additional information on: -Detailed costs/rates for childcare providerMore detail for breakfast club/school drop offs and collection i.e. does provider offer breakfast prior to drop off?More detailed vacancy information i.e. Current Vacancies (0-4yrs)?  Current Vacancies (Primary School Aged)?No Current Vacancies but will have vacancies on No Current Vacancies, none for the foreseeableParticular days vacancies are available?  AM or PM?Opening Times / Availability Term time only / school holiday only/all yearParticular days?Care outside typical hours to suit shift workers/unsocial hours (weekends, late evenings, early mornings)Supporting children with disabilities/additional needs - Accessible toilets/ Accessible parking/ Access by ramps/level accessSpecial/Additional Needs provision/experience e.g. Dyspraxia, Autistic spectrum, Asperger’s Syndrome Special Needs training/qualificationsSpecial Dietary Needs (have experience/ provision?)Allergies (have experience/provision?)Sensory Communication training? Sign Language (British/Irish)/ MakatonCultural/Language Support - Other languages spoken/ Irish Medium SettingThe creation of these additional information fields will be dependent on funding being available. it will also require childcare providers to supply the informatio

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11153/22-27 and AQW 10257/22-27, for an update on whether the scoping study has been received by his Department.
answer_text: My Department received a copy of the Scoping Study Report from RSM UK on 20 May 2024.  I will consider the report and make my decision on the dissemination of its contents in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether the £25 million allocated for childcare by the Executive was intended to be spent on standardisation of pre-school; and (ii) and for her assessment of whether pre-school constitutes childcare.
answer_text: As part of the Executive’s Budget 2024-25, it was agreed that £25 million would be held centrally, pending measures being brought forward by the Education Minister, informed by a cross-departmental task and finish group.On 23 May 2024, the Executive agreed a series of measures for early learning and childcare brought forward by the Education Minister to use this £25 million funding. The measures are set out in his written ministerial statement on that date.The Department of Education has lead policy responsibility and, should you require further information on this, you may wish to contact the Education Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there are any plans to carry out an assessment of ventilation and air quality across the school estate.
answer_text: The Department of Education and Education Authority (EA) work within the guidelines provided by the Department of Health concerning air quality and ventilation in schools and other public buildings.  Guidance on ventilation has been issued to all schools, providing advice on how to identify poorly ventilated areas and the measures that should be taken to improve ventilation if required. In line with best practice guidelines, the EA continue to promote and encourage natural ventilation in the first instance to maintain adequate levels of ventilation.  If a school has a specific concern regarding ventilation, they should contact EA to address the matter directly.  The Department does not monitor data on ventilation or air quality levels in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8579/22-27, to detail the engagement he has had with parents and carers to inform his view that time spent in pre-school education has the potential to contribute to parents’ childcare requirements.
answer_text: I have met with parents, providers and key stakeholders in the early learning and childcare sector to hear firsthand the issues and challenges they currently face.  In course of this engagement, parents have raised the childcare benefits of 22.5 hours Pre-school provision over 12.5 hours.  The positive impact of a standardised session length for both parents and children was a feature of the engagement informing the relevant actions and recommendations set out in Learning to Learn, Fair Start, the New Decade New Approach (NDNA) commitment and the Independent Review of Education Report.Parents take a wide range of issues into account when considering childcare arrangements and all funded provision has the potential to contribute to meeting their requirements. This includes attendance at funded educational provision such as school or pre-school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support he plans to provide to childminders and day care settings to support the inclusion of children with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 sets out my plans for 2024/25 support measures.  Implementing these, including those focused on supporting children with special educational needs and disabilities, will be informed by further stakeholder engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has any plans to provide CO2 monitors for classrooms.
answer_text: All new build primary schools and post-primary schools include CO2 monitors since 2011 and 2014, respectively. More than 13,000 CO2 monitors have been distributed across the schools’ estate and the Education Authority (EA) have advised schools that they can request additional monitors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) process; and (ii) criteria used to allocate car parking passes for staff at hospital sites.
answer_text: ProcessThree of the HSCNI Trusts do not operate staff parking permit or application processes; Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT); Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT). These Trusts have free car parking available for staff which operates on a first come, first served basis.  However, they have advised that an application process based on need is under consideration. The remaining three Trusts have an application process in place; Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT); South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT). A summary of the process in place used to allocate car parking spaces is outlined below as advised by Trust:-HSC TrustCurrent Process  1.NHSCT2.NIAS3.WHSCTNo allocation or application process, free car parking provided for all staff on a first come, first served basis.4.SHSCTAn application process in in place based on a first come, first served basis.  A waiting list is held.5.BHSCTA new application process has been introduced on a phased basis using criterion to assess demonstrable need.6.SEHSCTAn application system is in place using criterion to assess demonstrable need.CriteriaSHSCT allocate staff car parking permits on receipt of a written application on a first come, first served basis and formulate a waiting list until spaces are available.SEHSCT has four eligibility criteria as advised and outlined below:-No.Assessment CriteriaExample1Automatic EntitlementBlue Badge holder2Sustainability ConsiderationsDistance from home to base3Conditions of EmploymentUnsocial shift pattern4Personal or Professional Extenuating CircumstancesSection 75 CategoryBHSCT has ten eligibility criteria as advised and outlined below:-No.Assessment Criteria1Disability (holders of a blue badge)* 2Perm. night duty/evening (from 4pm onwards)/w/end working*3Required to use private vehicle on a daily basis to directly deliver patient or client care (i.e. Community Health Visitor/District Nurse/ Midwife/Social Worker)4Carry out shift working as a requirement of post and set rota (scheduled to reg. start before 7:30am or finish after 7:30pm)5Manage Services on more than 1 site (responsible for managing staff on other Trust sites) 6Provide services/clinics on more than 1 site (with a frequency of 1 or more times per month)7A public transport journey that involves one or more changes, and exceeds 1 hour of a commute. *Added bonus point of 1 point for every 10 miles from home address to base site up to maximum of 4 points for those staff that live 40 miles+. Zero bonus points for anyone who lives under 10 miles from home to base. 8Parent/Guardian/Carer that uses car to transport children or provide caring responsibilities on way to or from base site and public transport is not a viable option9Registered car sharer who is actively sharing on a daily basis. (to inc. BHSCT staff family members within same household.)10Required to participate on

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to monitor data on ventilation and air quality levels in schools.
answer_text: The Department of Education and Education Authority (EA) work within the guidelines provided by the Department of Health concerning air quality and ventilation in schools and other public buildings.  Guidance on ventilation has been issued to all schools, providing advice on how to identify poorly ventilated areas and the measures that should be taken to improve ventilation if required. In line with best practice guidelines, the EA continue to promote and encourage natural ventilation in the first instance to maintain adequate levels of ventilation.  If a school has a specific concern regarding ventilation, they should contact EA to address the matter directly.  The Department does not monitor data on ventilation or air quality levels in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of applications for staff car parking passes at hospitals in the last 12 months, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) health care site; (iii) successful applications; and (iv) unsuccessful applications.
answer_text: There are three HSCNI Trusts which have an application process in place to allocate car parking passes at hospital sites; Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT); South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT). The number of successful and unsuccessful applications over the last 12 months, by hospital site, as advised by respective Trust organisations is as follows:- HSC TrustHospital SiteSuccessfulUnsuccessfulSHSCTCraigavonDaisy Hill72011 (1)SEHSCTArdsUlsterLagan Valley907021854733082BHSCTBelfast CityRoyal Victoria1515(2)1547225 (2)n/a  (3)Notes:-SHSCT applications are processed on a first come basis and unsuccessful applicants are added to a waiting list until a space becomes available.Belfast City application numbers are an average based total numbers processed from 2022 to date (3579).Belfast Royal Victoria Hospital launched its new application process in March 2024 and have to date received 4100 applications.  These applications are being worked through and 1547 applicants have received an approved outcome so far.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria used to allocate car parking passes at the Royal Victoria Hospital, particularly for doctors and other staff who work in multiple sites.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) has advised that the arrangement for the allocation of parking passes is changing from a first come with a waiting list, to an application and assessment process with the available spaces being allocated to those in greatest need.As advised by BHSCT, this is currently being rolled out on a phased basis and expected to be implemented at the Royal Victoria Hospital site in the coming months (anticipated October 2024).  There are a total of 10 agreed eligibility criteria of which includes for those who manage services and provide clinics on multiple sites.The 10 criteria BHSCT use to assess demonstrable need for car parking applications are outlined in the table below:-RankingAssessment Criteria1Disability (holders of a blue badge)* 2Permanent night duty/evening (from 4pm onwards)/weekend working*3Required to use private vehicle on a daily basis to directly deliver patient or client care (i.e. Community Health Visitor/District Nurse/ Midwife/Social Worker)4Carry out shift working as a requirement of the post and a set rota (scheduled to regularly start before 7:30am or finish after 7:30pm)5Manage Services on more than 1 site (responsible for managing staff on other Trust sites) 6Provide services/clinics on more than 1 site (with a frequency of 1 or more times per month)7A public transport journey that involves one or more changes, and exceeds 1 hour of a commute. *Added bonus point of 1 point for every 10 miles from home address to base site up to maximum of 4 points for those staff that live 40 miles+. Zero bonus points for anyone who lives under 10 miles from home to base. 8Parent/Guardian/Carer that uses car to transport children or provide caring responsibilities on way to or from base site and public transport is not a viable option9Registered car sharer who is actively sharing on a daily basis. (to include BHSCT staff family members within same household.)10Required to participate on a scheduled rota for on-call on a regular basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will consult on the development of the final section of the Ravenhill Road cycle lane to ensure it connects with Belfast City Centre and Lanyon Place train station to facilitate multi-modal and active travel.
answer_text: The truncation of the Ravenhill Road scheme has been proposed due to technical difficulties encountered during preliminary design in delivering the lower section and a suitable connection to Albertbridge Road. Resolution of these issues will be dependent on a number of longer term considerations, including the development of the Eastern Transport Plan. Delivery of the proposed scheme in the meantime will provide improved cycling facilities to schools and parks in the local community. Consideration is also being given to alternative options to improve cycle linkages from the scheme to the city centre, such as the proposed Lagan Pedestrian and Cycle Bridge. Once complete, this will provide an attractive connection from Rosetta to Belfast City Centre and Lanyon Place train station along the Ravenhill Road, Ormeau Embankment and the Lagan riverside path.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of nominations received from the voluntary, community and independent sector for the Derrytrasna Pastoral Care Awards; and (ii) whether nursery school settings are eligible to be nominated for the awards.
answer_text: Educational settings cannot self-nominate for the Derrytrasna Pastoral Care Awards.  Parents / carers or others in the wider school community can nominate an educational setting that has provided exceptional pastoral care. There were five nominations received for non-statutory pre-school settings (with DE-funded pre-school education programme places) for the 2024 Derrytrasna Pastoral Care Awards.Awards are available for nursery, primary, post-primary and special school sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10840/22-27, to detail the (i) grade; and (ii) remit of those staff members allocated to the Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: My Department has nominated two staff members to the Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group. The first is a Grade 7, who is Head of the Department’s Family Policy Unit, with responsibility for the Department’s Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care, alongside other policy responsibilities. The second is a Band 8a member of the Childcare Partnership Team in the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group, who has lead responsibility for the Bright Start School Age Childcare Scheme, the Family Support NI website and is the childcare information lead in the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the next meeting of the Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group will take place.
answer_text: The Task and Finish Group met on 27 March and 24 April.  The next meeting is scheduled for 5 June.  Additional bi-lateral engagement (not all in meeting format) has taken place between my officials and DoH, DfE, DfC, DoF, DAERA and NISRA.  I hope to bring proposals to the Executive in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of (i) all meetings of the Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group; and (ii) all bilateral meetings that have taken place between the Department of Education and officials from other departments in relation to childcare that have taken place since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Task and Finish Group met on 27 March and 24 April.  The next meeting is scheduled for 5 June.  Additional bi-lateral engagement (not all in meeting format) has taken place between my officials and DoH, DfE, DfC, DoF, DAERA and NISRA.  I hope to bring proposals to the Executive in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will bring forward proposals to the Executive in order to access the £25 million allocated to childcare.
answer_text: The Task and Finish Group met on 27 March and 24 April.  The next meeting is scheduled for 5 June.  Additional bi-lateral engagement (not all in meeting format) has taken place between my officials and DoH, DfE, DfC, DoF, DAERA and NISRA.  I hope to bring proposals to the Executive in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Special Educational Needs Link Officers employed by the Education Authority.
answer_text: There are 36 Special Educational Needs (SEN) Link Officers employed by the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that have been required to repurpose (i) libraries; and (ii) computer rooms to facilitate the creation of additional classroom space in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.  Although some minor work schemes involve the formal reconfiguration or repurposing of internal space to provide additional teaching space, schools can, and have also informally repurposed internal space to provide additional classroom space.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children who have a statement of special educational needs.
answer_text: Figures from the 2023/24 school census data collection exercise report that there were 26,964 pupils with a statement of special educational needs in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the role of the Education and Training Inspectorate in reviewing (i) Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools; and (ii) the statementing process.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) has a statutory duty to monitor, inspect and report on the standard of education and of professional practice among teachers in relevant establishments, as set out in Article 102 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986. Over the past two years, ETI has evaluated the quality of education in a sample of Learning Support Centres, now referred to as Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS). The nature of this provision has changed significantly over this period due to the significant increase in the number of settings being established. A report of the ETI evaluation will be published in the coming months.ETI is currently undertaking an evaluation of the statementing process, referred to as the Statutory Assessment Process. The evaluation focuses on whether the current statutory assessment process meets the needs of children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) in schools, with reference to the practicalities of the process and the implementation of the statement. The findings are due to be published in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current waiting list for educational psychologists in relation to dyslexia assessments.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the Educational Psychology Service (EPS) do not hold a waiting list in relation to dyslexia assessments.I refer you to the answer provided in response to AQW 11592/22-27 in relation to the EPS role in supporting pupils with literacy difficulties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of educational psychologists who (i) are bilingual; (ii) are multilingual; and (iii) have Irish as one of their primary languages.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) does not hold definitive or up-to-date information in relation to this question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children in schools that have a diagnosis of dyslexia.
answer_text: The 2023/24 School Census data collection has been impacted by industrial action. Although data has been received from all schools, for 25% of schools (across primary, post primary and special schools) it has not been possible to obtain pupil level information.  This means that for these schools, pupil level information relating to special education need categories is not available. Figures from the 2022/23 school census data collection exercise recorded that there were 8,029 pupils who had a Special Educational Need recorded as Dyslexia or Specific Learning Difficulty - Language/Literacy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current waiting list for the Education Authority Literacy Service.
answer_text: As at 13 May 2024, 773 children and young people had their referral accepted and were waiting to receive support from the Literacy Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the advice provided to school special educational needs co-ordinators on the delivery of initial screenings to assess for dyslexia.
answer_text: The Educational Psychology Service does not routinely provide Special Educational Needs Co-ordinators (SENCOs) with information on initial screening tools for assessing dyslexia. SENCOs are familiar with a wide range of assessment tools which assist them in identifying individual children’s learning strengths and weaknesses, including a measure of severity of symptoms of literacy difficulties including dyslexia.  Where this information is requested, Educational Psychologists are happy to support SENCOs and, where appropriate, signpost them to specific training on dyslexia and/or literacy difficulties offered by the Education Authority’s (EA) Literacy Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to improve the uptake of modern foreign languages in post-primary schools.
answer_text: The decision to end the Primary Modern Languages Programme (PMLP) was taken in March 2015 by the then Education Minister, John O’Dowd MLA. This decision was taken to protect frontline education services. The current financial situation would prove prohibitive in bringing forward a similar initiative at this time.  However, the Northern Ireland curriculum offers flexibility for primary schools to provide pupils with the opportunity to learn additional languages either during core teaching hours or as part of their extracurricular provision.There have been numerous reports on the modern languages curriculum and qualifications in Northern Ireland over the last decade and more. These have identified issues which have led to a steady decline in the number of pupils studying modern languages in Northern Ireland at key stage 4 and post-16.  This decline is mirrored across the United Kingdom and other English-speaking countries around the world. Stakeholders generally agree on the main reasons for this decline, such as the promotion of STEM subjects, the perception of the difficulty of languages, and the lack of understanding of the multiple cognitive benefits of learning another language.Modern languages have been identified as a priority area for development within the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  My officials continue to liaise with counterparts across the Untitled Kingdon and with the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) to consider how best to support languages and guide the Department’s focus to encourage the development of language skills.  As part of these ongoing discussions, CCEA has recently provided updated proposals to enhance and strengthen the Modern Languages Strategy moving forward.  These proposals, recognise that a cross-phase approach is needed to address the decline in the uptake of modern languages.  My Department will consider these proposals in light of the current restrictive budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process (i) a pupil; and (ii) their parents or carers would be required to go through to receive an assessment for dyslexia.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that where a parent has a concern in relation to their child’s literacy, they should, in the first instance, discuss this with their child’s school.  All schools have access to a designated EA Educational Psychologist (EP) to consult with on these matters.  The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice is needs led and not dependent on a pupil having a diagnosis of dyslexia.  If following assessment of needs by an EP, a request is made to the EA Literacy Service (EALS) for support, the request will note the required support which may be in the form of advice and guidance, small group support or individual support.  Pupils experiencing literacy difficulties should be recorded on the school SEN register under the category Cognition and Learning.  The supports and resources available from all EA SEN and/or Disability (SEND) services to support schools in the implementation of the SEN Code of Practice are set out In the EA SEND Plan of Arrangements.  Further details can be viewed here: https://send.eani.org.uk/ea-plan-arrangements.If any parent or school has a specific query, they can contact the service at any time by e-mailing literacyservice@eani.org.uk or phoning 028 9448 2228.As part of the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN, all pupil support services delivered by the EA are being reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the draft Strategy for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland contained funding commitments in excess of the £50,000 that was allocated to the implementation of the Act in the published Strategy for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The draft Strategy for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland did not contain funding commitments in excess of the £50,000 that was allocated to the implementation of the Act in the Strategy for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland published in April 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to restore the Primary Modern Languages Programme.
answer_text: The decision to end the Primary Modern Languages Programme (PMLP) was taken in March 2015 by the then Education Minister, John O’Dowd MLA. This decision was taken to protect frontline education services. The current financial situation would prove prohibitive in bringing forward a similar initiative at this time.  However, the Northern Ireland curriculum offers flexibility for primary schools to provide pupils with the opportunity to learn additional languages either during core teaching hours or as part of their extracurricular provision.There have been numerous reports on the modern languages curriculum and qualifications in Northern Ireland over the last decade and more. These have identified issues which have led to a steady decline in the number of pupils studying modern languages in Northern Ireland at key stage 4 and post-16.  This decline is mirrored across the United Kingdom and other English-speaking countries around the world. Stakeholders generally agree on the main reasons for this decline, such as the promotion of STEM subjects, the perception of the difficulty of languages, and the lack of understanding of the multiple cognitive benefits of learning another language.Modern languages have been identified as a priority area for development within the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  My officials continue to liaise with counterparts across the Untitled Kingdon and with the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) to consider how best to support languages and guide the Department’s focus to encourage the development of language skills.  As part of these ongoing discussions, CCEA has recently provided updated proposals to enhance and strengthen the Modern Languages Strategy moving forward.  These proposals, recognise that a cross-phase approach is needed to address the decline in the uptake of modern languages.  My Department will consider these proposals in light of the current restrictive budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the pathways for support available for children who have been diagnosed with dyslexia.
answer_text: Where Dyslexia/Specific Learning Difficulties in Literacy have been identified within an Education Authority (EA) psychology assessment, the Educational Psychologist will recommend to the school a range of strategies and resources after considering those previously used by the school.  These targeted recommendations form part of the outcome of the psychological assessment and may involve a referral to the EA Literacy Service for either advisory support or direct teaching intervention. A range of additional resources including information, guidance on accessing training, suggested activities and resources to help parents and schools to support literacy difficulties where they arise or are at risk of arising can be found at https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services/literacy.Schools and parents can also contact the Literacy Service to seek advice, guidance and information for any pupil, either referred to the service or not, by phoning 028 9448 2228 during normal office hours.  Alternatively, the service can be contacted by e-mail at literacyservice@eani.org.uk.The End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and associated workstreams are considering all options for reform across SEN policy areas to ensure that children and young people with SEN have the right support from the right people at the right time in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any additional support he can put in place to improve road safety for pupils and staff at St. Bride's Primary School, South Belfast.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of traffic calming and road safety measures and am committed to exploring solutions that deliver better for communities and improve lives across the North.I can advise that standard school warning signs are in place on Derryvolgie Avenue and school flashing signs have previously been provided on the Malone Road on both approaches to Derryvolgie Avenue and on Windsor Avenue as part of a Safer Routes to School initiative.Both Derryvolgie Avenue and Windsor Avenue have also been assessed for proposed traffic calming measures.  Whilst the proposals for Windsor Avenue have not been assessed as sufficiently high to progress at this stage, I am pleased to advise that the Department is progressing plans for a traffic calming scheme along Derryvolgie Avenue, in the vicinity of St Bride’s Primary School, Belfast.  Officials have recently carried out a consultation on these proposals and an objection to the scheme has been raised.  Following appropriate consideration of this objection, it is planned that a decision on the proposals will be made in the coming months.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress towards the new build project for St. Bride's Primary School, South Belfast.
answer_text: St Bride’s Primary School is proceeding in design under the School Enhancement Programme.The project is at Technical Design stage (RIBA Stage 4), which is the final design stage before construction tender. Planning permission was received from Belfast City Council on 14 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support his Department can provide to the Special Educational Needs Advice Centre.
answer_text: The department is considering the role of the wider voluntary and community sector in supporting children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) and/or disability and their families as part of the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the Education Welfare Service transformation reference group.
answer_text: The Education Welfare Service (EWS) Transformation Reference Group provided the Education Authority with invaluable assistance and advice on the development of a revised model of EWS practice and the associated business case.  The Reference Group was stood down on completion of the project in November 2022.  Consideration will be given to re-establishing the Reference Group to support implementation of the revised model following approval of the business case and resolution of NIPSA led EWS industrial action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the data his Department collects on why children and young people may have reduced access to education.
answer_text: The department does not collect this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact of short school days on children with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: Current legislation enables schools to reduce their curriculum for pupils for reasons such as emotional, behavioural and welfare circumstances where, for example, a pupil has special educational needs (SEN) or is on a phased return to school after a long illness.  Pupils should only be excepted from the statutory curriculum and assessment requirements on a temporary basis if the school has carried out the appropriate procedures outlined in the Education (Curriculum) (Temporary Exemptions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1990.  These temporary arrangements must be kept under review by the school principal.Reduced school days for children and young people with SEN and/or a disability are only permitted if the school authorities have been advised by a qualified medical practitioner that it would be detrimental to a pupil to remain under instruction for the full specified period. The End to End (E2E) Review of SEN is considering the implications for attendance across the associated workstreams to ensure that all pupils have equal access to the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the involvement his Department has had in the development and implementation of the cross-departmental Collaboration Strategic Framework in light of the Children’s Services Co-Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: The Department of Education has been involved in the development of a Collaboration Strategic Framework along with many other NICS Departments since Spring 2022. That work is being jointly led by The Executive Office and the Department for Communities.The issues of pooled funding, more efficient ways of working and prioritising the needs of children and young people have been part of the discussions to date.
