question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration is given to the proximity to schools when the Housing Executive allocates temporary accommodation to families with children.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Northern Ireland Housing Executives' Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The decision regarding the suitability of emergency temporary accommodation offered in the discharge of the homelessness duty is made in accordance with the Housing (NI) Order 1988.  In determining suitability, specific Designated Officers within the Housing Executive will consider factors such as the individual circumstances and the needs of a given Applicant and will take on board any representation made on behalf of the Applicant. The consideration of individual circumstances will include proximity to schools for families with children and the Housing Executive will seek to provide a placement as close as possible to facilities such as schools.It should be noted that while the Housing Executive will endeavour to provide a placement that as far as possible, meets the needs of the respective household, since the onset of the pandemic there has been a sustained increase in demand for temporary accommodation with the most recent published figures noting a total of 8,328 placements having been provided between 1st April 2023 and 31st December 2023. In such circumstances, any provision of temporary accommodation will be dictated by availability on a given day.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to the importance of affordable childcare in improving economic activity, as noted in the Economic Inactivity in Northern Ireland report produced by Pivotal Public Policy Forum NI.
answer_text: I recognise the link between affordable childcare and economic activity.  It is one of a number of factors influencing labour market participation decisions.  My Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 sets out my plans for a package of measures in 2024/25 to support early learning and childcare provision.  My officials are working closely with counterparts in other departments, including Economy and Communities, to implement a Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to help working parents with the affordability of childcare.The interim Minister for the Economy had advised that her Department would be considering the scope to improve childcare provision within the skills programmes which it offers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to streamline the free school meal application process for parents of Special Educational Needs (SEN) children with specialised diets, given the requirement to complete a form to request a paper form while all other non-SEN free school meal applications can be completed online.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is tasked with securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas.  Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place; 89.2% at their parents’ first preference setting. Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway and parents will be advised of the outcome on 11 June. The EA has confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas, with 65 funded pre-school places still available in the South Belfast Parliamentary Constituency area at the close of Stage One of the pre-school admissions process.The EA PEG continue to monitor pre-school provision in all areas including South Belfast.In respect of primary admissions, my department works with schools to create additional capacity in sectors and areas where oversubscription arises.  At the conclusion of the primary admissions process the EA has advised that one child resident in South Belfast was still to secure a place with 237 places still available.Children with a statement of specialist educational needs (SEN) follow a different process than the general admissions process, one which seeks to secure an appropriate placement which meets their individual needs.  There is significant demand for specialist education places particularly for pre-school and primary one children, and the Belfast area is an area of pressure.  The Education Authority, supported by my Department, is working at pace to create additional capacity across the education system to ensure sufficient appropriate provision for all learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to review the Home to School Transport policy.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused, by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. The transport policy supports other policies within the Department, such as parental preference and Special Educational Needs (SEN).  My Department is currently considering the report published by the Independent Review of Education and work is progressing on an end-to-end review of SEN.  Work is also underway on an action plan arising from the EA Landscape Review, to include detailed consideration of the operational delivery of services such as school transport.It is important that any review of the Home to School Transport Policy complements and can build on the outcomes from these workstreams and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my Department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education pursuant to AQW 9341/22-27, how many meetings of the (i) Integrated Education Steering Group; and (ii) Integrated Education Strategy Working Group that have taken place in the last two years have been in-person meetings.
answer_text: To date, none of the Integrated Education Steering Group or Working Group meetings have been held in-person.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the procurement and tender timeline for non-statutory providers of Special Educational Needs and Disability pre-school places.
answer_text: Under the Education Authority’s (EA) ‘Pathfinder Project 2023-24’ four community and voluntary sector organisations were contracted to provide specialist pre-school education places for 42 children presenting with severe learning difficulties (SLD) in the Belfast area.  The Pathfinder Project was agreed for 2023/24, with opportunity for expansion should delivery of the pre-school curricular guidance be deemed effective and successful.   Evaluation will be informed by findings from the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI).Subject to positive evaluation, the EA has advised that it intends to award contracts for Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) non-statutory pre-school places for the 2024/25 academic year, utilising compliant Direct Award facility as permitted under procurement regulations and under PPN 02/21 Procurement of Social and Other Specific Services. Any longer term contracts for SEND pre-school places beyond 2024/25 will require a new procurement exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional support that can be provided to non-statutory pre-school providers to ensure parity in resources and support with statutory providers.
answer_text: My Department reviews the rate at which non-statutory pre-school education settings are funded on an annual basis.  The current rate of £2,150 per child attending a part-time non-statutory pre-school setting is higher than the Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) rate of £2,109 for a pupil attending a part-time statutory nursery unit and is due to be reviewed again in Summer 2024.I am also considering proposals for additional in-year support to be provided as part of a range of stabilisation measures for the Early Learning and Childcare sector, subject to the availability of necessary Executive funding.  The scoping study commissioned to provide a detailed evidence base for the development of a costed implementation plan for pre-school standardisation has considered how support and advice for pre-school settings could be improved.  Once the scoping study report is received and the necessary financial and staff resources secured, detailed planning for implementation can commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what provision can be made for people with pets who are in need of temporary housing from the Housing Executive.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive will seek to provide the most suitable form of temporary accommodation based on the needs of households. Any provision of temporary accommodation for households who have pets and require temporary accommodation will be dependent on arrangements with the respective provider. While arrangements will always seek to align households with pets with a provider who will facilitate a household with pets, the provision of temporary accommodation will be dependent on availability at a given point in time.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the progress of the site search commissioned in relation to the proposed Mid Down Integrated College; and (ii) when the site search report is expected to be received.
answer_text: Potential locations for Mid Down Integrated College are being considered along the main arterial route which extends from Carryduff to Crossgar. A site search report was received from Land and Property Services on 10 April 2024.The Department is currently preparing the scope for the procurement of an Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) to undertake feasibility studies on potential sites to identify if they would be suitable for the College’s long-term needs. The preferred site will be identified through the business case process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the appropriateness of Standing Stones Lodge, Belfast, being used as temporary accommodation by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation, as such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, specifically in relation to Standing Stones Lodge, who has advised me as follows:“Any use of Standing Stones Lodge for temporary accommodation are within arrangements whereby the Housing Executive avails of non-standard accommodation on a non-contractual basis as part of our statutory duties. The use of Standing Stones Lodge is concerned solely with securing the availability of accommodation units to enable the Housing Executive to discharge its statutory duty to make available temporary accommodation to individuals presenting as homeless, in certain circumstances, pursuant to the Housing (NI) Order 1988, as amended, (‘the 1988 Order’). Against that background, it is important to clarify that the accommodation at Standing Stones Lodge is made available as a short-term measure in order to meet immediate need for temporary accommodation provision and that there is a strong emphasis on moving an individual homeless applicant as quickly as possible to alternative standard temporary accommodation, or in some cases permanent housing." It is my intention to make homelessness rare, brief, and non-recurrent.  I will continue to work with Grainia Long, the NIHE and the wider sector to address this issue and to make a long-term commitment to developing Housing-Led and Housing First provision.That said, it will take time to achieve these ambitions and my short-term focus will continue to be supporting those currently in crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the additional (i) resources; and (ii) funding to be provided to schools that have agreed to open new Specialist Provision in Mainstream school classes for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: My department and the Education Authority (EA) are about to undertake a period of collaborative engagement with those schools that indicated in a recent survey they would be willing to establish specialist provision classes in mainstream schools for September 2024 and beyond. An individual approach will be taken with each school depending on the needs of the children who will be attending. Staffing resources provided for specialist classes vary according to the type of specialist provision and the profile of need of the children. Teaching and non-teaching staff have access to support, training and resources from the EA's Specialist Setting Support Team and support from the school’s dedicated Special Educational Needs (SEN) Link Officer.  Additional training and support is also available through the EA’s Pupil Support Services, outreach support provided by special schools and other organisations such as Middletown Centre for Autism. Essential accommodation works required to schools’ premises to meet the needs of children are funded and schools receive a one-off payment of £3,000 to support the establishment of new provision.  Those establishing new provisions for September 2024 will also receive a capital equipment payment of £20,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) qualifications; and (ii) experience required for teachers and classroom assistants working in Specialist Provision in Mainstream school classes.
answer_text: Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS) classes aim to provide high quality educational experiences for the children attending them and ensuring they have confident and competent staff who are equipped to support children with special educational needs (SEN) is key to this.  Schools have responsibility for recruiting their own teachers.  As a minimum, an applicant for a teaching post in a SPiMS must hold a teaching qualification which meets the requirements for recognition to teach in grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland.Each school then sets its own experience selection criteria using a template provided by Education Authority (EA), which includes ‘person specifications’ for teachers working with children and young people with SEN. Governors select from a list of experience, knowledge and skill requirements relevant to the post and may add any criteria that are specific to the needs of the pupils or school.Schools are also responsible for the recruitment and selection of classroom assistants and similarly will set out qualifications and/or experience deemed to be essential criteria as determined relevant to the post which may include a Level 2 childcare qualification as approved by the EA (a list of approved qualifications is available from the EA website www.eani.org.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many of the required 1,000 additional Special Educational Needs and Disability places have been secured.
answer_text: There has been significant progress made in agreeing solutions with schools to meet the demand for special education provision through the creation of additional capacity in special schools and new specialist provision classes in mainstream schools (SPiMS).   The position is fluid and changes daily as the flow of children through the assessment and review process is continual and as solutions for additional provision are scoped and agreed between the Education Authority (EA) and schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the quality and appropriateness of Statutory Provision in Mainstream school classes is evaluated and measured.
answer_text: The quality of statutory provision in mainstream school classes is evaluated by the Education and Training Inspectorate. The purpose of all inspection activity is to build capacity within the organisations inspected to provide high-quality education and outcomes for learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's work to prepare for the standardisation of the pre-school education programme.
answer_text: My Department has carried out significant preparatory work.  This includes the successful “Amalgamation for Standardisation” pilot which facilitated the approval of full-time pre-school places via a streamlined process.  A scoping study commissioned to provide a detailed evidence base for the development of a costed implementation plan is also nearing completion. Once the scoping study report is received and the necessary financial and staff resources secured, detailed planning for implementation can commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the contribution that core grant funding has made to the ability of the community and voluntary sector to operate and provide services; and (ii) the impact of cuts to this funding.
answer_text: I recognise the challenging financial environment in which the Community and Voluntary Sector is currently operating, which is why I have retained some level of core grant support this year despite the funding pressures also facing the health and social care system and the inequity of the existing closed scheme.I fully recognise the important role played by the Sector in the delivery of many of our strategic aims - for example, supporting the elderly, providing much needed help to vulnerable children and families and addressing health inequalities.While the funding pot available for core grant going forward will be dependent upon the Department’s future budget allocations, I want to make it clear that I am committed to supporting the sector, and to seeking its support and involvement in co-designing a new scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8382/22-27, for an update on the (i) terms of reference; (ii) membership; and (iii) and forward work programme for the cross-departmental Task and Finish Group on Childcare.
answer_text: The Task and Finish Group was established to bring a cross-departmental perspective to the development of an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  The Group includes representatives from the following departments: Economy, Health, Finance, Communities and Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  Specialist advice is also provided by economists and statisticians.  The Group’s initial focus has been on the identification and design of interim measures which could be introduced in 2024/25 to ease current pressures on parents and providers.  It will also play an important role in supporting the development of the longer-term strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to detail the engagement they have had with the Irish government in relation to their proposals for new legislation to enable asylum seekers to be sent back to the United Kingdom; and (ii) for their assessment of the impact this will have on Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Immigration policy is not devolved, rather it is an excepted matter falling to Home Office.  The Executive Office does not therefore have any remit in respect of return agreements for asylum seekers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the option to refer patients for vasectomy services will be available on the systems used by GPs.
answer_text: I was delighted to announce that non-scalpel vasectomy services in Northern Ireland will be transferred from secondary care to primary care.  This will take effect from April 2024, with a transition phase throughout 2024/25.  This service enables GPs to refer patients requesting a non-scalpel vasectomy to a designated host practice where an accredited GP Vasectomist will perform the procedure negating the need for a referral to secondary care. My Department is currently working with both HSC Trusts and the Federation Support Unit to ensure that a robust process is put in place to enable the safe transfer of care for patients suitable for the non-scalpel vasectomy service. This will ensure chronological management of patients waiting to access this procedure and who can be transferred to the primary care non-scalpel service. Whilst the aim is to re-open the GP to GP referral destination on the clinical communication gateway our priority is to ensure a robust process is in place to safely manage all patients currently waiting to access this procedure. It is anticipated that the CCG GP referral destination will be re-opened in May 2024 following receipt of confirmation from the Federation Support Unit that waiting lists have been validated and the service is ready to accept new referrals. A programme of communication and engagement is scheduled to take place with GPs led by the Federation Support Unit to advise on when referrals can commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department's (i) current; and (ii) planned commitment of (a) staff; and (b) resources to the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: I am fully committed to work with my executive colleagues to deliver the Childcare Affordability Scheme. I have assigned two officials from my Department to support the work of the task and finish group. In addition Invest staff will be made available to assist with the design of a scheme. That is envisaged to be two people for two days per week.A key part of my department’s contribution will be to consider how we can support the supply of skills into the sector. An early action has been to assess how the skills pathways support the sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's (i) current; and (ii) planned commitment of (a) staff; and (b) resources to the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: My Department has nominated two officials to participate in meetings of the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their Department's (i) current; and (ii) planned commitment of (a) staff; and (b) resources to the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: Access to high-quality and affordable childcare is a priority. We are committed to ensuring this is made available for all working families. Our department has pledged to provide support and input to the Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail her Department's (i) current; and (ii) planned commitment of (a) staff; and (b) resources to the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: My department currently has two representatives on this Task and Finish Group – one representative from a budget perspective, and one from a policy perspective.  No change to my Department’s staff and resource commitment is planned, but this will be kept under review.The Executive agreed the 2024-25 Budget on 25 April 2024.  This included £25 million to support the development of a Childcare Strategy. The Executive may increase the funding for this in-year when proposals are brought forward by the cross-departmental task and finish group established by the Education Minister to take forward the development of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's (i) current; and (ii) planned commitment of (a) staff; and (b) resources to the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: Minister Givan’s request for support from Departments noted that the group would need significant operational inputs primarily from DfE, DoH and DfC.  My Children’s Champion has been available from the outset to provide input to the task and finish group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether regulations permit developers to begin construction work before building plan approval is granted.
answer_text: ‘Development’ is defined by the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011. Carrying out development without the required planning permission constitutes a breach of planning control. Planning permission may also be granted by way of a development order or may be granted expressly. Where planning permission has been granted by a development order, a developer may commence that development without an application or plans.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether the public toilets in Parliament Buildings are considered to be Stoma Friendly.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is very aware of the need to ensure that Parliament Buildings is accessible for everyone and endeavours to be proactive in terms of accessibility adaptations. As such I can confirm that all of the disabled toilets in the public areas of the ground and first floors are stoma friendly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans for engaging with children and young people on the future development of Relationships and Sexuality Education.
answer_text: My Department uses a range of approaches to access the views of children and young people.   In respect of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE), this will include online focus groups, Education Authority Youth Service facilitated events and engagement with representative bodies such as the Secondary Schools Student Union of Northern Ireland and, subject to being invited to meet, the Youth Assembly Education Committee.  There is also regular contact both at Ministerial and official level with children and young people’s advocacy organisations.  This includes the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People, the Childrens Law Centre, the Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission and the Mental Health Champion. My Department will continue to adopt a range of approaches to engage with children and young people. My Department also expects schools to engage directly with pupils in the development or review of their RSE policy and taught programme and to reflect input received in each of these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to improving the financial education provided to pupils in post-primary schools.
answer_text: Financial capability is a statutory element of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  Schools are required to provide pupils with opportunities to develop a range of skills to promote independence through planning, managing and using resources, across a number of areas of learning.The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provide a range of resources to support financial education to pupils at post-primary level.  Additional resources have been added to the provision each year, over a number of years.  All resources can be found within the Financial Capability Post-Primary section of the CCEA website.CCEA has also worked in collaboration with the Consumer Council on behalf of the ScamwiseNI partnership to develop an online resource called ‘Scamwise’.  Over the last five years, CCEA has developed a strong working relationship with Young Enterprise Northern Ireland (YENI) and the Money and Pensions Service (MaPS) to promote financial capability and develop resources for teachers to use. For example, in 2023, YENI developed a Teacher Professional Learning Financial Education programme to support teachers in the delivery of financial education across all stages of the Northern Ireland Curriculum; this was funded by MaPS and aligned to the Department’s Strategy for Teacher Professional Learning – ‘Learning Leaders’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to providing additional resources to teachers to support them in providing financial education to pupils in post-primary schools.
answer_text: Financial capability is a statutory element of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  Schools are required to provide pupils with opportunities to develop a range of skills to promote independence through planning, managing and using resources, across a number of areas of learning.The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provide a range of resources to support financial education to pupils at post-primary level.  Additional resources have been added to the provision each year, over a number of years.  All resources can be found within the Financial Capability Post-Primary section of the CCEA website.CCEA has also worked in collaboration with the Consumer Council on behalf of the ScamwiseNI partnership to develop an online resource called ‘Scamwise’.  Over the last five years, CCEA has developed a strong working relationship with Young Enterprise Northern Ireland (YENI) and the Money and Pensions Service (MaPS) to promote financial capability and develop resources for teachers to use. For example, in 2023, YENI developed a Teacher Professional Learning Financial Education programme to support teachers in the delivery of financial education across all stages of the Northern Ireland Curriculum; this was funded by MaPS and aligned to the Department’s Strategy for Teacher Professional Learning – ‘Learning Leaders’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what additional support he can provide to local councils to encourage them to make all public toilets Stoma Friendly.
answer_text: My Department works in partnership annually with the disability sector, DAERA, local Councils and the Northern Ireland Museums Council (NIMC) to deliver the Access and Inclusion programme. The aim of the programme is to promote a more inclusive society by improving accessibility at arts, cultural and active recreation venues for people with disabilities. To date, the department has invested more than £6 million in over 300 projects to achieve this objective in developments such as:Sensory Rooms and GardensAccessible Doors Accessible play park equipmentChanging place facilitiesAudio Visual ImprovementsIn 2023-24 Ards and North Down delivered a project to provide stoma toilet friendly facilities across the borough, providing suitable amenities to over 40 venues, with a grant award of up to £30,000.I am currently working with officials to understand the implications of the recent Budget announcement. However, it is clear that the capital funding position for my Department in 2024-25 will be a significant challenge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to providing guidance to businesses on the development of Stoma Friendly toilets.
answer_text: My Department has not issued any guidance to businesses on the development of Stoma Friendly toilets.  I am aware that the Equality Commission has produced a range of publications covering disability issues in relation to facilities and provision of services.  These are available on the Commission’s website or by contacting their enquiry line.  The Equality Commission can also provide advice and training on anti-discrimination legislation and good equality practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider the introduction of traffic lights, or other road safety measures, at the Saintfield Road and Ballynahinch Road roundabout, Carryduff.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department is currently carrying out carriageway resurfacing scheme between the Carryduff roundabout and Green Pastures on the Ballynahinch Road in Carryduff. This scheme is substantially complete.  I can advise that besides the current resurfacing scheme no other works are planned for the Ballynahinch Road at this time.In relation to the introduction of traffic lights at the Carryduff Roundabout, I can advise that the location operates as a roundabout priority junction whereby traffic enters the roundabout in gaps and caters for the different traffic movements. The current roundabout layout would not lend itself to signalisation and could in fact be detrimental to the capacity and operation of the roundabout. As such, the Department has no plans to introduce signals at this time. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to improve the Relationships and Sexuality Education curriculum to better inform children and young people about consent and healthy relationships.
answer_text: The flexibility in our curriculum means that schools can discuss important issues such as consent and healthy relationships at a time when they are important to pupils and are the subject of wider debate across society.  My Department requires schools to consult with parents and pupils and to reflect feedback in their taught programme.My officials have established a task and finish group to consider the findings of the Education and Training Inspectorate’s (ETI) Evaluation of the Preventative Curriculum in Schools and Education Other Than At School EOTAS) Centres.  The report found that some sensitive topics, such as consent, were not covered sufficiently, or at all, in some schools.  My officials are currently working on guidance to address these issues and I will consider whether it is appropriate to issue further advice to schools in response to the ETI report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to the introduction of improved road safety measures on Ballynahinch Road, Carryduff.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department is currently carrying out carriageway resurfacing scheme between the Carryduff roundabout and Green Pastures on the Ballynahinch Road in Carryduff. This scheme is substantially complete.  My Department receives information from PSNI on collisions involving personal injury.  This information is monitored on an annual basis and used to inform the development and prioritisation of improvement schemes at locations where engineering solutions would most effectively deliver road safety benefits.  I can advise that besides the current resurfacing scheme no other works are planned for the Ballynahinch Road at this time.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the average length of time taken for the Victims Payment Board to complete Step 5: Determination Process of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board has advised that it is unable to provide the average length of time taken to complete Step 5.  This is due to the complex nature of cases.  Cases can also be adjourned for further information / evidence gathering to provide applicants (or their representative) with an opportunity to comment on specific issues or as a result of engaging with applicants (or their representative) to agree a date suitable to all parties for a face-to-face hearing where required.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the average time taken from initial application to the completion of Step 6: Notice of Determination to applicant by the Victims Payment Board in relation to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme process.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) has advised that the average time taken from submission of an application to notice of determination to applicant is currently in the region of 400 days.  It is important to recognise that every application received is different in nature and is considered on its own merits by the Board. Cases can also be adjourned for further information / evidence gathering to provide applicants (or their representative) with an opportunity to comment on specific issues or as a result of engaging with applicants (or their representative) to agree a date suitable to all parties for a face-to-face hearing where required.  The VPB is continuing to review and improve its internal processes and information sharing with stakeholders in order to reduce the average time in relation to each step of the process for assessing applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to improve safety for pedestrians at the junction of Ormeau Road and Carolan Road, South Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that officials have recently reviewed this location and have identified no particular safety concerns at this junction. Also, an examination of the recorded road traffic collision history, provided by the PSNI, did not highlight an issue at the junction.As such, at this time, there are no plans to carry out any works at the junction of Ormeau Road and Carolan Road, South Belfast.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what consideration they have given to the implementation of an independent process evaluation of the Victims Payment Board with participants who have been through the system and received an outcome.
answer_text: Under Regulation 52 of the Victims’ Payments Regulations, the Secretary of State must conduct a review of the Victims’ Payments Scheme within 3 years of it opening.  The review therefore needs to be completed by August 2024.  The Secretary of State has advised that the review is underway In preparing the report, the Regulations state that the Secretary of State must consult, amongst others, persons representing the interests of those injured or killed as a result of relevant incidents as the Secretary of State considers appropriate.We have written to the Secretary of State providing our feedback on the extent to which the Scheme has met the principles. The Victims’ Payments Board President, staff and representatives of Sectoral Support Groups have also been consulted and contributed to this Review. In addition, the Victims’ Payments Board keeps the Scheme application and assessment process under continuous review to identify any potential improvements which could increase the throughput of cases and improve the overall applicant experience. The Board has recently engaged an independent review of operational processes to ensure that those processes are as efficient as possible.The Board is happy to receive feedback from applicants at any stage of the process.A Sectoral Engagement Group, chaired by TEO, meets on a regular basis to discuss any issues of concern within victims and survivors representative groups in relation to the Scheme and look for ways to improve the client journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 5711/22-27, to detail the work of the Digital Inclusion Unit in supporting elderly people with digital accessibility and helping them to carry out online transactions and banking.
answer_text: The Digital Inclusion Unit, within the Department of Finance, promotes Digital Inclusion in response to the increased use of online channels, including those to access public services. The unit’s current Go ON NI programme, which began in 2012, has been very successful in assisting people attain the necessary digital skills and continues to grow by targeting events, digital skills training sessions and promotional activities at the harder to reach digitally excluded sections of the population.The programme provides free digital training sessions in libraries and community centres as well as Independent Living Centres. There is a particular focus on older people at times throughout the year, with specific events taking place during Safer Internet Day, Positive Ageing Month and Get Online Week. Sessions being delivered currently include:‘Organise Your Life Online’ – which helps people find out about online banking, paying bills and websites offering money advice and benefits information.‘Privacy and Security’ – which gives tips on keeping online personal information private and secure.‘Internet Scams’ – this provides tips on how to stay safe online and how to avoid online scams.So far this year over 230 participants have received digital skills training and follow-up support from our partner, Supporting Communities, with 45% of these people being aged between 56-70 years old.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to address the issue of vaping in schools.
answer_text: The issue of vaping is primarily a public health matter.  As such, my department will follow appropriate guidance from the Department of Health / Public Health Agency on this issue.  As part of the statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding (PDMU) at primary level and Learning for Life and Work (LLW), Personal Development (PD) strand at post-primary level – schools have a duty to deliver drugs education (including legal and illegal substances).  This should raise awareness amongst children of the risks / dangers associated with vaping.  Furthermore, there is a statutory duty on schools to both promote positive behavior and have relevant policies in place on issues such as drug misuse.  Should they require, schools have the flexibility to work directly with the PHA and other organisations to provide learning opportunities in support of delivery of the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to progress a pedestrian path from Cairnshill park and ride to Alderwood Hill, Belfast, to provide parents and pupils at St Ita’s Primary School with a safe drop off point at the park and ride, increase active travel and alleviate parking on Alderwood Hill.
answer_text: The proposed pedestrian path between Cairnshill Park & Ride and St Ita’s Primary School is being delivered by Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council.To help facilitate this path, my Department is in the process of making alterations to the internal layout of the Park & Ride site and providing a gate in the existing fence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 5436/22-27; and (ii) AQW 6943/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's commitment of (i) staff; and (ii) resources to the the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: Ensuring that the work of my Department aligns with the development of a Childcare Strategy is one of my key priorities. To this end, and in response to a request from Minister Givan for staff resources to assist development, three senior officials from my Department have participated to date on the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group. Discussions have focussed on how my Department’s existing supports and ongoing policy work, particularly on addressing poverty, can complement the work of the Task and Finish Group and my Department has not committed specific financial resources. As this work progresses, I will keep my Department’s commitments under review to ensure that they remain supportive of the broader Childcare Strategy’s objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to improve the enforcement of double yellow lines on (i) Colenso Parade; and (ii) Sandhurst Drive, South Belfast.
answer_text: The Stranmillis area including Colenso Parade and Sandhurst Drive is currently scheduled as part of a routine enforcement patrol, Monday to Friday between 8:00 and 18:00.  As a result of requests from residents, this area is also randomly patrolled in the evenings.In February 2024, in an attempt to change changing behaviour, my Department collaborated with the PSNI in a joint parking enforcement operation in the Stranmillis area, specifically Colenso Parade and Sandhurst Drive.  Enforcement patrols are specifically developed for each area and reviewed every six months to confirm they remain proportionate and address the needs of the area. Requests for additional enforcement can be made to the following email address Roads.Parking@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schools in South Belfast to be included in the next tranche of the 20mph schools programme.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m.  A review is underway to determine if a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme is justified. The findings of this review will be made public later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to the introduction of additional parking restrictions or parking schemes on (i) Colenso Parade; and (ii) Sandhurst Drive, South Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise my officials consider that the extent of the current waiting restrictions on Colenso Parade and Sandhurst Drive are adequate and assist with the traffic management and progression in the area. There are no plans to extend these restrictions.I can further advise that the review of residents’ parking schemes is complete, and the report will be published when I have considered the findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide the exceptional circumstance home to school transport policy used by the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Exceptional Circumstances Home to School Transport Policy can be found at https://www.eani.org.uk/publications/exceptional-circumstances-policyFor ease of reference a hard copy is attached.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the cross-departmental Task and Finish Group on Childcare.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the Refugee Integration Strategy 2022-27 will be considered by the Executive.
answer_text: Our officials are finalising the revised draft Strategy and associated Thematic Delivery Plans with input from key internal and external stakeholders. We will bring these to the Executive for agreement in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to circulating guidance to school leaders to support them in the phased resumption of activities, following the end of action short of strike.
answer_text: On reaching the teachers’ pay agreement on 9 April 2024 all industrial action by teachers including action short of strike ceased.It was agreed through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee that a joint communication would issue explaining about the ending of the industrial action and what was expected of both teachers and school leaders in the restoration of working practices in schools.The joint communication issued on 9 April to all teachers and school leaders. It explained that schools will be expected to use the period between the ratification of the agreement and the end of the 2023-24 school year to prepare for the beginning of the 2024-25 academic year. Workload should be managed within the terms of the Jordanstown Agreement and TNC 2020/1. Teachers are required to work within the provisions of their time budgets for the remainder of this academic year. A revised Workload Agreement TNC 2024/2 was also agreed and issued which is effective from 1 August 2024. Central to contractual rights and the conditions of service for teachers is the provision of directed time budgets. In practice this means the Principal is responsible for determining a time budget for each member of staff. Detailed advice on developing directed time budgets including FAQs and training has been produced by the Education Authority and is available for all school leaders and teachers.School leaders may contact their employing authority and seek advice and support regarding any employment matter.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 9021/22-27, for an update on the implementation of the test and learn programme that is being jointly led by the Executive Office and the Department for Communities.
answer_text: In September 2023 the NICS Board approved a cross-departmental Collaboration Strategic Framework (CSF) to test and learn how Departments could deliver better outcomes by improving how we work together, and how we work with community partners and local government.  This included identifying opportunities to reduce the burden on community organisations in terms of multiple departmental funding processes, and consideration of the Departmental staffing costs of running multiple programmes separately.  The CSF test and learn was absorbed within existing NICS teams, and we were clear with stakeholders that no new investment would be made available as part of the pilot.A cross-Departmental Steering Group (SG) was set up to progress the work.  The SG is chaired by TEO and administered by DfC.  A draft ‘Collaboration Framework’ (a copy of which has been placed in the Assembly Library) was developed to act as a flexible tool for collaboration.  In parallel, the SG worked with Departmental statisticians to identify and map the localities where multiple Government Departments were operating (including both urban and rural areas).  It was agreed that community background, Departmental presence and levels of spend would be key data points to inform decisions on potential locations for pilots.  Using the data, six potential pilot areas were identified (i) Duncairn and Falls Super Output Areas (SOAs) of Belfast; (ii) Gortin SOA; (iii) Aughnacloy SOA; (iv) The Strand and (v) Diamond SOAs of Derry/Londonderry; and (vi) Monkstown and Rathcoole SOAs of Whitehouse.  The SG decided to focus on the Diamond and Aughnacloy SOAs to facilitate comparisons between rural and urban areas and due to the availability of experienced local staff working in the areas.  In January and February 2024 workshops were held in both pilot areas to gather views from stakeholders on collaboration.  These views are being analysed and will be shared with Ministers before the Summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7470/22-27 and AQW 8692/22-27, for an update on when this information will be available.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that following further investigation, full and robust statistics for the reasons for sickness absence are not currently available.  Actions to improve this position and the reporting functionality for absence reasons are due to be scoped for development and implementation.  Once this work is completed a timeline will be available for provision of this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8578/22-27, for a timeline for the presentation of a report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 to the Executive.
answer_text: The first report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 will be presented to the Executive within the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5438/22-27, (i) whether the Scoping Study Report has been received by his Department; and (ii) what consideration he has given to sharing this report with MLAs.
answer_text: The final Scoping Study Report has not yet been provided to my Department.  RSM UK await some remaining key inputs that are essential to ensuring it provides all the information necessary to develop a costed implementation plan. When I have had sight of the completed report, I will make a decision on its dissemination.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to (i) improve road safety; and (ii) reduce speeding on the Ballynahinch Road, Carryduff.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department is currently carrying out a carriageway resurfacing scheme between the Carryduff roundabout and Green Pastures on the Ballynahinch Road in Carryduff. This scheme is substantially complete.  My Department receives information from PSNI on collisions involving personal injury.  This information is monitored on an annual basis and used to inform the development and prioritisation of improvement schemes at locations where engineering solutions would most effectively deliver road safety benefits. I can advise that besides the current resurfacing scheme no other works are planned for the Ballynahinch Road at this time.Enforcement of the speed limits are the responsibility of the PSNI. Should residents consider there are incidents of speeding or inappropriate driving they should contact the police directly.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8695/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the impact of these vacancies on the ability to deliver an effective and efficient statementing process for children; and (ii) to detail his plans to ensure these vacancies are urgently filled.
answer_text: Vacancies within the Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS) can have a direct impact on the ability of the Service to deliver an effective and efficient service across the breadth of service delivery, including the statutory assessment process. However, the Service works collaboratively, through regional support mechanisms, to minimise any such impact while recruitment is ongoing.	Vacancies within the SARS will be filled in accordance with the Education Authority’s Recruitment Policy and associated procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will bring costed plans to the Executive to support parents and childcare providers through (ii) interim support; and (ii) the implementation of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I have bid for funding to enable me to implement a package of measures in the 2024/25 financial year on which the longer-term strategy can be built.  I hope to be in a position soon to bring forward costed proposals for Executive consideration.  What I am able to do will depend on the funding made available to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to reducing speed limits to improve safety on our roads.
answer_text: My Department’s approach to road safety engineering, including the provision of  speed control measures, is outlined in a number of documents that are publicly available to view at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/road-safety-engineering. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to develop a new Active Ageing Strategy to replace the 2016-22 strategy.
answer_text: I will be engaging with my officials over the coming weeks to understand work completed to date on developing a successor to the Active-Ageing Strategy 2016-22 and to consider the next steps.Final decisions on the content and implementation of an Active Ageing Strategy would be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to prioritising cycling in the new Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030.
answer_text: In October 2022 I published the draft Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030. As this is a cross-cutting matter I aim to present an updated Road Safety Strategy to the Executive in the coming weeks for their consideration.  Cycling groups Cycling UK, Cycling Ulster, Sustrans and ROSPA and the Department’s own Active Travel Branch were represented as part of the original consultation for the Strategy development.The Strategy is founded on the internationally recognised ‘Safe System’ principles and adopts an outcomes-based approach. The Strategy sets out three strategic outcomes namely: Safe People, Safe Roads, and Safe Vehicles.  There is a renewed focus on making our roads safer to use for all, particularly those who are more vulnerable, including pedestrians, cyclists, motorcyclists and horse riders, and reducing the number of people killed or seriously injured on our roads.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. We all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.My Department currently supports a number of important programmes that help young people travel safely, including the Cycling Proficiency Scheme that is delivered in a number of schools each year, with officials training teachers and providing resources for them to teach their pupils these important life skills. It is also important to recognise that with respect to active travel, a key role of my Department is the delivery of high quality infrastructure that enables people to consider walking, wheeling, and cycling as the natural choice for many of their everyday journeys and to feel safe when making those journeys. I am pleased to say that my Department has taken a number of significant steps since the introduction of the Climate Change Act in 2022 to accelerate delivery of safe, high quality active travel infrastructure into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to developing advocacy campaigns to support healthier school meals.
answer_text: The current obesity prevention strategic framework, ‘A Fitter Future for All’ (AFFFA), takes an integrated approach to obesity prevention and contains outcomes and actions across the life course for the entire population of Northern Ireland. The latest short-term outcomes, published in 2019, include a number of specific outcomes relating to healthier school meals and healthier eating for children and young people.The Healthy Food for Healthy Outcomes, launched in 2014 and applying to all grant-aided schools, is the Department for Education’s overarching food in school policy advocating a whole school approach to all food and drinks provided and consumed in schools and developing knowledge and skills in relation to healthy eating and lifestyles. It articulates the policy in relation to all food and drink in schools, support available, roles, responsibilities, and funding. My Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) works closely with Department of Education through the Food in Schools Forum to continue to advocate for and to support healthier school meals.Minimum Nutritional Standards have been agreed for all grant-aided schools and childcare settings, applying to both catering and vending. The overall aim of setting nutritional standards is to help address overweight and obesity. These are food-based standards modelled on the Eatwell Guide. The Standards have recently been updated by an expert panel, taking into account the latest evidence and guidance, leading to, for example, increasing fruit and vegetables and wholegrains while decreasing the amount of saturated fat, salt and processed meats. The PHA, the Food Standards Agency (FSA) and safefood have co-funded an implementation lead with administrative support to support healthy eating in schools.You will also wish to note that work is underway to develop a successor strategic framework, and a public consultation on ‘Healthy Futures – A Strategic Framework to Prevent the Harm caused by Obesity, and Improve Diets and Levels of Physical Activity in Northern Ireland’ closed on 1 March 2024. This seeks to further build on this work and support the availability of, and demand for, healthier food in schools.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Urban Villages Initiative and a timeline for the announcement of the next round of funding.
answer_text: The Urban Villages programme has been successful for example working with communities to develop Strategic Frameworks and having a presence on the ground through Local Area Teams. There have been many positive outcomes such as new play parks, community shared spaces and learning events. A copy of the latest Programme Evaluation is available in the Assembly Library.TEO officials have developed a range of options on the future of the programme. We look forward to updating Members once we have considered how best to continue with our aim of promoting thriving places and achieving the best outcomes for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to commissioning updated research into food in schools.
answer_text: As part of the jointly led Food in Schools programme, the Public Health Agency is a member of the GENIUS School Food Network (www.geniusschoolfoodnetwork.com) which focuses on generating excellent nutrition in schools. The GENIUS network is funded by the UK Prevention Research Partnership to explore opportunities for intervention and innovation in the UK School Food System.  Membership includes academic researchers active in school food research across a range of disciplines, alongside major non-academic stakeholders in school food, as well as local government, policy development and public health bodies.The GENIUS network has a series of research priorities for school, including: What is the value to pupils’ health and well-being of a Universal Free School Meals (UFSM) vs means tested Free School Meals (FSM) policies, which may have different criteria for eligibility?How to improve uptake of FSM for those who are eligible?What are the facilitators and challenges for decision- makers to engage with and prioritise school food?How can school food systems deliver school meals to pupils while keeping within the school food standards, procurement requirements and budgets?How to implement changes to FSM policies (e.g. extended or UFSM) to achieve optimal participation and benefits?How do school food policies and interventions impact on and address inequalities in pupils from different sub-groups?How can policies create a positive food culture and affect pupils’ relationships with food?How to overcome the challenges that schools have in implementing an optimal eating environment?What innovative approaches can be used help drive uptake of healthy food consumption in schools?What is the impact of food consumed at school on pupils and to what extend can it reduce inequalities?

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to provide funding for the continuation of the Student Mental Health Service pilot programme undertaken by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 (MHS) recognises the importance of addressing student mental health and wellbeing, both through early intervention and prevention (MHS Actions 1 and 2) and by creating a dedicated resource for student mental health across tertiary education (MHS Action 8), building on the existing pilot project between Belfast Health & Social Care Trust, Queen’s University and Ulster University.The Student Mental Health Service pilot project is due to run until the end of the current academic year, however pending clarity on a future regional model, consideration is being given by the Belfast Trust and the two universities on the scope to extend the pilot for a further year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to update the Healthy Food for Healthy Outcomes food in schools policy.
answer_text: The Food in Schools Policy is a joint policy between the Department of Education and the Department of Health that has supported schools to implement a whole school approach to healthy eating and making healthy choices both at school and at home. The Food in Schools Policy has also enabled a joined-up approach through the Food in Schools Forum which brings together officials from the Departments of Education, Health and Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs with other key stakeholders such as the Education Authority, Food Standards Agency, Safefood, Public Health Agency and CCEA to share best practice and oversee the implementation of the Food in Schools Policy.There are no current plans to update the Food in Schools Policy and my officials continue to work with the Food in Schools Forum to ensure the framework remains relevant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to allowing parents and carers to split their 22.5 hours between pre-school and childcare provision.
answer_text: It is my intention to standardise the length of funded pre-school sessions to 22.5 hours per week (term time) for all children, subject to the necessary budget being made available by the Executive.  There is a strong evidence base for doing so to support children’s early learning and development.While the standardised Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) will be non-compulsory, the 22.5 hours will provide a coherent package of curricular-based learning.  It will be in the best interests of all target age children to attend regularly and for the full session to get the maximum benefit.  The childcare requirements of parents and carers is being considered as part of the wider Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to spending 10 per cent of his Department's transport budget on active travel.
answer_text: I am committed to  the positive change  the scale of  investment in active travel as outlined in the  NI Climate Act 2022 would make to the lives of people who want to have greater choice in how they travel, particularly for shorter everyday journeys, or in combination with using public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration he has given to scoping an uplift of the Educational Maintenance Allowance in line with inflation.
answer_text: A review of Further Education student support is currently being undertaken by the Department; this will include consideration of the Educational Maintenance Allowance. This work is expected to progress in the next business year, and I will consider options once they have been presented to me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of individuals who have availed of the Education Authority's School Age Mothers Programme in the last 5 years, broken down by academic year.
answer_text: The number of individuals who have availed of the Education Authority’s School Aged Mothers (SAMs) Programme during the last five academic years are shown below.  Some of these individuals have been engaged over multiple years so will be counted in each year.Academic Year Number of individuals 2023/24772022/23492021/22902020/21962019/20121

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to introducing an active travel bill.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to introduce an active travel bill. The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places challenging targets on my Department for investment in active travel and these will be set out in the Sectoral Plan for Transport in due course. The overarching Transport Strategy and transport plans currently being developed also acknowledge the importance of active travel in the sustainable transport hierarchy approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement his Department has had with counterparts in the other devolved nations and the Republic of Ireland in relation to the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care.
answer_text: My officials regularly liaise with counterparts in England, Scotland, Wales and the Republic of Ireland in relation to regulation of childcare provision. As part of the forthcoming review of the Minimum Standards, the position in each of these countries will be considered prior to recommendations for change being made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will reinstate core grant funding.

To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to reviewing the development proposal process for schools seeking to transform to integrated status to expediate, streamline and improve the process of transformation.
answer_text: Development proposals are the statutory means by which proposed significant changes to education provision can be effected, including transformations to integrated status.  Each proposal is considered on its individual merits, taking account of all the pertinent information and legislation, including the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 as it applies to proposals for schools to transform.  I have no current plans to review the development proposal process.You should note that my officials, working with partner bodies, are currently reviewing “Integration Works” which is guidance for existing schools seeking to transform to Integrated status.  Transformation is also a statutory process however the review may provide an opportunity to consider how the non-statutory aspects of the support could be improved by drawing on lessons learned from previous proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the duties and responsibilities of property management firms and letting agencies towards (i) landlords; and (ii) tenants that employ their services.
answer_text: The duties and responsibilities of the management company will normally be set out in a lease. Management companies are governed by general company law provisions, the Department for Communities has no responsibility for property management firms.Management companies are formed with the responsibility of managing a building after all of the various units have been sold. In leasehold properties such as apartments, the lease will require each owner to pay a service charge, designed to cover the common areas of the development. The management company collects that service charge which provides the funds for the maintenance of the common areas and provision of services.The duties and responsibilities of a managing agent will be subject to contractual or service agreements between the management company and the managing agents. The precise nature of the duties and responsibilities of the management company and, if present, the managing agent, can therefore vary significantly between different developments.Letting agencies are responsible for managing rental properties on behalf of landlords. These agencies act as intermediaries between the tenant and the landlord, providing a range of services that include marketing the property, finding tenants, conducting viewings, collecting rent, and managing repairs and maintenance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what funding he will commit to implementing the Strategy for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The current budget of £70,000 is sufficient to meet the anticipated implementation costs of the Strategy and of the Transformation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement he has had with the Northern Ireland Childminding Association in relation to the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care.
answer_text: My Department engages regularly with the Northern Ireland Childminding Association (NICMA) on the Department’s Minimum Standards and any issues facing childminders in relation to registration and inspection. NICMA, along with Unite the Union’s Childminders branch, will be invited to participate in the forthcoming review of the Minimum Standards to represent the views and experiences of their members.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered moving from annual inspections of childminders to inspections every three years, similar to Scotland and Wales, as part of the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care.
answer_text: As part of the review of the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care, which will commence in the coming months, we will consider the frequency of inspections.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to extending the permanent cycle path on Park Road, Belfast, to the junction with Ravenhill Road.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering options for segregated cycling facilities on the Ravenhill Road as part of the implementation of the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (BCN). Although the cycle lane Park Road cycle is not part of the BCN, an extension will be considered to connect to the Ravenhill Road scheme. There are a number of schemes on the BCN short term list at various stages of development, including the Ravenhill Road scheme. We are keen to seek the views of the public in the coming months and local elected representatives will be notified of upcoming engagement events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement he has had with businesses in relation to their support for providing the high-quality, accessible and affordable childcare to support an increase in foreign direct investment and new job opportunities.
answer_text: I have met and continue to engage with a number of Business Organisations on the importance of affordable childcare and how it will support the local economy and contribute to improved productivity.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the terms of reference; (ii) membership; and (iii) a breakdown of all meetings held in the last 12 months for (a) the Strategic Planning Group (SPG); (b) SPG Sub-group: Asylum Contingency Accommodation; (c) SPG Subgroup: Task & Finish; (d) Voluntary and Community Sector Forum; (e) NI Regional Delivery and Procurement Group; (f) the Council Engagement Group on Asylum Dispersal; (g) Move On Delivery Board; (h) the Move On Coordination Group.

Additionally, available agreed Terms of Reference have been placed in the Assembly Library and can be accessed there. It should be noted that these are kept under review and updated as required, and further Terms of Reference will be added as necessary.     The Move On Delivery Board is chaired by the Home Office and, as such, any requests for meeting information and Terms of Reference should be directed to them. The Regional Delivery Group (RDG) was chaired by the NI Strategic SMP and since its dissolution in April 2022 has been chaired by the Home Office.
