question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the consultation undertaken by his Department with each of the groups listed in Section 9 (2) (e) (i) of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 with regards to the development of the Action Plan for implementing the Act.
answer_text: The Department was required to publish a Strategy and associated Action Plan within six months of the Act coming into force.  The primary means of consultation was through Integrated Education Act governance structures: the Integrated Education Act Steering and Working groups. These groups included representatives from Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education, Integrated Education Fund, Controlled Schools Support Council, Association of Principal Teachers in Integrated Schools and Integrated AlumNI and other educational stakeholders.A public consultation on the Action Plan ran between 31 August 2023 and 30 November 2023. This period was chosen rather than immediately after publication during the Assembly election period or over the summer period to allow interested stakeholders to participate. The consultation process was publicised across social media and included two open online engagement sessions. All educational partners were asked to share the consultation survey widely and encourage active participation. This provided an opportunity for stakeholders within all the groups listed in the Act to have their say.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of all meetings of the (i) Integrated Education Steering Group; and (ii) Integrated Education Strategy Working Group, in the last two years.
answer_text: A breakdown of the meetings requested is set out below:Steering Group MeetingsWorking Group Meetings08/12/202211/01/202323/01/202301/02/202328/02/202310/05/202320/04/202311/01/202427/09/202321/02/202427/11/2023Outcomes Based Accountability Workshops – Steering Group and Working Group members attended.26/06/202312/03/202422/03/2024

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration has been given by his Department to assessing demand for integrated education through the balloting of all schools to assess the desire amongst parents for integrated education.
answer_text: Balloting of parents is part of the legal process of Transformation and is defined and described in the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989.www.legislation.gov.uk/nisi/1989/2406/contents. Legally, the Transformation Process can be formally initiated in one of two ways: the Board of Governors decides by resolution passed at a meeting of the Board to hold a ballot of parents; or the Board of Governors receives a signed request to initiate a ballot of parents from at least 20 percent of parents of pupils registered at the school. Guidance on the transformation process can be found at the following link. Integration-Works-Transforming-your-School-December-2017 (education-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of cancellations of the 6a bus service in Belfast in the last 12 months; and (ii) the reason for each cancellation.
answer_text: The scheduling and cancellation of Metro bus services is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact of the process of academic selection and post-primary transfer on the mental health and well-being of children and young people.
answer_text: Post-primary schools set their own admissions criteria and decide whether or not to use academic selection as part of that process if they are over-subscribed. The Education Authority operates the process that facilitates the transfer from primary to post-primary school of all pupils, with schools selecting children on the basis of their admissions criteria.My Department has no role in the operation or funding of the transfer test which is organised by private providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to upgrade Balmoral train station, to incentivise those travelling to a rebuilt Casement Park for major events to use rail as their mode of transport.
answer_text: Public transport services are frequently enhanced to facilitate spectators attending various major events in Belfast. The platforms at Balmoral and Finaghy train stations, which are a short distance to Casement Park, have previously been extended to facilitate six train car length platforms and therefore to cater for higher passenger demand. In addition, Casement Park is also currently served by Glider and Metro services operating a combined seven-minute frequency at peak and eight minutes off peak.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider installing a pedestrian crossing on Stranmillis Embankment to allow for safe passage between the cycle lane and Botanic Park.
answer_text: My Department previously considered a proposal for the provision of a pedestrian crossing facility at this location.  A detailed assessment carried out at that time, in 2019, concluded that the proposal did not rank highly enough on the priority list to be included in a works programme. As 5 years has elapsed from the date of the last survey, officials will arrange for a new assessment to be carried out and write to you directly with the result.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to upgrade Adelaide train station to better serve the National Stadium at Windsor Park and reduce traffic in the area on match days.
answer_text: Translink is currently extending the platforms at Adelaide station and when complete later this year will facilitate the stopping of six car train sets.  Subject to the availability of funding, Translink also plan to commence work to improve the pedestrian link between Adelaide Halt and the National Stadium during 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what resources will be allocated by his Department to the development of the anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland.		I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy to ensure it takes account of the current delivery environment and Executive and Ministerial priorities. 		Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Audit Office Report on Child Poverty in Northern Ireland, published on 12 March 2024.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the NIAO’s recent review of the Executive’s Child Poverty Strategy.As the Child Poverty Strategy was an Executive Strategy, there will be considerations for all departments, and I will make sure to raise relevant issues with my Ministerial colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the findings of the Northern Ireland Audit Office Report on Child Poverty in Northern Ireland, published on 12 March 2024.
answer_text: The Child Poverty Strategy is led by the Department for Communities and consequently it will be for that Department to comment fully on the NIAO reports findings. In terms of the key points mentioned within the report, I would concur with the sentiment that early intervention / preventative measures are an important part of the range of policies needed to reduce child poverty of which Sure Start and a Childcare strategy are two critical parts which require significant and ongoing investment.The report is also critical of the lack of joined up government. Close collaboration between government departments is mutually beneficial. Work is on-going across all nine Departments on a “Test and Learn” programme which has been endorsed by the NICS Board and which is designed to encourage greater collaboration across government, local government and the voluntary and community sector. This programme is jointly led by the Executive Office and the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail funding provided for youth services by his Department and the Education Authority in South Belfast, broken down by (i) financial year; and (ii) District Electoral Area, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: Funding provided by the Department of Education (via the Education Authority) for the last 5 years is included in the table below. These figures relate to the South Belfast area of Belfast City Council.YearEA RecurrentEA CapitalComments19-20£1,151,866£175,866 20-21£1,501,463£139,054Includes £196k Covid Relief Funding21-22£1,879,160£178,214Includes £462k of Covid Relief funding/DE Capital/MIS Scheme22-23£1,602,575£203,435 23-24£1,542,597£1,468Current year still in processThe Education Authority do not hold information by constituency or District Electoral Area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to protect and enhance youth services in South Belfast.
answer_text: I recognise and value the impact of effective youth services, not just on education but also its contribution to a range of priorities for government including health, community development, justice, and the economy.I have recently approved an interim DE youth work budget for 2024-25 of almost £36m which is ringfenced to ensure continued needs-based youth work across Northern Ireland, including South Belfast.  Within the existing 2023-24 budget, funding was provided for five EA statutory youth centres in South Belfast to support youth services, namely: Belvoir Youth Centre (full-time), Taughmonagh Youth Centre (part-time), Nubia Youth Centre (full-time), Charter Youth Centre (full-time) and Donegall Pass Youth Centre (part-time).  In addition, five funding awards (details in the table below) were developed and subsequently awarded to voluntary youth sector providers in the South Belfast. This is to meet the identified needs of young people and support youth services in the area.Award CodeProvision/AreaCore23-LA-BS-001  Core Youth Work Provision/Lower Ormeau Area       £96,82323-LA-BS-002  Core Youth Work Provision/Markets Area  £96,823  23-LA-BS-003  Core Youth Work Provision/Upper Ormeau Area       £96,82323-LP-BS-001 Targeted Youth Work Programmes - 6 Health & Wellbeing; 4 Learning & Achieving; 6 CRED and Inclusion/Botanic        £24,91523-LP-BS-002 2 Street Based Youth Work sessions per week for 26 weeks of the year + 4 Living in Safety and Stability Sessions/Botanic         £18,097  Total Voluntary Sector Funding Allocated to DEA £333,481  As set out in the Department’s youth work policy planning, funding, and delivery of youth work at both regional and local level is based on an assessment of need which includes significant stakeholder engagement to ensure that local plans for South Belfast meet the bespoke needs of its children and young people. The Local Assessment of Need 2023-26 for South and West Belfast  was recently reviewed and updated following further stakeholder consultation to reflect emerging as well as current needs of young people. As a result, additional funding opportunities in 2024/25 are planned to support targeted youth work provision. These are expected to be released in the coming week, pending approval by EA Board via an open application process for Voluntary Youth Sector organisations.To continue protecting and enhancing youth services across Northern Ireland (and within South Belfast), my Department has commenced a review of the existing youth work policy, Priorities for Youth, with a view to developing a new policy late 2025 / early 2026. To ensure the voice of children and young people are central to the process, the Department is taking a co-design approach in this process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what consideration they have given to the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration's report, An inspection of contingency asylum accommodation for families with children in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: We welcome the publication of the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration (ICIBI) report, following an inspection of Contingency Accommodation here, that was jointly requested by officials from The Executive Office (TEO) and Department of Health.  In particular, we welcome the Home Office response to and full acceptance of Recommendations 1 and 4, given safeguarding and the potential vulnerability of people living in Contingency Accommodation have been priority areas of concern locally. Whilst we recognise that the 5 recommendations within the ICIBI report were made for Home Office, TEO officials will continue to engage with Home Office officials on their planned approach to implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to his Department's responsibility for the implementation of child protection policy, for his assessment of the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration's report, An inspection of contingency asylum accommodation for families with children in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration report on Contingency Accommodation in Northern Ireland which was jointly requested by the Department of Health and The Executive Office.  While the recommendations within the report are for the Home Office, my Department, together with the relevant statutory agencies (including Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland), will continue to support MEARS and the Home office to ensure that safeguarding policies and procedures in contingency accommodation are robust and are in keeping with standards of safeguarding practice, policy and procedure in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for their assessment of key finding 1.2 of the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration's report, An inspection of contingency asylum accommodation for families with children in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working with key delivery partners to ensure that the length of stay of those living in contingency accommodation is as short as possible.   Since the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration report was completed, a Data Sharing Agreement (DSA) has been established between the contracted accommodation providers MEARS and statutory services. This DSA supports the efficient flow of information to the HSC Trusts and allows them to meet their statutory obligations in meeting the health and social care needs of individuals seeking international protection in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what actions they will be taking to address the concerns raised by the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration's report, An inspection of contingency asylum accommodation for families with children in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The key concerns set out in the report relate primarily to non-devolved issues for which we have no remit to take action. Whilst TEO does not have a statutory responsibility for the provision of services to asylum seekers, the concerns raised in this respect have informed the work the Department co-ordinates through the Strategic Planning Group (Refugees and Asylum Seekers) and its previously established subgroup on Contingency Accommodation. For example, work has been completed to put in place Information Sharing Agreements between Mears and agencies such as Health and Social Care Trusts, and family help models have been rolled out in all three relevant Trusts. We have also continued to raise the impact and need for further funding to meet increasing pressures as a result of Home Office policy. Whilst we recognise that the 5 recommendations within the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration (ICIBI) report were made for Home Office, we will continue to engage with Home Office on their planned approach to implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what plans he will put in place to ensure that all children with special educational needs and disabilities will have access to appropriate school places for the 2024-25 academic year.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what engagement they have had in relation to the provision of housing for refugees in the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister of Health what percentage of the overall budget for mental health services is allocated to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services.

To ask the Minister of Health how the handover of relationship and sexuality education commissioning from the Public Health Agency to the Department of Education will be handled to ensure there are no gaps in provision.
answer_text: There is no planned handover of Relationships and Sexuality Education commissioning from the Public Health Agency to the Department of Education. The Department of Education has responsibility for education within schools, whereas the Public Health Agency currently commissions Relationships and Sexuality Education in the community.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7470/22-27, how many of the 3042 days of the sick leave taken by employees were stress-related.
answer_text: The Education Authority advise that this information is currently not available due to the implementation of a new HR/Payroll system and the associated backlog in the keying of sickness absence certificates.  Work is progressing to clear the backlog and it is expected that this information will be available in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the cost of the formal offer on teachers’ pay made to the five main teaching unions for (a) 2021; (b) 2022; and (c) 2023; and (ii) how his Department will meet this pay uplift on a recurring basis.
answer_text: (i) The annual recurring cost of the formal offer for the teachers’ pay increase is:   (a) £12.8 million for the increase with effect from 1 September 2021.   (b) £64.8 million for the increase with effect from 1 September 2022.   (c) £88.4 million for the increase with effect from 1 September 2023(ii) The Finance Minister has not yet confirmed 2024-25 budget allocations, however it is clear that additional funding will be required to cover the recurring cost of this pay uplift.  My department has already submitted bids to the Department of Finance which include the funding required to meet all estimated pay costs in 2024-25, including the recurring element of this pay uplift. I will continue to emphasise this issue to the Finance Minister, as I work with her and other Executive colleagues in seeking to secure the necessary funding for Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7470/22-27, for an outline of the current vacancies, broken down by (i) grade; and (ii) job title, within the teams involved in the Education Authority Statutory Assessment and Review process.
answer_text: ANSWERThe table below contains information on the current vacancies within the teams involved in the Education Authority Statutory Assessment and Review process.Grade Job TitleNumber of VacanciesAssistant Principal OfficerSEN Link Officer2 Senior Executive OfficerSupport Officer3Executive OfficerExecutive Officer2Senior Clerical OfficerSenior Clerical Officer19

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for clarification on how his Department understands the term early intervention.
answer_text: My department’s understanding of the term early intervention is consistent with the definition in the Children and Young People’s Strategy, namely that it means “both intervening in the early years of children’s lives to give them the best start in life, and intervening early and as soon as possible to address problems that are emerging for children and young people at any point in their lives.”

I have several proposals which require careful consideration. I am aware of the importance of providing clarity to the proposers and the schools that are subject of proposals and in that respect, I will not seek to delay my determination any longer than is necessary.You will appreciate that I cannot comment individually on any proposal which has not yet come before me for a decision. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the term family support is understood and defined by his Department.
answer_text: My Department recognises family support as the provision of a range of supports and services to ensure that all children and young people are given the opportunity to develop to their full potential. Family support services are delivered to families across independent, statutory, voluntary and community sectors, generally in their own homes and communities. The primary focus is on early support and help, aiming to improve outcomes for all children, young people and their families, paying particular attention to those who are vulnerable or at risk. Some key characteristics of family support are:Support workers and family members working in partnership in the planning, delivery and evaluation of services;Strengths-based assessments approaches, which are mindful of resilience as a characteristic of families’ lives;A clear focus on outcomes for children and young people;Interventions that strengthen informal support networks;Support which is accessible and flexible;Promotion of social inclusion; andA combined application of theory, skills and reflective practice. The nature and duration of support will be determined by the level of need within the family, which will also determine whether the duties placed on Health and Social Care Trusts under the Children (NI) Order 1995 are engaged.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the difference between pre-school education and childcare.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) is an educational programme targeted at children in their pre-school year and delivered free at the point of delivery to every target aged child whose parents want it.  All funded PSEP settings including statutory and voluntary and private settings, are expected to adhere to the ‘Curricular Guidance for Pre-School Education’ published by CCEA.   The Department of Health sets out the minimum standards for registered childcare in Northern Ireland.  All registered childcare providers in Northern Ireland, including childminders and day nurseries, must adhere to the standards and are inspected against these on an annual basis.I recognise that high quality childcare will have an educational benefit and time spent in pre-school education has the potential to contribute to parents’ childcare requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on work being undertaken to prepare a report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015, pursuant to Section 5(1) of the Act.
answer_text: The first report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 has been prepared. It will be submitted for consideration by the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the length of waiting lists for the Education Welfare Service.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 7475/22-27.The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 8th March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the underlying factors behind school absences; and (ii) how these factors are impacting different groups of children.
answer_text: There are a range of factors causing pupil absence at school.   These can include illness, family problems, negative feelings towards school and so on.  For authorised absences, illness (which includes anxiety and emotionally based school avoidance) remains the most common reason for absence in all schools. Whilst we must have a universal approach to supporting pupil attendance, I am mindful that there are specific vulnerable groups of children and young people who may require additional support.  These groups can include those entitled to Free School Meals, pupils with Special Educational Needs, newcomer and Traveller children and Children Looked After or Care Experienced Children. I will be consulting on an "Attendance Matters" strategy in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7104/22-27, to detail the (i) terms of reference; (ii) membership; and (iii) forward work programme for the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: My officials are currently working to establish this Task and Finish Group.  All departments have been invited to allocate appropriate staff.  Terms of Reference and a workplan will be agreed by the Group when it is formed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW  6941/22-27, (i) for an update on when the review of residents’ parking schemes will be published; and (ii) for a timeline of when you will consider its findings.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete and the report will be published when I have considered the findings, which I expect to be in the coming months

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 5570/22-27, to detail the key provisions he intends to include in the forthcoming Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: I have previously stated that I plan to bring forward a Good Jobs Bill to help deliver my economic vision.  I have already announced that I plan to replace zero hours contracts with contracts that provide flexibility and protect workers’ rights, and strengthen the role of trade unions, particularly in low paying sectors. I have also confirmed that I intend to consider a range of options to help support working families as well as helping to promote a good work life balance. There are a considerable range of other potential policies that I will want to consider as part of the good jobs agenda.  In that regard my officials are presently developing options and will engage with stakeholders on a co-design basis, as well as hold a public consultation.  There will be full engagement with stakeholders and the Economy Committee prior to agreeing the content of the primary legislation for this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact on staff retention of delaying an uplift in funding for Sure Start ahead of the April rise in the minimum wage.
answer_text: I have met recently with many of the Sure Start managers who have conveyed the potential impact of budget uncertainty on retaining staff within the programme.  To provide budget clarity I have therefore confirmed an interim 2024-25 Sure Start funding allocation and aim to confirm final allocations as soon as I have confirmation of my Departmental budget from the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they intend to manage support for refugee resettlement.
answer_text: We intend to bring a Refugee Integration Strategy encompassing thematic delivery plans to Executive colleagues for agreement in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what engagement they have had with (i) the Home Office; (ii) Mears; and (iii) the Housing Executive to address issues with housing and education for asylum seekers who receive refugee status.
answer_text: The Department engages regularly at regional and local level on issues arising from the granting of positive asylum decisions. This includes through the fortnightly Move On Delivery Board chaired by Home Office and attended by TEO and NIHE officials, alongside a range of stakeholders from across the regions. At local level, we have established a multi-agency Move On Coordination Group which includes members from TEO, Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE), Department for Education (DE), Department of Health (DoH) and the Education Authority (EA). The Group meets fortnightly to coordinate the devolved response, scoping impact of the move on policy and seeking solutions at both regional and case-based level, including on issues related to housing and education.The move on process and impacts have also been discussed at a focused meeting of the Strategic Planning Group on Refugees and Asylum Seekers (SPG (RAS)).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have given further consideration to improvements that can be made to the transparency of governance in relation to asylum seekers support since assuming the functions of the Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) has agreed with Home Office to take on some of the Strategic Migration Partnership (SMP) functions.  These relate to engagement and coordination, encouraging multi-agency support for asylum seekers, and ensure ongoing engagement with Home Office on matters which impact locally.  These fit with TEO’s co-ordination role.The purpose of Strategic Migration Partnerships is to facilitate and enable collaboration between central and local government, civil society and regional and local stakeholders for the benefit of both migrants and the wider community. TEO achieves this through the Strategic Planning Group on Refugees and Asylum Seekers and through its Council Engagement Group. In order to ensure that voices from civic society can feed into this programme structure, TEO has recently established a Voluntary and Community Sector Forum which includes representative from organisations supporting and working directly with refugees and asylum seekers. We will keep our approach and role under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what additional support his Department can put in place to support young deaf children to access early education and childcare opportunities.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Sensory Service provides support to deaf children and their families from the time of diagnosis, often from birth, through all phases of education.  The service provides a comprehensive package of support in the early years such as:Home visits and family support to help them to understand their child’s hearing loss, support their child’s development of language and communication through play-based activities, monitor progress and set language and listening goals;Advice on communication choices and early literacy experience;Liaison with health professionals;Training for children, parents and school staff;Management of equipment such as hearing aids and cochlear implants;Assistance to pre-school settings and for transitions into pre-schools settings.The effectiveness of the sensory service is being considered as part of my department’s End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN).In addition, my department provides funding for childcare during holiday periods for children with hearing loss/hearing impairment through the Holiday Grant Scheme for Children with a Disability, part of the Bright Start School Age Childcare Grant Scheme.The development and implementation of an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy is a priority for me.  Provision for children with additional needs is being considered as part of the development of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures he will put in place to improve the level of compliance with the Addressing Bullying in Schools (Northern Ireland) Act 2016.
answer_text: The Department funds the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority (EA). ABSIT support schools to successfully implement the Act.  The Department plans to complete a new research study in order to gather up to date data on the nature and prevalence of bullying type behaviour and to baseline the impact of the Act in all schools. The Department will commission this research in the forthcoming financial year, dependent on the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to removing the term newcomer from use within education policy and replacing it with more sensitive terminology.
answer_text: The Department commenced a review of its Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy in October 2017 in response to the growing number and increasing diversity of newcomer pupils arriving in Northern Ireland.  In 2019, the Department held a public consultation on its review of the policy however, work was temporarily suspended in March 2020 due to the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic.  The consultation did not raise any major concerns in relation to the appropriateness of the term “newcomer”.  In 2022, work recommenced with policy proposals prepared for consideration of an incoming Minister.  My officials are now building on the previous consultation and revisiting policy proposals to account for the current context, with a view to presenting proposals for my consideration.  As part of that exercise, I will ask my officials to examine the continuing use of the term “newcomer”, with a view to considering whether there is a need for the Department to introduce alternative terminology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures or metrics are being used by his Department to establish the level of compliance with the Addressing Bullying in Schools (Northern Ireland) Act 2016.
answer_text: Under the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), a school’s Board of Governors is responsible for the implementation and schools are required to record data on incidents or alleged incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation and their outcome. The Act does not include the requirement for schools to share this data with the Department.  Given this context, the Department is not currently in a position to establish the level of compliance with the Act in schools in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration she has given to providing early years and childcare settings with a reduction in their rates bills.
answer_text: 141 registered childcare providers receive rate relief through the Small Business Rate Relief scheme, this has been extended by my Department for 2024/25.I am committed to ensuring we continue to develop the rating system to ensure it is fair, equitable and progressive and that it is aligned with the Executive’s economic vision, expanding our taxbase and supporting businesses to grow and create jobs.Alongside the findings of the recent Revenue Raising consultation, I will be assessing strategic policy options within the rating system, including the current rate reliefs.The Department of Education is the policy competent department leading on the Executive’s Childcare Strategy. Any new additional rate support would be provided directly to childcare providers, and it would be for the Department of Education to determine if that form of support aligns with the Strategy brought forward to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to commission comprehensive research on the forms of bullying taking place in schools.
answer_text: The Department published its first research study into the scale and nature of bullying in schools in 2002.  Subsequent similar research studies were completed in 2007 and 2011. I recognise that the nature of bullying has changed greatly since the last research was completed and a new study would therefore allow the Department to gather up to date data on the nature and prevalence of bullying type behaviour.  The Department will commission this research in the forthcoming financial year, dependent on the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to introduce a national curriculum for early years settings.
answer_text: All funded pre-school education settings participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP), including statutory and voluntary and private settings, are expected to adhere to the ‘Curricular Guidance for Pre-School Education’ published by CCEA.  The curricular guidance is used by the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) when evaluating the quality of provision and the outcomes for learners in all funded pre-school settings.In addition, one of the potential actions being considered for inclusion within an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy is the development of a ‘Quality Framework’, co-designed with the sector, for use in all early learning and childcare settings.  It would take account of the ‘Curricular guidance for Pre-School Education’, and ‘The Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12’.  Executive agreement and the allocation of appropriate resources would be required to take this forward.
