question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to enabling non-statutory pre-school settings to directly refer children to educational psychologists.
answer_text: Significant work has been completed to progress standardisation of the Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) which has included a detailed scoping study of all funded pre-school education settings.  The final report is due to be submitted to the department by the end of March 2024 and will enable my department to establish options and costs for the phased roll out of a universal, standardised, funded pre-school session length for every child whose parents want it, including options to address relevant inconsistencies and improvements in the pre-school sector.  Interim feedback indicates that issues regarding provision and support for children with special educational needs (SEN) in non-statutory pre-school settings have been raised by settings during the scoping study and I intend that these will be considered in the context of pre-school standardisation.  However, all work in progressing pre-school standardisation will require agreement and extensive investment by Executive colleagues.In tandem, as part of the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN, the department is considering the range of Education Authority (EA) support services available to all schools and educational settings, including referral pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how the Education Authority (EA) tracks the number of non-teaching staff employed by the EA and schools; and (ii) how many non-teaching staff are employed by the EA and schools as at 29 February 2024.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has provided the following information:The EA records new employees and leavers on a daily basis on the EA One system.  The EA One system is the database from which the payroll is run and it records all non-teaching staff employed by position name, working location and grade.The EA employs 40,763 non-teaching staff as at 29 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all planned road maintenance projects in South Belfast.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network. The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Belfast City Council and the Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council areas were presented to Council members and can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf and https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-lisburn-castlereagh-city-council-oct23.pdf.The resurfacing programmes along with other programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Councils in due course.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the definition of shared education.
answer_text: Section 2 (2) of the Shared Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 defines Shared Education as:Shared education means the education together of—(a)	those of different religious belief, including reasonable numbers of both Protestant and Roman Catholic children or young persons; and(b)	those who are experiencing socio-economic deprivation and those who are not,which is secured by the working together and co-operation of two or more relevant providers.Section 2(3) defines a “relevant provider” as:In subsection (2) “relevant provider” means a person providing—(a)	education at a grant-aided school, or(b)	services of any kind (including youth services) which provide educational benefit to children or young persons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support he can provide to Sólás in South Belfast to ensure the continued provision of therapeutic counselling services.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) currently have a contract with SOLAS to deliver the EMBRACE Home Support Programme to address information and practical support gaps experienced by parents of children with autism or disability.This programme reduces isolation leading to improvements in mental health and wellbeing of families.  The PHA have advised that SOLAS do not provide therapeutic counselling services. A full range of services they do provide is listed on their website at https://www.solasbt7.com/education-programmes

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to the introduction of tougher sanctions and policies to deter the provision of illegal, unregistered childminding.
answer_text: My Department will continue to raise awareness of the risks of using unregistered childminding and draw attention to the tax and other benefits of using registered childminding. The developing Early Education and Childcare Strategy will also assist in tackling this issue, by promoting the value of registered childminding, as well as making childcare more affordable and accessible for parents who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement he has had with the Minister of Education on the role of high-quality, affordable childcare in delivering the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to reallocating funding to ensure the delivery of all integrated school capital projects in the Fresh Start Programme.
answer_text: All Fresh Start projects in construction contract will be completed. I have placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in design and planning.  The projects will proceed to construction when planning is complete and affordable within the wider education budget context.All of the projects have been submitted as priority capital bids for 2024-25 to the Department of Finance and I trust all Executive parties will support this bid for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what additional support he will be providing Sure Starts to help them meet the rise in costs resulting from the increase in the minimum wage by the UK government.
answer_text: As I have outlined previously, stabilisation of Sure Start will be an important first step in the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) strategy.  My Department has been engaging closely with the Department of Health which administers the Sure Start programme, to identify and quantify factors relevant to stabilisation, including costs. However, as I have also previously said, stabilisation of Sure Start will be subject to the Executive making available the required funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to reviewing and updating the Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) Workforce Review 2019 - 2029 to account for the recent increase in demand for access to SLT.
answer_text: I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029 which will require increasing the number of undergraduate training places commissioned each year by 12 to reach the recommendation of 40.A budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set for 2024/25 and accordingly I am unable to make any commitment to expand commissioned places at this time.  I will continue to make the case for this essential investment in our future workforce although I expect that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.My Department’s Chief AHP Officer has begun the process of re-establishing the Advisory Committee for Allied Health Professions (ACAHP) a formal, Ministerially appointed advisory body.  A workforce subcommittee will also be established to oversee implementation of completed AHP strategic workforce reviews and to consider how to address demands upon the Speech and Language Therapy workforce.  Alternative routes to qualification, further development of recruitment/employment, and the commissioning of postgraduate education, training and skills development on a regional basis, will all be priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he has given to introducing government-owned guarantor schemes to ensure international university students and staff can access affordable accommodation.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for higher education policy in relation to teaching and research in the north of Ireland. Student and staff accommodation – whether university halls of residence or private rental housing – is a matter between the individual and their landlord. Where a university is the landlord, it is responsible for its own business practices in respect of such provision.		However I am open to suggestions that will improve access to affordable accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to the inclusion of modules on speech, language and communication needs in (i) postgraduate certification in education programmes; and (ii) training for classroom assistants.
answer_text: Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes in Northern Ireland (including post-graduate certificate in education (PGCE) courses) are delivered by the four Higher Education Institutions (HEIs). It is for the individual ITE providers to decide on the precise content of these programmes and the individual modules within them. Courses are designed to ensure, amongst other things, that new teachers achieve a set of professional competences identified by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI), and that they prepare teachers for the effective delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. It should also be noted that ITE represents the initial stages only of a teacher’s professional learning and teachers will continue to learn and develop professionally throughout their careers, led by both the needs of their pupils and their school. The Department for the Economy has responsibility for Further Education Colleges who provide qualification courses for aspiring classroom assistants. However, the Language and Communication Service (LCS) within the Education Authority provide a range of professional online courses which are universally available and suitable for classroom assistants.The LCS also work to support children and young people with a Statement of SEN for language and communication needs/developmental language delay (DLD) who have additional adult assistant hours. The LCS works alongside the SEN Classroom Assistants to help build their knowledge of language and communication needs and to help them develop the skills to provide support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many unregistered childminders have been investigated in each Health and Social Care Trust, in the last 12 months; and (ii) to detail the outcomes of these investigations.
answer_text: A total of 35 reports of unregistered childminding were brought to the attention of HSC Trusts in the previous 12 months. The table below provides a breakdown of this figure by Trust area and the subsequent outcomes. TrustNumber of notified casesOutcomeNorthern7Three were subsequently registered, one did not require registration under the Children (NI) Order 1995 and the remaining three cases were unsubstantiated. Western146 of the 14 reports resulted in Applications to become Registered Childminders/Assistants. The remaining 8 cases were assessed by social workers and closed for one or more of the following reasons:Lack of evidence of childminding;Minding family members; andNot requiring registration under the Children (NI) Order 1995.Belfast 3There was no evidence of childminding having taken place in 2 cases. In the third case the individual agreed to cease childminding and consider registration. South Eastern 1The individual admitted they had minded a friend's child on a short-term basis, unaware of the legislation prohibiting this, but were no longer minding at the time of investigation. Advice was provided on registration if the individual wished to childmind in the future.Southern10Each individual’s home was visited (unannounced) to determine if they were minding children and if they met the criteria for requiring to be registered.  Where an individual was found to be childminding, the children’s parents were contacted to confirm either that they were close family members or to that unregistered childminding was taking place.  Where individuals were found to be childminding in accordance with the Children (NI) Order 1995, the Trust’s Early Years Team informed them that they must cease minding the children immediately or register with the Trust. One referral proceeded to registration with three currently in the process of registration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of new childminders that have registered; and (ii) the number of childminders that have left the profession, in the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A total of 109 childminders registered with Health and Social Care Trusts in the last 12 months, while a total of 225 left the register. A breakdown of these numbers by Health and Social Care Trust is provided in the table below.Table 1. Number i) new childminders that have registered, and ii) childminders that have left the profession, between 28th Feb 2023 - 27th Feb 2024, by HSC TrustHSC Trust(i) The number of new childminders that have registered in the past 12 months(ii) The number of childminders that have left the profession in the past 12 monthsBelfast122Northern4366South Eastern2351Southern1757Western2529Source: HSC Trusts

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to address speeding on (i) Onslow Parade; and (ii) Ravenhill Park, South Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that Onslow Parade and Ravenhill Park, Belfast have been assessed for the introduction of traffic calming measures.  Following the assessment, preparation of a feasibility design  is now underway. It is anticipated that a formal consultation could take place late 2024. Further progress on this matter will be subject to funding and satisfactory completion of the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many days of sick leave were taken by Education Authority staff who are involved in the SEN statementing process, in the last 12 months; and (ii) what plans are in place to cover these absences.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has provided the following information from their records of 138 full time staff involved specifically in the Statutory Assessment and Review process: A total of 3042 days of sick leave were taken by Education Authority staff working in this area, in the last 12 months.  This includes a number of staff who were absent from work as a result of long-term medical conditions.The EA Statutory Assessment and Review Service work collaboratively across the region to assist in areas where staffing levels are depleted, as far as practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration he has given to introducing government-owned guarantor schemes to ensure international university students and staff can access affordable accommodation.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for higher education policy in relation to teaching and research in the north of Ireland. Student and staff accommodation – whether university halls of residence or private rental housing – is a matter between the individual and their landlord. Where a university is the landlord, it is responsible for its own business practices in respect of such provision.		However I am open to suggestions that will improve access to affordable accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail plans for the expansion of Family Support Hubs.
answer_text: There are 29 Family Support Hubs in operation across Northern Ireland. The Family Support Hubs have provided 100% geographical coverage since 2016, enabling children and their families to access early intervention services across Northern Ireland.The number of families referred to Family Support Hubs has continued to rise year on year. In 2022/2023, 9147 families were referred to Hubs across the region. Hubs do not have a service development role nor do they directly provide services to families. They are a signposting and referral service which promote and coordinate an interagency response to support vulnerable families who do not meet the threshold for statutory social services. The Department has established a Children’s Services Reform Process to reform Children’s Services. One of the workstreams from this process will be looking specifically at the area of family support. This will include the Family Support Hubs. Potential expansion of the capacity, role and remit of Family Support Hubs may be considered within that workstream. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail plans for increasing funding available for therapeutic counselling services provided by non-statutory organisations.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) invest approximately £900k in the Self Harm Intervention Programme (SHIP).  The Self-Harm Intervention Service supports individuals from age 11+ to receive support in relation to self-harm.  Referrals are made to SHIP by HSC Trusts, mental health services through a formal referral pathway.  The service is delivered on the ground by Community and Voluntary organisations across Northern Ireland. The PHA invest a further £3.2 million in the Lifeline service.  This is service is available to anyone living in Northern Ireland.  Citizens can call Lifeline free of charge on 0808 808 8000 if they are experiencing distress or despair. The Lifeline helpline is open 24 hours a day, every day of the year.  Those that phone Lifeline can speak to a professionally trained counsellor who will listen and provide help and support needed in confidence.  Lifeline counsellors are experienced in dealing with many issues including trauma, suicide, self- harm, abuse, depression and anxiety.The  PHA invested a further £40K in 20223/24 in health improvement support to the Aisling Centre, Enniskillen and £4K to the Koram Centre, Strabane to address pressures in their counselling service demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on when Bright Start School Age Childcare funding will be allocated for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Decisions have not yet been taken in relation to the available budget for the 2024-2025 financial year.  I appreciate that groups supported through the Bright Start School Age Childcare Grant Scheme are anxious to have an indication of next year’s budget and my Department will endeavour to provide information, as soon as is practicably possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to introduce a phone option for complaints to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Belfast HSC Trust Complaints Department has both a general telephone number for use by members of the public, and personal direct line telephone numbers for each member of the complaints department staff.Regrettably, there have been periods when staffing resource within the complaints department has meant that there is insufficient capacity to cover the general telephone line. This has recently been the case, however resource has been secured and it is planned that the general telephone line will be restored from Monday 11th March 2024.On raising a complaint with the Trust, every complainant is provided with the direct line phone number(s) for staff who will be dealing with their complaint. These direct line numbers (rather than the general complaints telephone number) are then used by complainants for the duration of their complaint investigation.Direct phone numbers for individual members of complaints staff have remained operational at all times.In the event that the general telephone line is unavailable, an automated message directs complainants to a number of alternative means by which to raise their concerns:- By e-mail- By post- Via the Trust websiteThe automated message also provides contact details for the Patient Client Council if complainants require assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to the Northern Ireland Skills Strategy, what engagement he has had with the Minister of Education regarding the provision of more opportunities for early years and childcare practitioners to develop skills and gain qualifications.
answer_text: High quality, affordable childcare is a priority for the Executive – supporting skills and education pathways for those working in the sector will be key to this. I have recently corresponded with the Minister of Education in response to an Executive Paper on the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, stating my support for the work in this area. I have given my commitment to provide operational input to the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group to develop and successfully implement the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to develop and grow the facilities available at Knockbracken Healthcare Park, South Belfast.
answer_text: Ownership of the Knockbracken Healthcare Park is split between Belfast Trust and the Department of Health.Belfast Trust has confirmed that there are no major plans to develop its portion of the site and there is no health development planned on any lands owned by the Department of Health at this site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what notification was given to those businesses negatively impacted by the road and footway closures at Bradbury Place, Belfast, on the evening of Saturday 10 February 2024; and (ii) what compensation is available for loss of business.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department issued a contractor with a lane closure permit to facilitate the dismantling of a large crane in Bradbury Place on 2nd February 2024. This permit was in place from 10pm on Saturday 10th February to 12 noon on Sunday 11th February 2024. While giving permission to implement a lane closure rests with my Department, it is the responsibility of the contractor to notify businesses in the local area about their work and deal with any compensation issues that may arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement he has had, or plans to have, with registered childminders on the review of minimum standards for childminding and day care.
answer_text: I am committed to involving all key stakeholders in the review of the Department’s Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care, including registered childminders and bodies who represent them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will facilitate a more joined-up approach between the Department and community childcare providers.
answer_text: My answers to your questions [AQW 6263/22-27, AQW 5437/22-27, AQW 5566/22-27 and AQW 5869/22-27] detail the current position on the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, including recent and planned engagement with childcare providers.   My officials are considering what approach would best support the next phase of the work, which is likely to require more focused, thematic, co-design activity.  Input from community childcare providers will be essential.  My Department already works in close partnership with many community and voluntary early years groups to deliver core Department of Education funded programmes including the Pathway Fund and the Pre-school Education Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on (i) what consideration he has given to the draft report on the review of residents' parking schemes; and (ii) when requests for residents' parking schemes will be considered by his Department.
answer_text: A review of residents’ parking schemes has been completed and the report will be published when I have considered the review findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assessment has been made by his Department of the potential impact on childcare providers of the rise in the minimum wage in April 2024.
answer_text: Minimum wage rates are a universal government intervention that apply to all employers across the United Kingdom.  While the rise in April 2024 will be good news for employees, it will undoubtedly increase the financial pressures on employers in several sectors, including childcare.  My officials, in conjunction with colleagues in other Departments are considering what short-term measures might be appropriate to ease the range of cost pressures which childcare settings and parents are facing.  Over the coming weeks, I plan to meet providers myself, to hear from them directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what policies and protections are in place for lone workers within health and social care settings who have faced false allegations from service users.
answer_text: HSC organisations are required to deal with all complaints in line with the Departmental guidance on complaints procedures. Standard 4 of this guidance requires organisations to support both complainants and staff throughout the process. The guidance may be viewed athttps://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hsc-complaints-standards-and-guidelines

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the George Best Belfast City Airport Noise Action Plan.
answer_text: The draft George Best Belfast City Airport Noise Action Plan has been drafted and will be with me for consideration shortly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will undertake a comprehensive road traffic assessment of (i) Onslow Parade; and (ii) Ravenhill Park in South Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that my officials have carried out a traffic calming assessment for Ravenhill Park / Onslow Parade, South Belfast which has determined that these locations scored sufficiently high to progress to design.  A traffic calming design has now been developed for Ravenhill Park & Onslow Parade and formal consultation is planned for 2024/2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to reviewing the planning decision made by Mid and East Antrim council to approve the oil terminal at Cloghan Point.
answer_text: Mid and East Antrim Borough Council notified my Department on 08 September 2023, as required by a notification direction previously issued by my Department, of its resolution to approve planning application LA02/2018/1145/F (Cloghan Point Oil Terminal). My Department is currently considering this notification, as well as a number of third party ‘call in’ requests, and when this consideration is complete I will then decide whether or not the application should be referred to my Department for determination.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what actions they are taking to reduce waiting times for the processing of applications by the Victims' Payment Board.
answer_text: As of week commencing 01 April, over £38 million has been paid to victims since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.We are aware of concerns around the length of time it is taking for applications to reach determination stage.  It is important to acknowledge that each application to the scheme is unique and with its own complexities, and many applications include multiple relevant incidents.Our department has provided £5 million of funding to support victims’ and survivors’ representative groups to facilitate recruitment of welfare officers and admin support staff who can assist applicants with the application process.  Additional funding has also been made available to provide health and wellbeing support. 	We are also supporting the retrieval of evidence on behalf of applicants and the Victims’ Payments Board.   			Our officials also continue to engage regularly with the Department of Justice and the Victims’ Payments Board to consider ways to help enhance the delivery of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what precautions are being taken by the Victims' Payment Board to ensure that the processing of applications is conducted in a sensitive and trauma-informed manner.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Scheme is administered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) on behalf of The Executive Office. The VPB is committed to integrating a trauma informed culture and continues to make progress in implementing the Scheme in a victim centred manner. Those involved in the administration of the Scheme are aware that the application process can be an emotional time for many applicants as they recall traumatic life events linked to relevant incidents.  The VPB strives to ensure a positive client journey by grounding its work in the interests of victims, and VPB staff place an emphasis on understanding, respecting and appropriately corresponding with every applicant.  Applicants are made aware of the support available from victims’ organisations across the sector and are signposted to the relevant support services where necessary. All VPB staff, Board members and Committee members are security cleared to CTC level to provide additional reassurance to applicants.  All VPB staff, Board members and Committee members also attend regular trauma focused information sessions and receive: A training course grounded in trauma theory facilitated by WAVE; and  Training on safeguarding / dealing with vulnerable applicants facilitated by Inspire. A key element of the Scheme’s process is the assessment of permanent disablement which is conducted by Capita.  All Capita clinical assessors are registered and appropriately qualified healthcare professionals with at least two years of experience working in a mental health setting.  Capita continues to work closely with the VPB to ensure a victim centric approach is adopted for all individuals that apply to the Scheme.Our department has provided £5 million of funding to support victims’ and survivors’ representative groups to facilitate recruitment of welfare officers and admin support staff who can assist applicants with the application process.  Additional funding has also been made available to provide health and wellbeing support. 		We are also supporting the retrieval of evidence on behalf of applicants and the Victims’ Payments Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to the expansion of SureStart.
answer_text: The Sure Start programme is delivered via 38 Sure Start projects, in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland as determined by postcode.  All children aged 0-3 years (and their families) within the catchment areas of the 38 projects are eligible to access services.  Around 41,500 children aged 0-3 years, and families, can avail of Sure Start support.Expansion of early years interventions currently funded by my Department (including Sure Start) is being considered within the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) strategy.  However, as I have previously made clear, before expanding, it is essential to stabilise existing provision and this and any expanded provision will be subject to availability of the required funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to the reintroduction of the School Holiday Food Grant.
answer_text: Following a decision by the Executive, the Department of Education, made, on an interim basis, School Holiday Food Grant payments from July 2020 to March 2023.  The ringfenced funding for this ceased in 2023 and the payments were therefore discontinued. I will be considering the way forward on this issue in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what options are being costed to support the childcare sector and affordability for parents as part of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I outlined my plans to the Assembly on 12th February for the development and implementation of an ambitious Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  I also indicated that the work to date had involved reviewing and estimating the cost of adopting variations of the schemes operating in other neighbouring jurisdictions. However, I stressed that I wanted to deliver a bespoke scheme for Northern Ireland and conscious that it will take time to get the right model in place, I am also considering what short-term measures might be possible to address current pressures.  Over the coming weeks, I plan to meet parents, providers, and others with an interest in the strategy, to hear firsthand the issues which they are facing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the proposed new build projects for (i) Hunterhouse College; (ii) Malone Integrated College; and (iii) St Joseph’s College, Belfast, as announced under the Major Capital Programme in March 2022.
answer_text: In my Statement to the Assembly on 12 February I advised that seven of the projects announced under the Major Capital Programme in March 2022 would be restarted and would progress in planning.  Hunterhouse College was not one of the seven projects and thus this project remains paused at this time. Malone Integrated College was one of the seven projects and procurement of an Integrated Consultant Team to undertake initial technical feasibility studies will now be progressed.St Joseph's College was not included in the seven projects and therefore also remains paused at this time. For the twenty-one remaining projects on the March 2022 announced list I have confirmed that all of these projects will be kept under review and should additional capital funding be made available progression of these projects will be further considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much funding has been received as a result of Barnett consequentials following the expansion of the 30 free hours of childcare scheme in England.
answer_text: The Executive received £57.2 million of Barnett consequentials for 2024-25, following the announcement in the Spring Budget of the expansion of the 30 free hours of childcare scheme in England. Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, meaning they do not have to be used for the purposes for which they were issued.  It will be for the Executive to allocate this funding as part of its Budget 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current shortage of domiciliary carers in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Ongoing challenges relating to the retention and recruitment of domiciliary care workers are being felt right across the region. The Belfast HSC Trust are experiencing a number of challenges relating to limited applications for posts, applicants withdrawing from applications due to the length of time it takes to go through the appointment processes, and a high attrition rate associated with an ageing workforce. In addressing these issues, the Trust has employed a dedicated member of staff to engage with applicants, and with the Business Services Organisation throughout the recruitment process, with the aim of keeping candidates involved, and to try and expedite the recruitment process where possible.There is a focus on recruitment, with a rolling advertisement for roles on the HSCNI jobs website, engagement in promotional events, and participation in regular recruitment fairs.The Trust is taking tangible steps to try to address the shortage and I recognise that it is working in an extremely competitive wider recruitment market. There is a real need to recognise the value in these roles, and to reward workers appropriately for the work they do.  An immediate priority on my return to this Department is to secure pay settlements for healthcare staff in the statutory sector and work is actively continuing in that area. Whilst my Department has no say in the rates that independent sector providers pay staff, we are also looking at the current rate paid to providers, and to what extent an uplift could be applied.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to allowing non-folding bicycles on Translink rail services prior to 9:30am on a trial basis.
answer_text: The stowage of bicycles on trains is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of the proposed North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route.
answer_text: I announced the preferred route for BRT2 in October 2022, along the Antrim Road in the north and along the Ormeau Road and Saintfield Road in the south. I decided that the north and south routes should extend to Glengormley and Carryduff, respectively with the feasibility of these extensions being further assessed as part of the detailed design. The announcement also noted that the existing G2 City Centre to Titanic Quarter Glider service will be extended to connect with Queen’s University and the City Hospital. In line with this decision, work is almost complete to determine the feasibility of extensions to Glengormley and Carryduff, to conclude the Outline Business Case, and recommendations on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration has been given to expanding the Toybox programme to include providing support to newcomer children.
answer_text: The Toybox project supports the early development of Traveller and Roma children aged 0-4 across Northern Ireland, through helping children and parents engage with education.  Expansion of early years interventions currently funded by my Department (including Toybox) is being considered within the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) strategy. Any extended provision will be subject to availability of the required funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on work being done by his Department to support and progress the development of Mid Down Integrated College.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have been engaging with the Mid Down Integrated College Steering Group on site and accommodation requirements to facilitate the establishment of Mid Down Integrated College.  A site search has been commissioned and a further meeting with the Steering Group will be scheduled when the site search report has been received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policies and protections that are in place for lone workers within health and social care settings, including for domiciliary carers.
answer_text: On 12 December 2023 my department launched a new Violence and Aggression in the Workplace HSC Framework. The purpose of this framework is to outline the HSC commitment in partnership with staff representatives, to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace, and to ensure associated structures, policies and support is in place to enable staff to work safely. This regional framework is the basis of any policies and processes developed locally by the various HSC Bodies in relation to dealing with such incidents in a diverse range of working environments, including lone working.The regional framework is accessible on the Department’s website.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-afc-6-2023_0.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of registered childminders, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: There are currently 2,047 registered childminders in Northern Ireland. The breakdown by Trust area is as follows:Belfast Health and Social Care Trust – 234;Northern Health and Social Care Trust – 621;Southern Health and Social Care Trust – 347;South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust – 416; andWestern Health and Social Care Trust – 429.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what support their Department will provide to local councils for projects to support asylum seekers and refugees in the 2024-25 financial year.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on plans to address the recruitment and retention crisis currently facing our early years education and childcare sector.
answer_text: Recruitment and retention issues in the early learning and childcare sector will be considered within the context of the development of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.   Conscious that it will take time to get the right model in place, I am also considering what short-term measures might be possible to address current pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration she has given to providing MLA offices with access to Land and Property Service maps and other facilities.
answer_text: Datasets are made available as per Section 45 of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 for use by the Assembly. Land & Property Services (LPS) can arrange a supply of datasets if MLAs require LPS Data Products to use for Assembly/parliamentary business or judicial proceedings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline (i) the evaluation process used to assess the use of Good Relations Funding by recipients; and (ii) how feedback from these evaluations is shared with organisations in receipt of funding.
answer_text: Evaluation is an important part of our Good Relations work. There are a number of ways in which funding is assessed. A key aspect of understanding the impact of Good Relations delivery is the measurement of three key metrics which assess changes in participants attitudes, knowledge and intended behaviour towards those from a different political, religious or ethnic background. Feedback from evaluation is routinely shared with contributing organisations. Provisional project level data can be shared with organisations on an ongoing basis, while programme level data is compiled annually and published. More broadly, shared learning forums and shared learning reports are also used to disseminate best practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to review foster carer allowances to ensure parity in allowances provided in other jurisdictions of the UK.
answer_text: On an annual basis, the Department of Health considers an annual inflationary increase to apply to foster care allowance rates applicable in Northern Ireland (NI). This annual review process considers inflationary increases anticipated for the year, within the context of affordability and the financial envelope available.The review to consider an annual uplift to foster care allowances for 2023/24 was completed following confirmation of the Department’s budget for the 2023/24 financial year, and a 3% uplift increase to foster care allowance rates was agreed and implemented for 2023/24. As in previous years the review to consider an annual uplift to foster care allowances for 2024/25 or a decision to bring allowances in line with other UK jurisdictions will be completed following confirmation of the Department’s budget for the 2024/25 financial year. As part of that review the Department will also consider the uplifts to minimum fostering allowances awarded in other UK jurisdictions.Any future annual inflationary increases to fostering allowances or increases to bring foster care allowance rates in line with other UK jurisdictions, will of necessity need to be considered within the context of affordability and the budget available.It is important to note, the allowance rates in NI and other jurisdictions in the UK may not be directly comparable as the age bandings for rates (to which allowances are applied) are not all co-terminus, and there may also be other differences in the amount of allowances payable and the costs which the minimum allowances are intended to cover.The Fostering Network’s Foster Care Allowances Survey 2023/24, notes that allowances in England, Wales and Scotland vary considerably and that Northern Ireland is the only nation where all Trust foster carers (which includes kinship carers) received the same rate of allowances.  The Department of Health recognises the essential contribution foster carers make to the lives of children in care. Following the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland the Children’s Services Reform Board was established.  A workstream was initiated under this Board to develop a strategy for fostering in Northern Ireland. This workstream will also consider fostering allowances, and other financial support for foster carers over the coming year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work is being done to improve digital accessibility for older people in line with the objectives of the Active Ageing Strategy, particularly in relation to online banking and financial transactions.
answer_text: The Active Ageing Strategy 2016-2022 contained actions which focused on helping older people gain digital skills. The Department of Finance has a Digital Inclusion Unit who promote Digital Inclusion. As part of their work, they deliver initiatives which ensure older people are not excluded from accessing information and services by working with various organisations to recruit digital champions, providing tuition, and detailing the help that is available to assist older people in accessing online services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will consider the introduction of a grant scheme for the installation of low-carbon air source heat pumps, similar to those available in other jurisdictions in the UK.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps. https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/Energy-Strategy-2023-Action-Plan-Report.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the schools in South Belfast that are awaiting the beginning or completion of a new build project; and (ii) to provide the current ranking for the prioritisation of works for those new school builds that are yet to begin.
answer_text: Five schools in the South Belfast constituency have been announced under the Major Capital Works Programme and one school has been announced under the Fresh Start Agreement.  Major Capital Programme Scoil an Droichid Primary School was announced to advance in planning in June 2014.  The new school build project is fully designed with planning approval in place.  Due to the current capital budget position the project has had to be paused, prior to appointment of a building contractor.Hunterhouse College, Malone Integrated College and St Joseph’s College, Belfast, were announced to advance in planning in March 2022.  All three projects were paused in April 2023 due to the current budget position.  No feasibility work has commenced on any of these projects.   However, on 12 February I announced that seven of the projects from the 2022 announcement would now progress in planning, which included Malone Integrated College. Procurement of an Integrated Consultant Team will now be undertaken. Breda Academy was announced to advance in planning in June 2014.  The project was subsequently paused by the Education Authority. It will be for the Education Authority, as the Managing Authority for the school, to reinstate the project when it is in a position to do so. Fresh Start Agreement Forge Integrated Primary School was announced as part of the Fresh Start Agreement in 2016. The new build school project is currently in the design stage and the planning application process is ongoing. It is anticipated that construction of the new build will commence on site in early in early 2026.Without additional capital funding Scoil an Droichid cannot progress to the construction stage and the twenty-one remaining projects from the March 2022 announcement cannot be progressed. Should capital funding become available projects will be reviewed and prioritized for delivery.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for an update on work evaluating the implementation the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025; and (ii) to outline work to develop a new strategy beyond 2025.
answer_text: An independent review of progress on the implementation of the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025 was launched in August 2023.The Independent Review Team is at the stage of preparing the final report.Along with the report there will also be further engagement and collaboration with the wider minority ethnic sector to inform any new strategic approach beyond 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on correspondence with HMRC in relation to the Tax-Free Childcare scheme, to take into account the call for the removal of the cap and extension of support for families.
answer_text: There has been no response from His Majesty’s Treasury to the previous Education Minister’s request that the Government contribution to Tax-Free Childcare be increased from 20% to 30%.  I intend to pursue this issue further in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) embarked on an End to End Review (E2E) of Special Educational Needs (SEN) in early 2023 to examine the entire SEN system from a policy perspective to ensure delivery and outcomes are in line with objectives and policy intent. Phase one of the review is complete which involved scoping the review and establishing appropriate governance structures.  Phase two has commenced and primarily focuses on stakeholder engagement to ensure that all decisions and solutions are underpinned by meaningful engagement. A programme of events is currently being developed for schools, parents, children and young people and wider stakeholders so that they can share their knowledge and experience and be part of co-designing a more agile and refreshed SEN system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans his Department has to make requesting flexible working days a right for employees in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following a co-design and consultation process, it is my intention to bring forward an employment rights Bill within the current Assembly mandate to ensure the employment law framework remains fit for purpose and operates effectively for businesses and workers. Revision to the current flexible working arrangements to remove the current qualifying period before a flexible working request can be made is an issue I will be happy to consider in that context.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work to develop an updated NI Executive International Relations Strategy.
answer_text: We have seen significant changes internationally in the last 10 years since the first International Relations Strategy was published in 2014.  An updated strategy is needed that reflects our current situation and is agile in its response to developing challenges and opportunities.We recognise the importance of our international presence and engagement with strategic partners and are considering how a future strategy could help to deliver the Executive’s priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work being undertaken to re-establish the Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership.
answer_text: Since the Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership (NISMP) ceased to function, the Executive Office (TEO) has been working with Home Office to cover some of the NISMP functions.  TEO has now agreed to formally take on some of these functions which focus primarily on communication and engagement, to co-ordinate and encourage multi-agency support for asylum seekers and their integration into our society.  A Memorandum of Understanding is in place with Home Office to facilitate this and a small quantum of funding has been made available to the Department, by Home Office, to support delivery.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work being undertaken to implement the Refugee Integration Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Ministers will be considering this work and the Refugee Integration Strategy shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to introduce child-centred, affordable, flexible, high-quality childcare in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Since my appointment as Education Minister, I have made the development and implementation of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy a priority and while I have stated that our ambition should not be limited, it is important that we take time to carefully consider our options and to secure the necessary funding.  To that end I plan to meet parents, providers and those with an interest in the strategy, to hear firsthand how best we can meet the developmental needs of young children whilst helping to reduce the cost of childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update regarding the standardisation of the pre-school education programme.
answer_text: Significant work has been done by my Department to make progress towards standardisation of the pre-school education programme, including via the ‘Amalgamation for Standardisation’ pilot and the commissioning of a detailed scoping study which was informed by extensive stakeholder engagement.In late 2022, the Department launched a pilot programme ‘Amalgamation for Standardisation’ to facilitate change to a full-time (minimum 22.5 hours per week) pattern of pre-school provision where there is evidence demonstrating an historical pattern of over provision within the setting and sufficient capacity in the surrounding area to continue to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target-aged child whose parents want it. Feedback from settings participating has been universally positive and the learning from the pilot will help inform a smooth transition process for further roll-out.In early 2023, RSM UK were commissioned to carry out a detailed scoping study which involved a survey of all funded pre-school education settings, as well as desk research and interviews with practitioners and sectoral representatives. This scoping study is nearing its conclusion, with the final report due to be submitted to the Department by the end of March 2024. The Scoping Study Report will enable my Department to establish a costed implementation plan for the phased roll out of a universal, standardised, funded pre-school session length for every child whose parents want it.  I will be looking to Executive colleagues to prioritise the funding required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether, in the last 18 months, his Department has received data from the Home Office or Mears PLC relating to the number of children currently living in contingency accommodation.
answer_text: My Department sought and received information from the Home Office on the number of children living in contingency accommodation in June 2022. Mears provides information to Health and Social Care Trusts about the number of people residing in contingency accommodation, to assist with the planning and provision of services. The Public Health Agency has confirmed that it has also requested information to be provided by Mears on an ongoing basis from September 2022, in order to support service planning and delivery.In addition, arrangements are in place with Mears to make a referral to the relevant Trust where a child arrives without a parent or legal guardian, including where they may be in the company of an adult sibling, to ensure that Trusts can fulfil their statutory obligations to these children. My officials have met recently with representatives from the Children’s Law Centre and the South Tyrone Empowerment Programme to discuss the issue of children living in contingency accommodation. It was agreed that my Department would urgently consider what further action can be taken to ensure that the Health and Social Care Service is able to fulfil its responsibilities to children and their families living in contingency accommodation, including identifying any additional data requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether, in the last 18 months, her Department has received data from the Home Office or Mears PLC relating to the number of children currently living in contingency accommodation.
answer_text: The Department has not received data directly from the Home Office or indirectly from the Mears Group over the last 18 months, regarding the number of children currently living in contingency accommodation.The Department works alongside the Education Authority’s (EA) Intercultural Education Service (IES) to provide specific support to Asylum Seeking and Refugee (ASR) children including those living in contingency hotel accommodation.  The EA does not receive data directly from the Home Office or indirectly from the Mears Group and no Data Sharing Agreements are in place between the bodies.With the consent of Mears and the parents, the EA receives data directly from the families when a face-to-face meeting takes place within their accommodation.Officials from my Department and The Executive Office are continuing to engage with the Home Office and Mears Group to develop and implement proposals to support the increasing number of ASR families arriving in Northern Ireland, including on the issue of data sharing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether, in the last 18 months, her Department has received data from the Home Office or Mears PLC relating to the number of children currently living in contingency accommodation.
answer_text: The Management of accommodation for asylum seekers by Mears is an excepted matter which is the responsibility of the Home Office.  In the last 18 months, my Department has received data relating to children living in contingency accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what more his Department can do to address inconsiderate parking on Collingwood Avenue, South Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that legislation to create new waiting restrictions (double yellow lines) along Collingwood Avenue was advertised in the local press on 17 August 2022. There were no objections to the proposal and therefore the Department is hopeful that an Order will be made in the coming months to allow the restrictions to be marked on the ground.In addition, the Department’s Parking Enforcement Unit will arrange for additional patrols to be carried out in the Collingwood area.  Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) will be issued to any vehicle found to be parked in contravention of the existing waiting restrictions.  Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he and the Permanent Secretary of his Department will be attending the meeting, on 14 October 2022, with representatives from programmes currently funded by the European Social Fund and officials from the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities.
answer_text: Reverend Andrew Irvine recently wrote to me on behalf of the East Belfast Mission to outline concerns on the loss of the European Social Fund and to inform me that this meeting would be taking place with the Department of Levelling Up Housing and Communities (DLUHC) and informed me that an invite would issue to my Permanent Secretary.I have responded to Reverend Irvine to assure him that the pressures faced by this sector with the loss of the European Social Fund are recognised and a regular topic for discussion across Departments. In my response, I also highlighted a recent engagement which has taken place jointly between the Permanent Secretaries of the Departments of Finance, Economy and Communities and DLUHC. I have also provided my full support to Reverend Irvine with his efforts in lobbying the British Government to get full replacement for ESF.Unfortunately due to prior commitments my Permanent Secretary is not available to attend. However senior officials from my Department will attend on his behalf and the organisers of this event have been informed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for a breakdown of how his Departmental budget is spread across (i) Environment; (ii) Agriculture; and (iii) Rural Affairs.
answer_text: As my Department has not been allocated an agreed budget for 2022-23, it is not possible to provide a breakdown of how it is spread across these three areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is on target to produce the first three carbon budget plans by December 2023, as outlined in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places a duty on my Department to set carbon budgets to cover 5-year periods beginning from 2023-27.  Carbon budgets are not plans, rather are legal caps on the total amount of net emissions allowed in Northern Ireland within the relevant 5-year period. In line with the requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, my officials have commenced work to set the first three 5-year carbon budgets for Northern Ireland by the end of 2023.  They have formally written to, and have met with, the UK Climate Change Committee to seek advice on the first three carbon budgets and this engagement process is ongoing.In addition, my officials are also progressing work in respect of the development of Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan, which has to be published by the end of December 2023.  This plan will include policies and proposals from each Northern Ireland Department and will set out how we aim to meet the first carbon budget (covering the period 2023-27) and the longer-term emission reduction targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Belfast Cycling Network.
answer_text: The Belfast Cycle Network Delivery Plan (BCNDP) contains a large number of schemes that are intended to encourage cycling in the city and reduce the dependency on the use of the private car.  My officials are currently developing a number of schemes within the BCNDP for inclusion in a short term plan for delivery between 2022 and 2025.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to improve the appearance of the Finaghy bridge.
answer_text: I am very aware that the appearance of Finaghy Railway Bridge is a significant issue for the local community and my Department is continuing to work closely with Belfast City Council (BCC) to develop proposals to take forward this improvement project.My Department is presently consulting with Translink and has engaged the original designer and manufacturer of the bespoke bridge parapet system to undertake a detailed assessment of the feasibility and costs of a number of potential options. When this work is complete and costings become available later this financial year, my officials will liaise with their BCC counterparts to discuss the way forward.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department upholds Section 25 of the Northern Ireland (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2006 in relation to Government Departments and District Councils having a statutory duty to promote the achievement of sustainable development in the exercise of their functions.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2006 does not place any statutory requirement on my Department to monitor or enforce Section 25 nor does it provide any enabling powers. My Department has however, previously engaged with the Department for International Development (DfID), the Office for National Statistics (ONS) and the other Devolved Administrations to undertake and publish the UK Voluntary National Review (VNR). The VNR took stock of progress towards achievement the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and provided a mechanism for independent review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what work his Department has undertaken to establish an Independent Environmental Protection Agency, as committed to in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave previously to AQW 30715/17-22.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work her Department is undertaking to address child poverty.
answer_text: My Department is leading on the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy through a co-design process with a focus on ensuring that stakeholders’ voices and lived experience are considered as part of the Strategy development process. Development of the Strategy will identify inequalities and will focus on actions to address the issues, barriers and disadvantages that undermine equality of opportunity for all those including children in or at risk of poverty.The Executive’s Child Poverty Strategy was extended to May 2022 to ensure that government continues to work collectively to tackle the issues faced by children and families impacted by poverty. Work on the Strategy drafting process is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive. Subject to Executive approval, the Draft Strategy will be issued for public consultation and all interested parties will have the opportunity to respond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of what work is required in order to revisit the potential transfer of regeneration powers to Councils.
answer_text: Significant work would be required to revisit the potential transfer of regeneration powers to Councils as much has changed, both at a Central and Local Government level, since 2015 	It is clear that a functioning Executive will also be required to agree the scope of any new legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department is exploring and costing models for affordable childcare schemes other than a 30 hours free childcare scheme.
answer_text: I made a Ministerial Statement to Assembly members on 7 September 2022 announcing an expanded and standardised offer for every child in their pre-school year and progress on developing an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  The offer of a minimum of 22.5 hours pre-school education per week is a core component, and viewed as a vital first step, to ensuring equality of provision for all children aged 3-4.  Further proposals, including for other age groups and additional hours, will be considered as part of the ongoing development work on the wider Strategy.    A Review of Childcare Services in NI is already underway and will provide essential evidence to inform this work.	I have written to key stakeholders on 7 September 2022, inviting them to join the new Stakeholder Engagement Forum which will support the development of the Strategy.  It is envisaged that the first meeting of the Forum will take place in October 2022.The implication of the cost of living crisis on individuals and businesses is recognised across Departments.  Any response to the crisis needs to be in the context of national measures.You can view the full Ministerial Statement at the following link https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/written-ministerial-statement-update-executive-childcare-strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will establish an emergency fund as an immediate priority to support childcare providers in meeting their higher bills during the current cost of living crisis, and as they await the development of the new Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I made a Ministerial Statement to Assembly members on 7 September 2022 announcing an expanded and standardised offer for every child in their pre-school year and progress on developing an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  The offer of a minimum of 22.5 hours pre-school education per week is a core component, and viewed as a vital first step, to ensuring equality of provision for all children aged 3-4.  Further proposals, including for other age groups and additional hours, will be considered as part of the ongoing development work on the wider Strategy.    A Review of Childcare Services in NI is already underway and will provide essential evidence to inform this work. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to identify mitigations to allow existing home care dog boarding providers to operate, given The Animal Boarding Establishments Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1974 does not recognise home care dog boarding.
answer_text: It is acknowledged that complying with existing licensing requirements can be difficult for dog home boarding businesses. The Department has not, therefore, required businesses undertaking this activity to cease trading but works closely with operators to encourage them to meet licencing conditions where they are able to do so.My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes, during this mandate. To this end, my officials are presently planning a schedule of animal welfare related legislation to be progressed during this mandate. This will include action on this matter.Please be assured, however, that, in the meantime, any dogs cared for in a home setting continue to be protected by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 which provides enforcement agencies with substantial powers to act in situations where an animal is subject to unnecessary suffering in any setting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support her Department is providing asylum seeking children and their families who are being accommodated in contingency accommodation.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters and do not fall within my Department’s authority. The Home Office is the operational and policy lead on these matters while the Executive Office is the lead here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what support her Department is providing to asylum seeking children and their families who are being accommodated in contingency accommodation.
answer_text: My Department works alongside the Education Authority’s (EA) Intercultural Education Service (IES) to provide support to asylum seeking children living in contingency accommodation in Northern Ireland.  The IES’s role is primarily to provide educational advice and support through a small team of teaching and social work qualified staff.  The team is experienced around escalating any child protection or safeguarding concerns which they might observe when visiting the accommodation centres.Since August 2021, the EA has responded to the unprecedented increase in numbers of asylum seeking school-age children living in contingency accommodation by offering a varied complement of provision including welcome clubs for primary and post primary pupils; laptops and Wi-Fi connections; English for speakers of other languages (ESOL) classes; assistance with school registration, free school meals, uniform grants and transport applications; arranging visits by Educational Psychology staff; and making statutory assessment referrals where necessary.  The EA has also supported children in the NI Libraries’ weekly stories and craft sessions and Barnardo’s twice weekly ‘learning through play’ sessions in one of the contingency accommodations.In addition my Department and the IES have provided online ESOL courses for asylum seeking children aged 16-18 who are not enrolled at a school or college of further or higher education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support his Department is providing asylum seeking children and their families who are being accommodated in contingency accommodation.
answer_text: Public Health Nursing Teams through either the Northern Ireland New Entrants Service (NINES) or Core Health Visiting and School Nursing Services are addressing the needs of asylum seekers and their children living in contingency accommodation.  When they are made aware of families living in contingency accommodation, these teams complete a family health needs assessment.  Based on this assessment, the teams provide either a core or targeted service for families dependent on their needs, and refer and signpost to other services as required. They also commence registration of children with the Child Health System which allows for generation of appointments for vaccinations and future health reviews. Where school nurses are made aware of asylum seekers in schools they complete a school-based health review for those children from primary 1 up to and including year 8. NINES also provides drop-in health clinics in hotels being used as contingency accommodation. These clinics give asylum seekers who may otherwise be unknown to services the opportunity to engage with health services and to commence their GP registration.  Covid-specific pop up vaccination clinics are currently being organised to take place in the contingency hotels in Belfast for both adults and children who are eligible for these vaccinations.My Department has also worked with the Business Services Organisation and support organisations to ensure the simplest and quickest process is in place to enable those seeking asylum to register with a GP in Northern Ireland for the first time.  This includes individuals who are housed in contingency accommodation.  Information on the process for asylum seekers to register with a GP is available in a number of different languages on the BSO’s website at https://hscbusiness.hscni.net/services/1814.htmIn addition, my Department is engaging with the Home Office to ensure that referral pathways are in place so that any family support or childcare concerns which are identified by its staff working in contingency accommodation are brought to the attention of the relevant Health and Social Care Trust for an appropriate response.
